description:
Trivia cards about ice hockey legends past and present with over 200 different trivia questions.
With questions on each card getting progressively more challenging – starting with “Rookie League” questions for young fans and ending with “Hall of Fame” questions for true baseball aficionados.

Read the question out loud and try to answer the question correctly. If you answer correctly, pat yourself on the
back and score a card. If you answer incorrectly, try to make up
an excuse to justify your lack of baseball knowledge
When you are done the card, move to the next card.
Who has the most cards at the end of the game is the winner.

This expansion will introduce mall tiles combining building and street zones for a different perspective on lines of sight. There will be also four new survivors (including their Zombivor versions) with new skills, and the six Zombivor versions of the 2012 core game heroes.

The Toxic City Mall will also include new equipment cards (such as assault rifles, laser pointers or flamethrower) and new game modes: Toxic City Mall will introduce barricade rules, giant-sized rubble tokens to change your boards and Zombivor rules. Players will be able to lead these undead heroes against the zombie hordes, with totally new mechanics. Then there’s the Ultrared Mode, allowing players to gain new Skills after reaching the Red Danger Level, and super Ultrared weapons usable only by Red Level survivors.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description: This expansion to Win, Place & Show is a second program to use with the game. You will need a copy of Win, Place & Show to use it. It was originally included as an insert in The GENERAL Magazine Vol. 30, No. 4 (July-August 1996). It includes six full new races with new horses to use sometimes in place of the original program, for variety. The accompanying article also included some miscellaneous variant rules, including rules for interchangeable apprentice and veteran jockeys.

How to play
Begin the game by having every player roll a die to see who goes first. The person who rolls highest goes first, followed by the person to their left and so on and so forth. Should two or more people roll the highest number than they must roll off until someone has rolled higher.

When it is your turn you must do the following.
1.    Draw a card from the draw pile.
2.    Use a power card, place a character card in play, or use a character card power.
3.    Discard cards from your hand until you have only five left.

Power Cards
Most power cards may only be used on your turn. When you play a power card you immediately do whatever it says, then discard it. Well… not immediately, but before you do anything else game related. So you could eat a sandwich or something.

Some power cards may be used at other times, which is indicated on the card itself. When in doubt, the card is right.

Character Cards
Character cards may be placed in front of you during your turn instead of using a power card or a character power. Place the character card in front of you and leave it there until something tells you to place it someplace else. This is now considered to be an “in play” character card. You may not use the Character Card Power the turn you place a character card in play. You may only have three character cards in play at a time. If you place a fourth you must discard one.

Character Card Powers
Instead of placing a character card or using a power card you may use the power on one of your “in play” character cards. This power works just like a power card, except you do not need to discard the character card to use it.

Note, some character cards have passive effects that do not require you to use them on your turn. These abilities are always in effect and should be easy to determine. If an argument does ensue the following characters have “passive” abilities.

Khan-El, Biel-Tel, Beorn, Penelope, Neil and Harris.

How to Win
This is the part you have all been waiting for isn’t it? To win the game all you have to do is have all of your in play character cards be the same characters as on your Dream Team card. Once you do that you win!

Card game published as a promotion for UNESCO. The tile means 'The Unesco Rainbow'.

The cards feature the letters U N E S C or O. There are eight of each letter and, in the main, they have different coloured backgrounds.

Each player, in turn, may lay a card on the table, that can legally go next to another card. There are none at the start so a U must be placed. After that, you may lay an N next to the U. You can place the N alongside the U or beneath it. The next player may now play a legal letter next to any card on the table. Thus, the word UNESCO will be spelt many times perhaps, horizentally or vertically. Think of it as a UNESCO crossword. If you cannot play a card, you draw one from the stock.

The first player to use all their cards wins the round. The other players total the value of cards left in their hands and take a score for them. Colour matches can matter because this makes a more penal score. After an agreed number of rounds, the player with the lowest score wins.

Blankity-Blank! is a word game that will appeal to anyone who likes Scrabble, Boggle, Scattergories, and other word games. It's a family game designed for 2 to 6 players ages 12 and older.

To begin a round of Blankity-Blank!, you take a card from the box and let all the players see it. On the front of the card is an incomplete word with some letters left blank; for example, P--N-. You and every other player have three minutes to write down all the words you can think of that would complete that pattern, like phone or piano or plant or prank. When time is up you all read your lists aloud and cross out any word that more than one person wrote; you score points only for words that you alone thought of.

The front of the card indicates whether this is a 1-point, 2-point, or 3-point round, according to how many potential matches there are for this pattern. 1-point cards have dozens of possible solutions, 2-pointers not so many, and 3-point cards have the fewest of all. So you score 1, 2, or 3 points for each unique word in your list. (P--N- would be a 2-point card.)

When you've recorded the scores for this round, you may look at the back of the card. It shows a list of the most common words that would be correct solutions, so players can see some of the words no one thought of.

You continue to play with another card and another until one player has scored enough points to win. This can be 25 points, or 50, or any number the group agrees to.

The game includes over 500 cards, a pad of answer sheets, and a timer.

Child Beauty Pageant Tycoon is a game where you and your friends take the role of a mother whose greatest goal in life is to have her children win Beauty Pageants.  The first player to win five Beauty Pageant crowns wins the game.  There are 4 very different ways to make your Child ready to compete in a Pageant.  1) Spending a night with a Stud to give your child an edge from the very start. 2) Gaining experience by simply competing in Pageants. 3) Training your Child in a special Skill. 4) Buying a special 1-use Item to be used in any Pageant.  Each Pageant will have different entry fees and winnings, and they will focus on different stats.  When a pageant focuses on a stat, it counts for double towards winning the pageant.  For example, let's say Mary Beth has a 'Smarts' score of 3. In the Pageant shown above, she will get to double her smarts score and it will instead give her 6 points toward winning the Pageant!  Glitz or Talent stats still count toward winning, but they aren't doubled in this Pageant.

A unique version of Monopoly based off of the Northern Kentucky town of Fort Thomas. Properties include historical content and prominent places around town. The game is played nearly the same as Monopoly, but with a few variants. There are different cards, and different rules for gaining money in relation to the cards and properties on the board.

Players customize a deck of 30 cards depicting the heroes, villains, Zords, and Megazords of the Power Rangers universe. Cards are found in special starter sets, booster packs, and also packaged with Power Rangers toys from the 2013 line.

Turn Order:

1. Draw 2 cards.
2. Choose a Ranger or Villain to play, or pass. Discard cards equal to the play cost.
2. Optionally, players can summon Zords or Megazords if a Ranger was played in the previous step.
3. If only one player played a card, that card deals damage equal to its damage value twice.
4. If both players played a card, each player performs a "judgement" by rolling a die (or using an app or Morpher toy) to determine what power level their card fights at. The higher Power value wins. The loser is dealt damage equal to the winning card's damage value.
5. If the winner's card's rock/paper/scissors icon defeats the opponent's, the winner deals damage twice.
6. Damage is represented by moving cards from the draw deck into the discard pile.
7. The winner's card is discarded, the loser's is moved to the reinforcements area, where it can boost the "judgement" value in subsequent battles.
8. Play continues until one player runs out of cards in his or her draw deck. That player loses and the opponent wins!
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
This is a game for children with an American Civil War military theme published in 1890.

Players begin the game as a drummer boy and attempt to rise through the ranks and become a general. The board is styled like a maze and players move based on the results of a spinner.
Certain spaces on the board have special rules. A drum symbol allows the player to spin again. Other spaces have instructions like "Surly. Go to Guard House" and penalize the player.

The first child to reach the "General" space is declared he winner.

Includes a colorful 13" x 13" board illustrated with Civil War scenes, metal tokens, and a drum-shaped spinner.

Game designed by creative team Lans Macabre. It's an area control strategy based on resource use, betting and bluffing. Players take the roles of megacorporations’ CEOs to enlarge influence and dominance of the world. To do so they must spend resources to get control of cyberpunk freelancers - but it's not as easy as it sounds. The cyberpunks are very hard to control and everyone will try to recruit them. Each player will bet on different resources to get best results.
Future INC. is all about getting control over locations and gathering resources.
Each player starts with the same amount of skyscrapers and tries to expand by building new and capturing or destroying those belonging to other players. Thanks to skyscrapers players gain control over locations – areas of the cyberpunk world - and gather resources.

There are five types of resources in the game:
Credits - represent financial capabilities - used to build new skyscrapers.
Cybernetics - represent technological advancement - used to change ownership of enemy skyscrapers to yours.
Gear - represents military potential - used to destroy skyscrapers.
Programs- represent defense against "cyber terrorism" - used to counter enemy actions.
Influence - represents political power and intel – used to gain an advantage by manipulating game’s mechanics.

The most important thing in Future INC. is using free mercenaries - known as Cyberpunks - to do the dirty work. Using resources, corporation may influence a group of Cyberpunks to capture enemy building and gain victory points.

Game is divided into five turns, each has three phases:
1st phase, collecting resources: in which players gather resources, determine new targets for Cyberpunks and update points status.
2nd phase, mission: in which players, using bluff, bet on resources to gain control over the Cyberpunks.
3rd phase, actions of corporations: in which players spend resources to perform actions against each other.
Points are gathered by winning control over Cyberpunks and locations. The player with the highest score at the end of the fifth turn wins the game.

Future INC. High-Tech Stakes is a dynamic strategy for everyone who likes power struggle and a poker-like experience.

By the same designer and publisher of the Judge Dredd Miniatures Game, 2000AD: Rogue Trooper Miniatures Game is a science-fiction skirmish 28mm miniatures wargame based (as the title suggests!) on the Rogue Trooper stories of the classic British comic, 2000AD.

A Kickstarter project started in February 2013, successfully funded within one day of launch, the intention is to publish a hardback rulebook and a range of miniatures for both sides, of the perpetual civil war on Nu-Earth.  The Nort and Souther armies are asymmetrical (army lists are featured in the rulebook; units each have a base points cost and upgrades available for additional points).

The scope of the game is intended to be from a handful of miniatures (one GI vs. a Nort squad for example) up to company level actions, including light vehicles.

The range planned for release currently includes:
GI Rogue Trooper
Souther soldier squad set (8 figs)
Nort soldier squad set (8 figs)
Nort elite assault squad set (8 figs)
Nort & Souther officer squad set (6 figs)
Souther lazooker team squad set (6 figs)
Nort snipers and Black Dome sniper squad set (7 figs)
Skorpion Battlewagon (light tank, 3 variants planned)

Troops have their own basic statistics (points cost, movement, defence and armour values, morale (called Will-to-Fight; WtF in this game!), individual abilities (traits) etc. which are then augmented by the range and damage ratings of the weapon with which they are equipped (range, damage, traits).  The more powerful a weapon is, the more combat dice rolled.

Rules cover ranged and close combat, remote weapons, indirect fire, elevation, suppression, air units, fighting from and in structures, etc.

Missions can be generated within the 'Engagement' chapter by combining various mission profiles (attack, defend, probe, hold, etc) to give an alternative to simple meeting engagements of equal cost forces.  Success or failure is adjudicated by victory points specific to the various missions.

NB: This information is based upon the first release of the draft rulebook, and thus may be subject to change before release.

Objective:
Be the first to reach target space
 - moving by turns -

Typical 'roll and move' game, probbably published ~1920/30's
Roll the die, move forward; ending on a marked space read aloud the datails about that sight-seeing place and follow instructions, printed on a 2-sided rule sheet. Because it was played by adults you have to spend money first before you start. 
Very nice drawings/printing and - of course - pewter pawns

The first fixed Shadowpack for Shadowfist.

From the website:

"The first 50-card expansion set for the new format features a combination of new cards and reprints (plus a few familiar old cards with a new slant!). The set is non-randomized and features seven cards per faction and eight Feng Shui sites."

This 1903 card game by L.T. Hodges is played with 65 cards. There are six suits with 10 cards in each, plus 5 "cabbage" or trump cards.  Players try to match cards in their hand to those on "the board" (the table). The object of the game is get the greatest number of cards, cabbage cards and fives. The 1904 edition also contains rules for playing Wiggs Auction.

From the box:

All the fun of a music quiz night in your own home.

This chart-topping quiz is packed full of fascinating music trivia questions (both popular and classical) guaranteed to keep everyone entertained.  Suitable for all the family and any number of players.

Your knowledge will be pushed to the limits by the following categories: One Hit Wonders; 60s & 70s; 80s & 90s; Albums and Singles; Music Charades; Music & the Movies; General Music Trivia; Pop Music; Name That Tune (on CD).

The [i]Complete Music Quiz Night In[/i] contains everything you need for night after night of fascinating music trivia fun - all you need to supply are the players!

Contents:
50 question cards containing 1,900 questions and answers.
Audio CD for Name That Tune category.
Answer sheets.
Quick start instructions.
4 pencils.

Starter deck expansion for Shadowfist.

From the shadowfist.com website:

"This two-deck follow-up to Combat in Kowloon features 50-card starters for the Monarchs and the Jammers, to fill out the new six-faction standard for Shadowfist!"

Promotional game for the Dutch bank Raiffeisenbank and this is The Bank Letter Game.

Simple crossword card game, similar to games like Lexicon and Kan-U-Go. In your turn you may play a maximum of 4 cards. Words must be formed that use at least one letter of an existing word (except for the first word obviously which, incidentally, must be a four letter word). Any word can be extended though, if possible. Game includes two Jokers which have an image of an old motor car.

The first player to use all his cards wins the round. The other players total the values of their unused cards and the winner of the round scores a total of all those points (each card has a number value on it).

The winner of the game is the first player to reach a pre-agreed score.

The backs of the cards are quite novel. They form one of four different pictures. It is suggested that 6 to 8 year olds might like to do the jigsaws. I doubt it, they are extremely difficult for their size.

A family game based on the Swedish television show with the same name, hosted by Harald Treutiger. The goal of the game is to advance to the final act where you get to see if you got what it takes to win the elusive gold bar. You do this by drawing cards that have all kinds of weird statements on them, put money on if they are either true or false and then roll dice to tell who was right. This is mixed with some contextual statements like for example "will the phone ring during the game session?". Though usually it's mostly a game of plain luck.

The Giraffalaff Limbo will have your preschoolers howling with laughter as they try to limbo alongside the ticklish Giraffalaff giraffe. A quick spin of the wheel reveals which silly animal's walk you'll have to do under the limbo bar--whether it's the bird walk or the crab walk. description:
Cheeky rabbits race around the field, hiding behind bushes to outwit the sharp-eyed fox.

A roll and move, players choose three rabbits of the same colour and place them on one corner of the field on the START hole.

1. The starting player throws the dice and does two things in the following order: 
a)Moves the fox to the sector of the centre circle corresponding to the number,
b)Moves one of his Rabbits in a clockwise direction on the track.

E.G. If a 2 were thrown, the player first moves the Fox onto sector 2; secondly, he moves one of his rabbits two places in a clockwise direction.

2. If the fox can "see" any rabbits in that sector of the board, they must go back to their START hole.

3. If a rabbit is in the same sector as the fox BUT is behind a bush, then it is hidden from view and does not go back to START.

4. Play moves in a clockwise direction.

5. On their first three turns, each player must bring out all three of their rabbits. If a rabbit is sent back to his START hole, it must be brought out onto the track on the player's next turn.

6. Any number of Rabbits may occupy the same space on the track.

7. If a six is thrown the player may have an extra turn.

8. A player does not have to throw the exact number to get a rabbit HOME.

The same rules apply for a two or three player game.

Annihilation is a Battleship-style game for two players using pencil and paper.  Each player receives an identical printed sheet from a game pad, featuring a circular overhead map of the northern hemisphere.  The map is divided into boxes by longitudinal and latitudinal lines, which are labeled by numbers and by names of constellations.  Some boxes are shaded, representing the oceans.  Also present are boxes to track each player’s hits; in place of ships are (in order of most to least boxes) Capital, Trade and Industry, Air Base, Navy, and Peace.  Each player “hides” each of these objectives on his own side of the map, vertically, horizontally, or diagonally.  The Navy must be in the ocean, Peace is a single box that may be anywhere, and the rest must be on land.

Each player then takes a turn firing volleys at the other side.  A volley consists of 15 bombs, which can be any combination of airplane bombs, rockets, or atomics.  Airplane bombs count for 1 bomb against the limit, rockets for 3, atomics for 5.  Volleys are done all at once, with the opponent not announcing hits until the volley is over.  Players track these developments by crossing off boxes on their paper.  When a player sends a volley, if he has previously received any hits, those hits are subtracted from the 15 bombs he may fire on his current turn.

Airplane bombs strike only the square called by the player, rockets strike the first square and any two adjoining squares called immediately by the player, and atomics strike the initial square as well as the four adjoining squares along all four sides.  Any target hit on one square by an atomic is immediately destroyed, any target hit by a rocket suffers an additional box hit after what the player targeted.  Should any player strike the target of Peace, the game is immediately over, otherwise the game is won when all of a player’s other targets are destroyed.

Each card has both a letter and one face of a standard die.  Before each turn, 5 dice are rolled and placed on the dice board in order of their colors. Description:
“Oh My Word” is not just one game, but is 12+ games in one, with more being developed and added frequently! You can play any of the games with the same deck of cards!

One of the most fun and exciting games ever created is “Battle of Words!” It comes in “quick start,” "basic," and "advanced" versions. The basic version can be mastered in a matter of minutes and is fun for the entire family. The play consists of forming words, lengthening existing words, combining words, and stealing words from your opponent(s). The advanced version is easy to learn but will stimulate and challenge even the most astute word gamers. In addition to the basic play, there are 20 action cards that create all kinds of excitement and dynamics between the players. In either version, each player competes for high score through word values, word length values, bonus letter values, bonus word values, other word bonuses, and the number / quality of words acquired by the end of the game.

What if dragons really flew and giants really walked the earth? What if berserkers actually were bear-men, man and bear joined together? The Exodus TCG is an extremely fast-paced card game, designed to play in an average of 5-10 minutes suitable for all ages and skill levels. It's the first trading card game in the world to feature mechanics that affect card rarity! There's no banlist, no rotation, and no reprints from set-to-set. Expansions offer new and interesting mechanics and themes without relying on power-creep.

A mass Exodus to the newborn planet Eeventide is occurring. As you venture from Earth to Eeventide, you become a Drifter, gaining the ability to fly and drift across a new world. Forge a special deck of cards from stardust, and battle other Drifters by summoning massive creatures to fight for you! Your very existence is bound to the planet by a mysterious contraption that glows with an ethereal light. You can join a faction or fight solo as you pave a path.

The first three guilds have risen, Dragonis, Skyborn & the Order, each fighting over a planet that requires constant conflict in order to remain stable.


Players begin the game with 10 Life Points, which represent their will to battle. Each player takes turns playing by following the 5 Phases of a Turn:

         Draw Phase: Draw 3 cards from your Energy Deck and 3 cards from your Main Deck
         Energy Phase: Play your energy onto the field by stacking them into piles in any arrangement you'd like (maximum 3-energy per pile by default)
         Creature Phase: Summon as many creatures from your hand as you'd like by placing them on blank piles of energy
         Attack Phase: Attack your opponent's creatures using your own, or attack their Life Points directly if they have no creatures
         End Phase: Pass the turn to your opponent
    


Players win the game by using their creatures to attack their opponent's Life Points directly. Each energy behind a creature counts as 1 point of damage in a direct attack. Players can attack with as many creatures as they want during their turn, and use instant-speed Symmetry cards to make their creatures stronger or to drastically change circumstances during a duel. Symmetry cards are like quick-play instants that can be played literally at any time to interrupt gameplay (they can be used on your turn or your opponent's turn, on any phase)!

Energy cards are universal in function, so players can use any combination of cards. Decks can be constructed by following the one simple rule of limiting 2 copies of a card per deck (excluding energy).


(Set 1) Birth of Creation Harmonized Dragonis & Skyborn Starter Decks (Unlimited Edition) are now available, Premiere Edition is sold out,
(Set 2) Galaxy Tides (First Edition) Boosters are now available,
(Set 3) Crystal Forge Treeforge & Shardveil Competitive Decks (First Edition) are now available, released August 2017,

Each turn, players choose to draw cards from the deck or take the discard pile, make pairs, discard and pass the turn. Each symbol appears on three different cards. Each card has two different symbols. Easy enough for children to play and elegant enough for adults to enjoy.

From the Rules

The Bombardment of Alexandria 1882 is an additional historical scenario for use with BoneGames game of late 19th century warfare Queen Victoria's Navy. This scenario is not a complete game. Ownership of Queen Victoria's Navy (and the Admiral on Deck expansion) is necessary in order to use this scenario. Visit the BoneGames website at www.bonegames.com for information about Queen Victoria's Navy, Admiral on Deck, and other games by BoneGames.

Historical Background

Early in 1882 the Egyptian Army forced the Khedive, Tewfik Pasha, to appoint Colonel Said Ahmed Arabi AKA Arabi Pasha as the new Minister of War. In this position, and with the backing of the army, Arabi was the de facto ruler of the country. Arabi was viewed with distrust in both Britain and France. This distrust was not without reason as he and his followers viewed the Khedive as a puppet of the Europeans, attacked Europeans in the streets, and threatened to nationalize the Suez Canal leaving the stockholders without a cent, and to repudiate Egypt's foreign loans.

A combination of fear for the Canal, the shortest route to India after all, the outraged cries of the investors and a pinch of concern for the Europeans in Egypt, plus a heavy dose of Jingoism led the British government to prepare for war. France, for some very convoluted internal political reasons, reversed policy and refused to help.

While an Army was being assembled the British decided that their first step should be to neutralize the extensive coastal batteries defending Alexandria. On 10 July,Vice Admiral Sir B. P. Seymour demanded that the forts, then being strengthened, be surrendered. The navies of other nations and all commercial shipping quickly sailed out of port. The Egyptians refused this demand and Queen Victoria’s Navy cleared for action. On 11 July, 1882 the bombardment began at 0650 hours.

boardgame: Paddy Griffith's Napoleonic Wargaming for Fun
description:
From the publisher:"
In the early days of ‘modern’ wargaming, Napoleonic games were tremendously popular. They allowed the new, not necessarily young, wargamer to fight the glorious actions they had heard about in their school history lessons. Often starting with the Battle of Waterloo as their first action, the general would deploy his hastily painted soldiers from Airfix’s Napoleonic range. Airfix offered British and French infantry, as well as cavalry and guns for both sides.

Napoleonic tactics were simple in principle, but were hard to master in practice; for example infantry in a line maximized its firepower, but was vulnerable to cavalry. Changing to a square kept the infantry safe from the cavalry, but made them vulnerable to artillery and reduced their musketry to ¼ of the unit’s strength2. The games, just like the battles of history, were all about combining the efforts of the infantry, cavalry and artillery using ‘combined arms tactics’ to sweep the enemy away. The key problem with these games was the ‘helicopter overview’ of the action the players had of their largely divisional sized games. While representing the battles of the Napoleonic Wars in some ways, they did not recreate experience of the commanders of the time.

Paddy Griffith’s excellent book aimed to fill the gap and offer no less than seven different sets of rules to simulate the experience of command at different levels. Just as the early wargamers took the Airfix Napoleonic’s and converted them for use in a wide range of periods, the ideas offered by Paddy in these rules have equal potential to transform any wargamer’s tabletop experience. I am very pleased for the opportunity to reprint this classic work.

This book contains no less than seven sets of rules:

Skirmish level

Divisional game

Brigade game

Army level game

Generalship game

Map Kriegsspiel

Tactic Exercises Without Troops
"

A standalone miniatures wargame of future air superiority from One Pound Wargames, AirFrame allows players to assemble and customize their formation using existing air combat models from their collection.  The game focuses on tactical manoeuvre and resource management in the 3-dimensional battlespace.  AirFrame can also be combined with MechaWar for combined arms battles.

Each airframe comes with a variable number of stat points depending upon their value.  These are allocated by the player across four stats covering speed, firepower, armour and defence.  Each airframe performs two manoeuvres a turn, with enemy airframes able to react with responsive manoeuvres.

Damage will degrades your ability to stay in the air, but a critical hit may blow your opponent out of the sky in a single, devastating strike!

Key features:


     Miniatures wargame of future aerial combat.
     Integrated turn sequence.
     Action-based turns.
     d12s for shooting.
     Customizable statlines.
     Fully compatible with MechaWar from One Pound Wargames.
     Designed for use with any range of modern or science fiction aircraft, and compatible with scales from 1/805 (2mm) to 1/161 (10mm)

The objective of Fuji's World is to be the first to obtain the required number of victory points. To obtain vicory points, players try to open branches of Fuji and put a number of products on the market there.
There are four regions where you can open branches. Each region consists of several countries. The aim is to market a complete set of products in a country. A product set consists of photo paper, film rolls, offset plates and R&D.
If you market all the products in a country, your competitors no longer have a chance and you gain a victory point. But until that moment, you can lose your branch at any time!

"Invest and conquer new markets for our Fuji Tilburg products! This is what Fuji's World is all about. This game has been specially developed as a Christmas gift for all personnel and temporary staff at Fuji Tilburg. Not just for them, either: it is also important that those at home feel involved with Fuji. We hope that Fuji's World will provide the basic for many exciting family evenings and perhaps it will lead to new inspiration for achieving our objectives."

SQUAR3 (pronounced "Square") is a small but complex tactical tile laying game for two to four players. The players take turn placing square tiles on a 3x3 grid that forms the game board. Each tile has a point value and will influence all adjacent tiles. The players also place gems on top of these tiles to claim them for the final scoring. Some of the tiles have special actions that the player claiming that tile can use.

SQUAR3 is similar to games like Isis & Osiris or Go as the placement of tiles and player pieces is crucial for scoring the most points. A further depth of play stems from the orientation of each tile, the layering of tiles and the management of the actions available to each player.

Games of SQUAR3 can last for only a couple of turns or up to 30 min and can be quite mind-burning.

Several unique "Activation" and "When Established" abilities didn't make into the final version of the Legacy: Forbidden Machines expansion – but since those cards are still interesting and fun, they're available as the promotional expansion Legacy: Forbidden Machines - Deep Vault and are playable with all other technologies from the Forbidden Machines expansion.

Game Wonders is a fan expansion for 7 Wonders. It adds new wonders with a game theme to extend the game play of 7 Wonders and it's expansions 7 Wonders: Leaders and 7 Wonders: Cities.

Play Transylvania and get stronger for every enemy you defeat, Jerusalem that gives you additional Gold and Victory Points for every new leader your neighbors hire, or devote your wonders to the different deities on Olympus.

12 New Cities are available to play with new functionality and powers.

Bad things are going down at Rattlesnake Creek. Decisions taken in the heat of the moment may scar some desperate men for the rest of their wicked lives. When the dust settles, only one side can claim victory. Will it be yours?

Revolver: The Tarnished Star – the fourth in a series of expansions for Revolver – adds an optional "duel" scene at Rattlesnake Creek and an extra decision for the McReady player that may equip the imprisoned Santiago with a hidden weapon. The expansion also adds new cards to the Frontier Deck that was introduced in Revolver Expansion 1.3: Vengeance on the Frontier.

Revolver: The Tarnished Star also includes new cards for both players that give them the opportunity to customize their decks, bringing new variety and replayability to each game.

boardgame: Revolver Expansion 1.5: Death Rides a Horse
description:
A whole mess of danger lurks in the wilds: from a deranged masked knifeman, to strange lights in the woods, to a secret valley flush with hazards - both lawman and bandit alike will need their wits about them to survive!

Combine this expansion with the base game and build your own deck by adding new cards to the pre-constructed decks, and explore new tactics and define your own play style!

This expansion develops all the existing decks, from which both players may draw unique cards - giving your games more replayability and adventure.

Revolver: Death Rides a Horse also includes new cards for both players that give them the opportunity to customize their decks, bringing new variety and replayability to each game.

Undead Escape is a cooperative styled map building game designed for 2 to 5 players. In Undead Escape, one player plays as the human character, whose goal is to set enough fires to burn the zombie's factory to the ground. The remaining players will play as zombies doing everything they can to thwart the human player.

The game is built using pieces of the factory floor, which includes 16 movement spaces and locations for items. Items are placed face down and must be discovered by the players and stored in inventory and eventually used or destroyed depending on their player type.

Zombies will attempt to find newspapers and throw them into one of two water barrels randomly placed on the map. The human player attempts to find pieces needed to make fires, such as newspaper and matches, or a molotov cocktail. When the human player has enough items to start a fire, he must get to one of the fire locations on the map and light it.

To make things even more difficult for the human player, the zombies may also stash other items in their inventory, in which the player can only get back by attacking and winning a battle.

description:
Objective: players striving to be the first to reach center and back out again.
 - moving by turns -
each player moves according spinner, starting from a pink corner space; each player attempts to move towards the center space - called 'Heart of the Woods'. Red spaces on the board must be reached by exact spin in order to move in (later out) of the circle; once reaching the centre, players then turn around and move back out to any of the (pink) starting spaces.
remarks: 
should be published ~ 1900

Road Wolf is a free, home made, tabletop, turn based game of highway combat.  Players choose vehicles, crew, and components and engage each other in high-speed scenarios on the road.

Physical components include a road (easy to make your own), vehicles (modified Matchbox or Hot Wheels cars work fine), dice, measuring sticks, record sheets for vehicles, and counters for crew and equipment (downloadable for free).

Players choose a scenario and purchase vehicles as described by the scenario.  Most scenarios pit one player (or team of players) against another, with different win conditions depending on the scenario.

Unlike many vehicle games that rely on arena combat, Road Wolf follows vehicles traveling down a long, dangerous road. Movement is relative to the other vehicles, as all vehicles are assumed to be hurtling forward down the road, represented by a "road" move at the end of each turn.  Hazards such as bridges or debris can move "towards" the vehicles during the road move, as can immobilized vehicles.

Each turn, vehicles get two actions to spend moving, shooting, or other actions. Vehicles may Ram or Melee each other as part of a move action, encouraging close combat.  Degrees of success can damage or destroy vehicles or crew, or cause wipeout checks that can lead to lost ground or even chain reaction crashes.

This game is played with 2 or more players, and 1 or more standard decks of cards.

After shuffling the cards, one card must be hidden. Then each player receives 9 cards, and the remaining cards forms the stack. Each player must try to make combinations of 3 cards, either 3 of the same number, or 3 cards in sequence. The combinations must be discarded. After make all possible combinations with the cards in hand, the players must take cards of the stack in order to make new combinations. All players can play simultaneously; there is no order of play.

When the stack run out of cards, the players, starting with the dealer and continuing in clockwise order, take cards of the player to the right (without see the cards of the player).

At the end, one player will remain with the last card in hand, and this card must be the same of the card hidden at the start (if not, somebody have make a mistake). This player is the winner.

Khaki in Flames introduces a whole slew of new units to World in Flames, such as NKVD divisions, flak, coastal fortifications, shipyards, home guards, garrison divisions, border troops, the Red Sea Flotilla and more.  Khaki in Flames also upgrades all Commonwealth units from WIF and all previous expansions on an easy-to-read khaki background, and gives players territorials and Chinese warlords in new colors for when these units "switch sides".

Contents
• 1200 full color counters
• Rules for over a dozen new types of land, air, and naval units
• One WIF Combat Chart

description:
CONTROL A FACTION
DESTROY YOUR ENEMIES
EMERGE VICTORIOUS
  
 Skirmish Tactics Apocalypse (STA) is a two player dice-based skirmish-level tabletop game set in a post-apocalyptic near future.

There are two ways to play STA: skirmishes and campaigns.  Skirmishes are standalone games played for a single victory, with no lasting repercussions.  Campaigns are a series of skirmishes. In campaigns, players begin with a squad of recruit characters.

The game board for STA is built from modular tiles which are themed to represent different environments.  The default game board is a destroyed urban setting.  Players build the game board with nine modular tiles, each of which are divided into a 7x7 grid.  Characters use the grids for movement.

STA is a scenario driven game.  Each scenario typically has multiple objectives, and combat between factions is usually a secondary or tertiary objective.  Players win skirmishes in STA by gaining victory points through accomplishing these objectives.

Each character in STA is represented by a token and has a corresponding character card.  During campaign play, characters improve their attributes and develop new abilities as they progress from Recruit to Veteran and then Elite status.

Players control characters from one of the game’s six factions.  The Combine and Marauders are part of the core game; the other four factions can be integrated seamlessly.


     Combine: The Combine is the military arm of the government that rules over much of what is left of the world. 



     Echo: Echo is a faction lead by a secretive cabal of humans with psychic powers. 



     Erthen: Erthen are a biologically developed race formed from a mixture of human, mineral, and organic matter. 



     Marauders: The Marauders are a loose-knit band of human rebels who specialize in guerrilla warfare. 



     Order of the New Dawn: The Order of the New Dawn are creatures from the strange subterranean realm known as the Basement. 



     Soldiers of Light: The Soldiers of Light are humans who believe the fall of mankind was brought about due to a dependance on technology.

A Goose-type game for pre-5 year olds. The game appeared in the 1978 Playhour Annual. Mr. Toad was a character who first featured in Kenneth Grahame's The Wind in the Willows, written in 1908.

Rules
To play this game with your friends, you will need a dice and counters. To start playing the game, each player has to throw a six, and then throw the dice again and move his counter along the numbered spaces. On the white spaces there are things you must do if you land on them. The player who reaches number 50 first, is the winner.

Baby Doll was a comic strip character in Playhour, a favourite comic for the 5's and under. It is a typical Goose-type game and is taken from the 1978 Playhour Annual.

Rules
You need a dice and counters to play this game with your friends. Baby Doll wants to get to the doll's house. But to get there, she has to go along a path of stepping stones. You and your friends can have a race to see who gets to the house first. Throw a six to start, then throw the dice again and move your counter along the numbered stepping stones the dice shows. On the grey stepping stones there are things you must do if your counter lands on them. The one who reaches the house first, wins.

Saga: Northern Fury is the first supplement to Gripping Beast's Dark Ages miniatures skirmish game "Saga". The 22 page full color supplement comes with four additional card Battle Boards - The Anglo-Saxon, The Breton, The Jomsvikings, and The Scots.

description:
Ronnie Peterson (1944–1978) was a Swedish racing driver known by the nickname 'Super Swede'. He was a two-time runner-up in the FIA Formula One World Drivers' Championship. (Source: Wikipedia)

The 1974 edition of the game has Ronnie in his Lotus 72 with the number 1 on the front cover. The 1977 edition has him in his six wheel Tyrrell P34 on the front cover. (Source: http://www.ronniepeterson.se/subc/eng/p.html)

This game is different from other versions of Formula 1 because it has "Chans"(Eng: Opportunity) and "Depå"(Eng: Pit) cards, and rather than a dashboard with a speedometer, tire and brake wear gauges, the player boards are gear boxes used to indicate what gear/speed the car is in as well as indicators for "Slitage"(Eng: Damage) and laps. The course is also different than other Formula 1 games.

You play the part of a Fairy Lord, commanding one of the four armies of Fairy in an epic tournament.

For each tournament, four humans are 'invited' to join by the fairy lords. Often kidnapped or unwittingly stumbling into the Fairy land, these humans exemplify the fairy's love of fools and foolish people.

You can use your powers to change the outcome of each battle, but be wary! The nobility of Fairy are very honorable in nature and won’t stand for such trickery! And watch out for the fools! They can ruin your whole battle.

Fools! is a simple card game, with one deck of 64 cards. Each player draws a small 'army' of 3 face down cards and a hand of three cards. The army cards represent those fairies who will distinguish themselves in battle. Your hand represents fairies and fools you can use to hinder your opponents or strengthen your own army.

Cards can be played as Standard Bearers, some with special effects. Players are then required to replace a card in their opponent's army with one from their hand. They may then replace a card in their own army with one from their hand. Through this all, their army cards remain hidden from everyone.

At the end of each turn, your hand is discarded and your army cards (actually those who've distinguished themselves in this round of battle) are revealed. Points are added up and the highest score wins the turn.

2 player games play to 5 points, 3 players to 4 points and 4 players to three points.

This light card game is meant to fill the same roll as Zombie Dice - a fast, light and portable game to be played anywhere. Also great with kids.

You play as a young officer whose ambitious goal is to become the leader of the NYPD. Thanks to your staff and the support of your colleagues, you will stop gangsters, regain control of neighborhoods, traffic capture, in brief all which will allow you to be promoted and to fill the quotas of the mayor of the city.

City Police is based on a mechanism of simultaneous placements: each turn, you send your staff (inspectors, sergeants, lieutenants ...) on the site of the city by conducting joint actions with other players. The hardest part will not necessarily stop the gangsters, but you agree you later for the distribution of benefits of each intervention.

Thus, at every moment of the game, you will make the right decisions and advance good arguments in a very short time. Negotiation and reactivity are your main weapons. But beware: if you and your colleagues will too shoot in the legs, it will be the Mafia who eventually win the game.

City Police is a fun and relaxed which makes great negotiation and role-play. Unlike Gangs City, you need to know to make decisions very quickly, on the stroke of the pulse. It will show you reagents both intellectually and physically, since the investments are trades like an hourglass time of 30 seconds. Thus, the parties are very lively and rhythmic.

So, all the time of the game(set,play), you will have to make the good decisions and to move forward the good arguments in a short time. Negotiation and reactivity will be your main weapons. But pay attention: if you and your colleagues if you do not hear enough, it will be the Mafia who eventually win the game.

Travel size MindTrap card pack what focused less on the storytelling mysteries and riddles like the classic game and more on pattern recognition, logic problems and well, geometric riddles.

Objective: earn as much money as possible carrying goods with your ship all over the world - game ends after 50 rounds
 - acting/moving by turns -
each player buys goods (max 2 - only one per kind of goods) at your starting port; roll the die and move forward; ending at a port you may sell goods (up to your choice) and may buy (whats available - if there is space enough) at the current rate; 
if the marker - after each player has finished her/his turn - is moved forward ending on a red space draw as many 'chance cards' as ships are in a port; before next turn all changes have to be executed. 
Starting round 50 each player has to move her/his ship to the next port.

The object of the game is to identify the bird species in as few clues as possible in order to win the most points for that card.  With a point scale of one to five, a player can earn the most points by identifying the bird in just one clue.  If it takes a player five clues before identifying the bird, then one point is awarded.  A beautiful photo of the bird is on one side of the card for all to see, after the bird has been identified, or if all the clues have been given.

Player with the most points is the winner.

MOVE 'N FLIP is a 2 player Strategy game played on a 8x8 Chess Board.

Each player has 12 pieces which are double sided – with different directions of movement on each side. At the end of each move the piece is ‘flipped’ to the other side giving different directions for the next move.

Movement is similar to DRAUGHTS/CHECKERS i.e. either a single move or a jumping move over one or more of your own AND/OR your opponents’ pieces. Any opponents pieces jumped over are captured and removed from the board.

The sole survivor is the winner!

From Designer's Web Site

Welcome to the world of Monster Truck Rally! You are the driver of a monster truck, bent on the destruction of the smaller cars. Will you race to the finish in the races, or will you smash the course in the freestyle course?

Monster Truck Rally will have three different types of game play,you will have Racing, Free Style Stunt events, and a 12 part Campaign game using both racing and free style.

Winning Conditions:
The winning conditions depend upon which style of game play is used. If you only use the racing aspect of the game, then it is the fastest truck. If you use the freestyle aspect then it is the truck with the highest point total. However if you use the campaign game the winner is the truck with the most points (money) after all 12 events.

description:
User summary

A tile-laying game for 2 players. The game consists of square tiles with blue and red stripes on them. Players take turns placing the tiles so that they match, and the goal of the game is to get, in your colour, a stripe loof that covers 5 or more tiles.

From Spiel 2011, a sample version of Blockers!, packaged with Zatre 2 in 1 Schnupperspiel.

This version plays 2-3, unlike the full-size version, which plays 2-5. The grid is 5x5, rather than the 9x9 of the regular version.

It uses one game board and 48 cardboard tokens:

A, B, C, D, E, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, Club, Moon, Diamond, Circle, Star, and Blockers in red, yellow, and blue.

The goal is to have the fewest number of groups of tiles on the board at the end of the game.

The players take turns placing one of their tiles on the grid and then draw another tile to replace it. There are three tiles which each player can place on each space: a letter (rows), a number (columns), or the shape on the space of the board (five areas; the corners plus diamonds form a cross in the center).

If another player has placed a tile it may be captured by playing a legal tile in its place, only if removing the tile doesn't break a group of tiles belonging to the player into more than one group. If players tie for the fewest number of groups at the end of the game, then the winner is the one who captured the fewest opponents' tiles.

From Spiel 2011, a sample version of Zatre, packaged with Blockers! 2 in 1 Schnupperspiel.

This version uses 25 cardboard tokens (five 1-pip, and four each of 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6) versus the 121 tokens of the regular version.

Zatre is short for Zahlen & Treppen, numbers and steps. Players alternately draw and place tiles on the board extending the pattern already laid. When you make a vertical or horizontal line that totals 10, 11 or 12 (12 is the limit), you score 1, 2 or 4 points accordingly.

Simple and quick, but surprisingly challenging when you get the idea. The idea being that you must keep an even, balanced score pad, scoring 10s and 11s, not just successive 12s. Easy to pick up, but it requires some effort to score well; spotting those opportunities to place one tile which scores each way, making sure you keep clocking up 10s, to turn into 11s or 12s.

boardgame: Omen: A Reign of War – Echoes of Myth
description:
The fires of war have begun to smolder, and it's clear that an end to the Reign of War is in sight.

Echoes of Myth, the final planned expansion for Omen: A Reign of War, introduces twelve brand new units, including an all new unit type: Spirits. When a Spirit enters a City, it has two different abilities, and you choose which one you will use. However, you have the option of paying the Spirit's cost, plus a little extra, and discarding it from your hand to use both abilities.
family: Game: Omen A Reign of War

 - moving by turns -
place your flower board in front of you and move forward according die roll:
ending on 
an arrow => roll die again
a blank space => miss one turn
a space with white dots => take as many red meeples as dots you stand on and place them on the board
a space with black dots => remove as many red meeples as dots you stand on

Designed to be played by up to four people on a road trip, where players are searching for different items outside the car. Items inside the car cannot be used. The driver may have to referee, but should not try to play – be safe and keep your eyes on the road!

Each player needs a game book and a pencil.
All players are racing to spot the same items. When you spot an item - call it out and make sure you have a witness to your find. Whoever spots an item first checks it off - while
everyone else must keep looking for the next occurrence of that item.

Player who gets 3 (or 4) items first is the winner of the round and he gets the sticker to their book. Once a winner is declared, move on to the next round.

Player with the most stickers at the end of the game is the winner.

Designed to be played by up to four people on a road trip, Players search for combinations of letters and numbers on license plates to make a winning hand. The driver may have to referee, but should not try to play – be safe and keep your eyes on the road!

Each player needs a game book and a pencil.
A license plate is dealt when your vehicle either passes - or is passed - by a vehicle in the next lane. 
Once everyone has finished collecting their license
plates, it’s time for each player to figure out their best hand to check their boxes on their book.

Player who has the most boxes checked get the sticker on his book.

Player with the most stickers at the end of the game is the winner.

Designed to be played by up to four people on a road trip, CarTag Letters & Numbers has players searching for letters, numbers, and words on passing signage and vehicles (including license plates). Items inside the car cannot be used. The driver may have to referee, but should not try to play – be safe and keep your eyes on the road!

Each player needs a game book and a pencil.
All players are racing to spot the same items. When you spot an item - call it out and make sure you have a witness to your find. Whoever spots an item first checks it off - while
everyone else must keep looking for the next occurrence of that item.

Player who gets 3 (or 4) items first is the winner of the round and he gets the sticker to their book. Once a winner is declared, move on to the next round.

Player with the most stickers at the end of the game is the winner.

Space Dogsbody is a quirky game of space exploration, resource collection, and reputation management. The players fly around space mining asteroids, fulfilling menial missions, ferrying tourists, dodging pirates and upgrading their ships, trying to make a name for themselves and some quick cash!

40% of the board starts off face-down, and players will have to explore these tiles by flying onto them, hoping to find sources of valuable minerals or lost space stations offering powerful upgrades.

There are also tourists who will pay to be carried around space, as long as the players have a Spare Bed for the tourist to sleep on in the cargo bay. Upgrades like Business Cards will increase the reputation payout for completing missions, and the players can sell Advertising Space on the side of their ship for another source of income.

Players acquire reputation in each of four categories, all of which carry sizeable monetary rewards for certain levels. Merchant reputation comes from selling resources, Freelancer from fulfilling missions, Hacker from installing upgrades on your ship, and Vigilante from destroying pirates with lasers or explosives.

Objective: be the first collecting 6 flowers - learn the names of different flowers
 - moving by turns -
simple 'Roll & Move' game, collecting flowers; ending on a space without flowers name those flowers you've collected already - this rule changed within the Spika edition to: miss one turn

Objective: gain as many winning points as possible
 - moving/acting by turns -
each player moves according die roll - ending on a space draw a card with a matching symbol: 
try to manage your task successfully (a bit like 'Activity');
if it was accepted by majority of players gain that cart (winning points are shown - depending on difficulty)

typical tasks are: 
- mimic a profession
- name two different fishes
- repeat 2x the sentences:"Esel essen Nessel gern, Nessel essen Esel gern." witout any mistake
- count down from 40 to 20; replace multiples of 5 & 7 with a special name...

Colorfast is an abstract game where players each represent a primary color.  On their turn, players will either place a cube of their color, or convert an existing cube to another color that is one away from the current one on the color spectrum (red, orange, yellow, green, blue and purple).  The object is to get five cubes in a row made of your primary color and any derivative secondary colors (e.g. red, purple and orange).  When (re)placing cubes, players may not choose a space that is adjacent (orthogonally or diagonally) to one of the last two pieces played on.

Rules are changed slightly for the 2 player game, having each player also take a turn with a neutral color (yellow).

Microbadge: 


User Summary

This set of rules was in constant use for the better part of two decades by the Western Washington Wargamers (W3). Imperialism is an eminently playable and reasonably realistic set of miniatures rules.

The game is a simultaneous movement wargame, utilizing paper maps (or overlays and grease pencils) to plot movement.  The game also uses casualty record sheets to record "damage" prior to a stand being eliminated. Six sided dice are used for combat randomization and modified according to a unit's type and morale.

The rules were originally written for use with 20mm/25mm figures, however the game is easily scaled to 15mm (and even 6mm or 2mm).

Appendices include army lists and values (so that custom armies can be created), suggested reading and viewing lists, and sources of miniature figures (several of which are still valid 30 years later).

Game description from the publisher:

Battle rages through the halls of Subterra Bravo as unearthly creatures and human military fight over control of the underground facility. Amid the turmoil, a few desperate prisoners have managed to escape this hellish place.

You were not so lucky.

Subterra Bravo is now locked down, its exits sealed – and with them, perhaps your fate. Your only hope is to descend back into the bowels of this nightmare facility in search of another way out, but waiting within its unexplored depths are new dangers and even more deadly denizens, ready to put a grisly end to your bid to escape. Can you and your fellow survivors overcome your fears and the horrors that stand in your way, or will you fall prey to the frenzied monsters that hunt you?

LEVEL 7 [ESCAPE]: Lockdown is a death-defying expansion for LEVEL 7 [ESCAPE] that adds new enemies, room tiles, and rules along with five new scenarios that will put your will to survive to the ultimate test.

boardgame: Once Upon a Time: Enchanting Tales
description:
Publisher Blurb:

In Once Upon a Time, players tell a story together using cards that show fairytale elements and endings. Now add more magic to your game with the Enchanting Tales expansion. Inspired by enchanted princesses like Cinderella and Snow White, the 38 Story Cards and 17 Ending Cards in this set can be shuffled into your Once Upon a Time storytelling card game deck to add new themes and more variety to your game. For an even stronger magical feel, try removing some of the core Story or Ending Cards.

description:
Battle for York is an area control/battle game set in a fictional world inspired by the Napoleonic Wars of the 19th century. The Empire of York has fallen apart after centuries of misrule. Now, many factions have formed, each with a different idea for how the next Empire should be run.  The game plays with 2-4 players in an hour or less.

In this low-luck game, players carefully manage their hands of cards for one of two purposes: adding reinforcements or activating powerful tactics which may aid them in battle. There are no dice in Battle for York, so victory in battle is the result of player decision, planning, and sometimes, a careful bluff.

The game features 4 unique factions, each with unique tactics and abilities to gain the upper hand in the conflict. Every faction plays differently, which provides a deep and varied strategy game in a short amount of time. For first time players, a generic shared faction is provided to help you learn the game and the mechanics before diving into the complexities of factions.

Players take one action at a time, so there is little down time in this fast-paced game. Battle for York is a great light to medium game for game night.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

boardgame: A Game of Tales and Thrilling Stories: Adventure 1 – The Museum Case
description:
You are a group of four kids and best friends. It seemed to be a day like every other day when something happened that got you really upset. A boy from your neighborhood stole money from one of your friends. You decide to get the money back. The thief hides in an old villa and you go inside. This is where the adventure begins that will turn to The Museum Case.

A Game of Tales and Thrilling Stories is a rules system for story-driven board games that does not require one player to take the role of a game master or narrator of the story. The system is similar to paragraph driven games, but follows a pre-defined story and therefore provides a more integrated story experience. The story elements are not written in a paragraph book but communicated through cards.

Game description from the publisher:

Mage Knight: Resurrection is a fantasy miniatures line utilizing the Combat Dial System game platform that the original Mage Knight game pioneered. Each of the 25 miniatures in the line will incorporate SwitchClix technology, and include two dials: one designed for the Mage Knight 2.0 rules, and a second HeroClix P.A.C. -compatible dial.

Mage Knight: Resurrection will be sold in both full starter sets and collectible booster packs.

The battle of Bannockburn in 1314 defended the Scottish nation and was a major milestone on the path to the independence. The  Scottish highlanders slaughtered the mighty English army that included more than 2000 knights and 15000 infantrymen.

The game allows to play the battle of Bannockburn as well as two predecessors: battle of Stirling (1297) and battle of Falkirk (1298). Players are given the command over English heavy cavalry or Scottish shiltrons and their goal is to eliminate units and leaders of the opponent and/or secure strategic targets of the army.

This is a typical hex and counter game. Initiative is determined in each scenario and kept for the entire game. Players perform their actions in order, with possible reactions (when enemy units enter missile range).

Example:
1. Player A moves cavalry (reaction fire possible)
2. Cavalry of player A attacks
3. Player A moves infantry (reaction fire possible)
4. Infantry of player A attacks
5. Player B moves cavalry etc.

Movement is determined by the terrain and the unit type. The battles are determined by comparing unit strength, which is modified by various factors (armor, charge, terrain, leaders etc.), and dice roll. Some interesting elements of game mechanics include  fatigue of units, morale and cavalry movement (turning requires more space).

The game includes: three paper maps (2 made of two parts that should be duct-taped together or glued, on a single part), chits with units (punchable) and additional chits (for marking "charge", "fatigue" modifiers) that need to be cut out.

This game uses the "Medieval" system of "Taktyka i Strategia", implemented also in Grunwald 1410 - revised and updated. Therefore it can be used with other games from the series. 
An english translation of the rules with scenario and examples is provided within the game.


A number of scenarios for this game was published in Taktyka i Strategia magazine:
- Pięść Szkocji (No. 39)
- Falkirk Stream (No. 38)
- Falkirk Otimus (No. 37)
- Szarża Gloucastera (No. 20)
- Walna Bitwa (No. 20)
- Strumień Bannock (No. 19)
- Niebieska Linia (No 19)
- Stirling Maximus (No. 16)
- Królewskie Polowanie (No. 16)
- Waleczne Serca (No. 15)

A card party game.

Gather friends around the table, select the level of "hotness" of the actions to be done (from simple questions on lower levels, 1 to 3, to actions on higher levels 5 to 7. Level 4 is a balanced level).

Once you've chosen 3 levels to play with, the first player draws a card from the pool and select one of the two action written on it. Ask a player to execute it;

If he doesn't what he'll have to draw a Supplices card and will have to execute the action written down on it. You'll keep the card and get the points associated with it.

If he does the action, he will take the card and it's associated point.

The game continues like this until the deck of action card is completly depleted. The winner is the one with the most points.

Higher level cards score more points.

Brass: Catalonia is based on applying the mechanics of Brass to the Industrial Revolution in Catalonia. The rules changes reflect the geographical constraints in Catalonia and the different process of industrialization.

To play, you will need the following from the files section:
- the jpg map of Catalonia.
- the new deck of cards (you can also get them at Artscow).
- the one-page rules booklet (in fact, three pages, but they are each in one language: Catalan, Spanish and English).

RULES CHANGES FROM BRASS

1.    The Canal Period is now the Road Period.

2.    In the Road Period, a player may have as many Level 1 industry tiles as they wish in a given location, if and only if they are all Level 1 industries; mixing of Level 1 and higher industry level tiles from the same player at the same location is not allowed.

3.    The building of a cotton mill in a location with hydraulic power (marked with the turbine symbol) gives a price reduction of 2 PTA in buying coal.

4.    You can sell cotton overland to the Peninsula as long as the cotton mill is not connected to any face-up port. In that case in the act of selling you just flip the mill counter to its other side.

5.    Selling cotton to a Distant Market is also possible, but only through Barcelona, that needs an already built port.

(Source: rules booklet)

This is a simple spin the spinner and move game where you begin your adventure at sea as the lowly CABIN BOY and by successfully moving around the board eventually get promoted to Captain and win the game.

Complete game: 10 game pieces, spinner, & box. The bottom of the box is the game board

This is an 11 card expansion for Briefcase.

It includes 8 City Hall office cards and 3 Inspector cards.

Offices: For each game players randomly choose 4 of those offices to use in the game. At the start of each turn, each player may activate one office and take advantage of its one-time ability.

Inspectors: All Inspector cards are shuffled in the Buy deck. If, while producing a new B card, a player reveals an Inspector card, he/she must also place the Inspector card in to his/her discard pile.
When a player draws an Inspector card, he/she may play a Buy card to pass the Inspector card to the player on their left. When the game ends, players lose one victory point for each Inspector card remaining in their deck.

500 questions about the Swedish language in 5 different categories, such as dialect & slang and funny synonym. Besides the cards with questions and answers the game also includes category cards to determine from which category questions are asked. All cards fit in the little cube shaped box.

A fast bidding card game that expands upon the CON! Card Game.  Similar in rules, this one represents a convention going badly for all involved.  Slightly darker humor with the guests, game now includes guests that factor in traits about the players themselves.

From the Malifaux Tactica Wiki:

A long ranged minion that can hit hard from a distance at a medium cost. This minion can be hired by any Ten Thunders Crew. This Minion is best used away from the main fighting. It has some very good tricks to get shots in but only has bash in melee. It has a couple of good zero point actions to bolster its attacks and also a 1 point action called Take Aim. This is a (1) action focus strike giving you a positive flip on your shot but can only be used once per activation.

The best thing about this model is that its ranged attacks are usually further than an opposing minions range. This enables you to move it in to place to cover your melee fighters. Remember to use the Threading the Needle ability by moving it with something like a Torokage as it can effectively shoot through the model to the enemy. Remeber the Take Aim will usually be your first shot and then decide whether you need to use Reading the Wind if you have low cards in your hand or if the model you are shooting at is in cover or the Spell Yajiri with the second attack to bolster its damage or cause non living models to slow down.

From the Malifaux Tactica Wiki:

Mei Feng is a melee caster. She excels at fighting multiple opponents, being able to dart into combat using her own constructs as stepping stones, once engaged she can move from opponent to opponent laying down damage and burning tokens. Mei Feng can combo her spells and strikes together into chains, which though card intensive allow her to pull off some impressive feats.

When starting a Mei Feng crew, you will have to buy the Rail Crew box set (Mei is not available to purchase individually at present). This allows you to build a crew for either Arcanists, or 10 Thunders, which will affect which other models you may wish to purchase. The Rail Crew contains Mei Feng, Kang, the Emberling, and 3 Rail Workers. This is all that you need to play either an Arcanist crew, or 10 thunders crew at the 25 soulstone level.

Mei can hire fire gamin, metal gamin, Willie the demolitionist, and a Rail Golem regardless of which faction she is used with. She may never use any models which have the frozen heart ability.

From the Malifaux Tactica Wiki

Not a minion at all, but a suit of armour resurrected at the whim of its master. Izamu is an ancient blood thirsty warlord that lived during the old days of the Katanaka clan that Yan Lo was also a member of. Today, he is called upon by Yan Lo to bring death to all those that oppose him.

Izamu's mobility is pretty unimpressive with a bog standard 4/6 move/charge and only one mobility trick to speak of which is Shove Aside (allows Izamu to nove through ht1 and 2 models). He does have a very respectable 3" melee range though. He also benefits greatly from mobility tricks given by other models, be it the Soul Porter with Empower Ancestor, Toshiro with Tenki no Juden or Molly and Imbue Vigor.

Now we are cooking with gas! Izamus is a melee monster packing Melee Expert, a base + to all his damage flips, a respectable combat of 6 on a range 3 weapon and a very nice damage spread of 3/4/6. That damage spread is his edge compared to the dead rider, Izamu can nuke a most low mid cost models in one severe strike. He also has a cute trigger with his Daodao that lets his weapon be considered magical if you get a tome which is nice since it's a suit ressers don't use much and is always nice to have that possibility when facing spirit heavy crews.

Ironically, Izamu does not even have an armour rating. Instead he is an Object 2, which is actually better. This means he is reducing 2 damage from anything heading his way unless it ignores object and for now, very few things actually do that. His tankiness doesn't end there, he is also immune to influence, so WP strikes do nothing against him. He is able to nullify enemy triggers if he cheats fate on his defense and resist flips, the suit of the card he uses to cheat will not cause any of the triggers the enemy may be packing, so Lady Justice getting you down? cheat with a Ram and yeah, you may be taking the hit, but there is no onslaught or critical strike going in with it.

From the Malifaux Tactica Wiki:

Oiran are the Ten Thunders escorts in Malifaux. They understand the power of words, and find their place in the city by whispering secrets and lies, twisting others to their objectives.

Oiran only have their Concealed Weapon, which is close range. If Oiran is Harmless when they make a Concealed Weapon Strike, you can choose to add a positive flip to either the attack or damage flip, which helps them out a lot.

Oiran are Harmless, and have two ways to regain Harmless after they have lost it, (1) Sultry, and a Cb trigger for killing a model with Concealed Weapons.
Expert Defense improves the benefits of taking a Defensive Stance, giving Oiran an extra positive flip, and only reduces walk and charge by -1/-1.

The Defensive Dance trigger allows Oiran to assume a Defensive Stance after casting Tranquil Dance for a 6+ mask. If you have the card to spare, this allows you to buff your crew or debuff the opponent, if in range, while still protecting Oiran.
If fighting a Wp duel heavy list, keeping models around Oiran can be very useful. She can (0) Appealing to give Wp +1, and (1) Tranquil Dance can give a positive flip to Wp Resist Duels to models within 6".

From the Malifaux Tactica Wiki:

A powerful model that uses Burning Tokens to power its spells, abilities and attacks. It can be the heavy hitter many crews lack, and even though expensive, you are getting a massive piece for only 9 (or 10) SS.

The Rail Golem works well with models that can put Burning Tokens on it, but it is self sufficient enough to work with any crew.

The Rail Golem has very significant offensive abilities. Its melee weapon has a good reach, is Paired, and causes grievous damage, and it can use Burning Tokens to get positive twists to attack or damage, or an extra melee attack.

The main "trick" with the Rail Golem is to balance out how it accumulates and uses Burning Tokens. Often, you will need to balance between increasing attacks in a short-term way (by increasing attacks and damage flips, or getting an extra attack), healing up (by burning all tokens for 1 Wd of healing each), or saving them up to Reactivate (which costs 5 tokens, but can be very powerful.)

From the Malifaux Tactica Wiki:

Nekima is a dual role minion with specialized abilities to provide support, and hard hitting melee power. She does however lack, in the resilience department, and costs quite a lot of SS to bring on the table.

Since this model’s release, there was an important change to the Nephilim Heart ability, restricting its application to Terror Tots and Young Nephilims only. This prevented some abuse from the synergy Nekima had with other models, but also severely limited her support abilities to specific minions.

With the latest FAQ update, Wyrd stated that “Nekima will get her turn” so we can expect that somewhere down the future, she will get a touch up, but there is no way to know to what extent this will affect her.

Nekima is quite specific with her synergies. She works well with the typical Nephilim growth list or other Young Nephilim and Terror Tot based lists. However intended, Nekima’s support abilities have short range, and her melee prowess is only really useable if she’s protected from harm.

Nekima plays hand-in-hand with growth lists, because she has means to provide blood counters. Rather than draining blood when she kills, she gets a count whenever a living or undead model dies within 3". This occurs automatically, and even if another model used its Drain Blood, such as a Terror Tot. Remember that most Nephilim are alive and she will get a counter from them too when they die.

The Chemical Chaos promotional expansion pack adds 14 new Compounds and 1 additional Lab Fire to your game of Compounded (marked by chemical element symbol in corner of cards). These mix in more strategic play and chaos to the game.

When playing with the Chemical Chaos expansion pack, remove the starting Compound cards from the game. Shuffle the 14 new Compounds into the Compound deck and set up the starting research field with all random Compounds. Then separate the deck into 7 stacks and add the 6 Lab Fires. Game play then continues per the normal rules.

Contents:
1 Lab Fire
3 Isocyanic Acid
3 Cyanogen
3 Disulfur Dinitride
2 Hydrogen Cyanide
3 Hydroxylamine

Trivia game that combines choclate-tasting with  knowledge about choclate. Each correct question advances the player's meeple along a track and at specific stages in the track, the players taste the chocolate. Then at the end a blind test of all chocolates will take place and each correctly identified chocolate adds points to the players score. The person with most points at that stage wins the game.
