description:
From the Malifaux Tactica Wiki:

Lucius is tricky. His crew generally consists of lower cost models such as the Guild Guards. Even his "heavy hitters" do not pump out a lot of damage on their own, but rely on a combined arms approach. He, of everyone in the Guild, supports his crew the most and loses well over half of his capability when not running his own crew. His limited crew of Guardsmen and Elite Division can pull off some cool "alpha strike" tactics, but it's pretty tricky to make them work. Focusing on any single part of his crew can take it apart quickly and make it tougher to play.

Overall, Lucius crew is all about the whole being greater than the sum of it's parts. For the most part your crew will be made of very "average" models (average movement, average attack, average resilience), but can be made to work very well by using a combined arms approach.

The Elite Division box comes with 1 Guild Guard (4 SS), Ryle (8 SS), the Guild Guard Captain (7 SS) and a Lawyer (7 SS), for a total of 26 Soulstones (plus Lucius' cost of 10 SS if not leading the crew). The starter box thus gives you a very top-heavy crew, with a trio of high-cost minions. Lucius tends to work best when he has a good number of minions around him however, so your first purchases should definitely be cheaper models with which to replace one of the 7-8SS minions in small games.

Your first purchase after the starter box should definitely be a pack of Guild Guard, who are cheap, resiliant and decent in both melee and ranged combat. Adding at least one Austringer is recommended to begin with. Good early buys are a blister of Guild Guard, a second Austringer and one of the Totems. Which one you choose depends on your play style, but if in doubt the Drill Sergeant has several different uses.

Lucius is trickier than a normal Guild Master due to his reduced list options, but can do well if you use the right pieces at the right time.

From the Malifaux Tactica Wiki:

The Exorcist is a minion for the Guild.

With a walk of 3” and no charge the Exorcist is one of the slowest Guild models. To get around this the Exorcist has a zero action Driven by Purpose which makes the model suffer one wound and pushes it 3”.

With only 7 wounds and a lack of healing in the Guild Faction, the Exorcist will not make much use of this action but it is useful for positioning him or pushing him out of melee range.

The Exorcist is armed with a Sanctified Crossbow. This weapon has range of 10” and is Magical. Like most Guild models it has Critical Strike built in, giving the weapon a nice damage profile of 3/4/5 with the abilities to get to 4/5/6. With a CB of 5 the Exorcist is likely to hit most minions.

The Exorcist enables Lady Justice to deal full damage against Spirits by granting her already impressive Greatsword Magical. Combined with that and Blind Justice to ignore Pitiful and Harmless, she can shore up her weakness against Kirai with this minion.

From the Malifaux Tactica Wiki:

Scales of Justice is a Totem for Lady Justice. He is on the average scale for a lot of its stats.

The part where the Scales shine brightest though, is its Tip the Scales spell. It requires a high card, but it can be any suit, and if cast, can inflict a positive, or negative twist on its target during its next duel. The opponent can resist it, but if you manage to cast it, it can set up a huge power play. Or, cast it on a friendly target, and give it a better chance at fighting, or resisting a spell.

The Scales also have one (0) action, that allows you to cycle your hand, one card at a time. Use it to get rid of any low cards you might not want, and draw a new one.

From the Malifaux Tactica Wiki:

He's a guy with a bird. How scary can that be? The answer, apparently, is "very scary." With a very long range and the ability to ignore Line of Sight, the Austringer has quickly become a premier Guild Minion.

The Austringer has an average 4" Walk, cannot Charge and beyond Quick Retreat (see below), has no other way of enhancing his own mobility. Fortunately, after the Austringer finds a nice piece of terrain to hide in, his long reach means he'll rarely needs to move anyway.

The Austringer is armed with a Raptor - a Range 12 weapon that ignores cover modifiers and Line of Sight. This means you can sit behind a huge piece of blocking terrain and still attack up to 12" away, ignoring any negative flips from cover that your opponent is trying to use to avoid getting hit.

The Austringer has little direct synergy with any one Master. The closest is Lucius (a Henchman), but in any case, he still makes for a good choice in your average Guild crew, regardless of who's leading.

From the Malifaux Tactica Wiki:

A minion for the Guild.

The Guild's own Rider, the Pale Rider is a fast-moving ranged combatant, with some clever ways to get increased offensive ability, resilience, and mobility. Depending on when you activate the Rider, the Pale Rider can be a hard-hitting ranged attacker or a fairly resilient and fast (if expensive) objective-grabber.

Like the other Riders, the Pale Rider is fairly fragile for its cost, and likely to be destroyed quickly if directly targeted. However, the Rider is full of tricks and options to keep that from happening. Also like all the Riders, the Pale Rider has a complex adjustable Ability that you can use to give the Pale Rider different abilities depending on your need.

The Pale Rider is a Guild model and offers a lot of mobility to the usually-slow Guild, but it also has significant synergy with Leveticus. You're most likely to see the Pale Rider with either C. Hoffman or Leveticus.

C. Hoffman can benefit from the Pale Rider's fast movement with his Drawn to Metal ability, and can boost the Pale Rider with healing, Reactivations, or just using its attacks with Machine Puppet. The Pale Rider is one of the three most likely models for carrying Hoffman around (alongside the Peacemaker and the Mechanical Rider.)

Leveticus doesn't have a lot of synergy with the Pale Rider as a Master, but the Pale Rider usually operates solo anyway, and Avatar Leveticus gets significant benefit from having the Pale Rider in play. Avatar Leveticus can also summon the Pale Rider after Manifesting.

Other Guild Masters may benefit from the Pale Rider's speed, since many Guild models are quite slow.

From the Malifaux Tactica Wiki:

The Peacekeeper is the Guild's massive premiere construct. Its heavy armor and powerful weaponry are offset by a pair of setback Talents that can Paralyze the Peacekeeper or reduce its defense.

Despite its bulk, the Peacekeeper is a very fast model, especially considering it's a Guild model. His movement is slightly above average 5/8, but he is allowed to discard a card to take an additional Walk action each activation due to (+1) Relentless.

The Peacekeeper has two separate abilities it can use to move models. This is a great way to put enemy models into harmful terrain. It is also a great way to get them to drop a Treasure Counter or just move them off the terrain they need to be on for there Strategy or Scheme.

The Peacekeeper is a big mean-looking model and people naturally want to take him out. This is actually a great trick to take advantage of. Many players will concentrate a lot of firepower on the Peacekeeper and ignore the rest of your crew. The fact that the Peacekeeper can survive a beating means they have to dedicate resources for several turns sometimes. Take advantage of this. Let your enemy take out the Peacekeeper while you maneuver to take the Strategies and Schemes you need to win the game

Like most Guild Minions there is not a whole lot of synergy with the Peacekeeper. He works okay in Perdita and Lady Justice crews. He does better in Sonnia Crews because he can use his abilities to push models onto Flame Pillars.

The exception is Hoffman. In a lot of ways they seem made for each other. Hoffman can make the Peacekeeper ignore his Experimental Automation and Faulty Circuits abilities. Meanwhile the Peacekeeper can be a great ride for Hoffman.

Leveticus
This Guild construct is a big brutal enforcer with some solid stats and good Cb. It will return its investment in Scrap Counters on death as well. However, it can be unreliable and is a Control Card hog, so be wary as it could leave Leveticus high and dry. There is no additional benefit to giving this minion Blessings of Desolation.

From the Malifaux Tactica Wiki:

Hoffman is a Master for the Guild.

Appropriately enough, C. Hoffman crews play like a machine full of complex moving parts with Hoffman as the clockwork heart of the whole amalgamation. He both grants power to his constructs and borrows their own, creating powerful synergies between himself and his metal bodyguards. His low speed is offset by his ability to catch a ride (from Drawn to Metal) with a nearby construct. Hoffman does not fight the enemy directly, instead he enhances his Constructs to fight for him even taking temporary control of them with Machine Puppet to make a Strike. But all of this power and synergy with his crew comes at a price, as he is very reliant on his crew for his success and if you are too careless with them you will soon find Hoffman stuck in the middle of the fight without anyone to help him.

Naturally, C. Hoffman's synergy with Constructs makes them the best choice for his crew. The Guild offers these in the forms of the Guardian, the Hunter, the Peacekeeper, the Watcher, and Ryle. C. Hoffman's Arcanist Ties ability gives him access to the Steampunk Arachnid, Large Steampunk Arachnid, the Steamborg Executioner, Joss, and the Soulstone Miner.

Minion constructs included in Hoffman's starter box:

The Guardian - With potent spells like Shield Wall and Protect, plus the ability to armor nearby enemies, a healing flip that can be Assimilated, and a way to keep up with the rest of the crew, the Guardian is a stellar addition for any Constructs of Order crew. His weapons include a Sword and Shield, the latter granting a damage flip (with 2 negative twists) even on a miss, guaranteeing a minimum of 2 damage when used in tandem with Machine Puppet.

The Hunter - With the ability to shadow speedier models, the Hunter offers a much needed independent Construct to guard objectives and chase down models. It's not going to stand up to a lot of punishment, however, so make sure to utilize Ambush when possible, and keep it at range of your foes.

The Watcher - It not only has an invaluable set of deck maintenance abilities, it also has some great abilities for C. Hoffman to Assimilate, notably Slow to Die.

Colovero is an easy trick-takting game.

rules from the publishers homepage:

The player to the left of the dealer is the first to play a card. In clockwise order, all the other players also play a card. Once all players have played their card, the orange and green cards are scored separately. The color with the higher score wins the trick. If the colors tie, the color with the highest individual card wins. If that is also a tie, the color with the highest card played last wins.

If a joker is part of the trick, it wins the trick for its color, no matter what the score is. If both jokers are played, the joker played last wins. The trick goes to the player who played the winning card. The player who won the trick places the trick in front of himself with the winning card face up.

At the end of the round, when all players have played their nine cards, the color that won the most tricks in that round is the winning color.

Players receive one point for each trick of the winning color and lose one point for each trick of the losing color.  This total score is the result of this round. The results of the rounds are added up as you keep playing. It is up to the players to decide how many rounds they would like to play. At the end of the game, the player with the highest score wins.

A variant of Tiddlywinks. Three games are described.

Game 1: Normal Tiddlywinks.

Game 2. Place the board flat across the empty box. Each player has six small counters and one big one. A counter is flipped towards the target board which is mostly green but also a slaller white area and a hole in the centre. Each counter can only be flipped once. If you miss the target you score nothing with that counter. If you can get a counter on to the board, score as follows:
Green = 25 points
White = 50 points
In the hole = 100 points.
Play 6 turns each and the one with the best score wins.

Game 3: Use the Goal board. Stand it up on two supports so that it is vertical. Now take turns to flick a counter through the hole. Each goal scored is recorded. After an agreed time or number of turns, the player with the most goals wins.

Experience the legendary leather industry of Ubrique. Play as one of the main leather manufacturing companies and grow from a simple workshop to a leading factory.

Each player begins the game by selecting one of Ubrique's most famous factories. All players begin as modest workshops with a limited workforce. At the start of each turn, two 'market' cards are placed on the board. You may find either an 'order' or an 'event'. Events happen automatically and you will have to follow the instructions on them. As for orders, you may pick one out and begin manufacturing for top international fashion brands. On order cards you'll find how many handbags and wallets need to be made and how much you'll be getting paid when delivered. The bigger the order, the more workforce you'll need. The solution is hiring external workshops. But be careful! There's only a limited amount of workshops in Ubrique, so make sure they're not manufacturing for other players!

Also, in order to manufacture handbags and wallets you'll be purchasing real leather tokens! The more packages you buy, the lower the cost. It's up to you to decide how much you're willing to invest.

Given your limited starting funds and factory size, it's always a good idea to start off with small orders. As the game goes on you'll be able to expand your factory and increase your workforce, requiring less external workshops to complete small orders and making it possible to pick up bigger orders.

In the advanced rules, players may negotiate with each other, trade leather, and share orders - just like in real life.

'El Rey de la Piel' is an educational game that faithfully reflects the leather goods industry, where victory depends mostly on business strategy than luck.

The goal of the game is to expand your factory to the highest level and become the leading factory in Ubrique.

FIVE-OH! Is a card game with a humorous approach towards society’s finest.  The “boys in blue”.  Collect cards to form units of different types of cops.  Solve crimes as they pop up with specific units you have gathered.  Watch out, there are action cards also that can change the police force you have so diligently been recruiting!  You never know what will come up, just like in real life “on the beat”.

Players start with five cards in their hand.  During game play, you have numerous different actions to choose from on your turn.  You are attempting to collect different types of "cop" cards in order to "address" calls for service you choose to play.  Solving or rather "resolving" calls for service results in points, depending on the difficulty of the call for service and the number/types of cops required.  The winner is the first one to acquire 50 points worth of solved calls for service.  The trick to that is you want to make sure you don't have a lot of unresolved calls in front of you because they count against your final score at the end!

The game incorporates dice as well for "shootout" cards.  A simple mechanic in which the bad guys hit on a roll of "6" and the cop(s) hit on a "5" or "6".  Winning shootouts get you points as well, losing shootouts costs you cops you have gathered.

There are also actions that allow you to recruit (steal) cops from other players (precincts), get extra cards for "grants", etc.

There is a lot going on in this game!  You must keep an eye on your opponent's precinct, as well as try to manage your own.

Space Miners of the Remna Sector are cutthroat crews. They’ll do anything to score the next mother lode! When the government opened up a new Parsec for mining, they leapt at the chance to be the first in!
 Players are competing to harvest the most value out of Asteroids around the game board. Many asteroids are  of hidden value and so player totals are secret, so you never know the true winner until the end!

The primary mechanic is drafting one-at-a-time, and then programming in "Commands" for your ship from a pool of Commands cards dealt anew each turn.  Matching Commands can be assigned to "Special Commands" that do special (and mostly powerful) things.  There are six ships to choose from at the start of the game, each with a different set of Special Commands.  The gameboard is composed of modular squares, so the board changes every game.
family: Players: Games with expansions that change player count

description:
BattleAxe
by Nick Ryan

BattleAxe is an dark age battlefield warefare simulation tabletop game for 2 players (or 2 teams) that allows the players to use their strategic skills against each others armies. The main goal is to kill the opponent's King. Each army consists of 26 numbered men, 2 warhorses. Your army including axe-men, spearmen, swordsmen, archers, an engineer, horsemen and 1 king, all with different and balanced attributes. The fighting/attacking mechanism is an elegant 1 to 3 custom dice rolling system. Each player has the exact same army, similar to Chess, as to see which player really has superior strategic skills. Asymmetrical games can also be played with endless scenarios.

Each player has an "Army Map" sheet for his army which illustrates and keeps track of each warrior and warhorse. Each man in your army has his own health meter, skills, attributes, and even a place for the player to name them. This gives an unparalleled  up-close-and-personal view from the battlefield. Engineers are a big part of the game, they can cut down trees, construct archer towers to enhance their attack range, walls, fire trenches and even make extra arrows for your archers. The concept of fire, from fire arrows and fire trenches symbolized by "wild fire" tiles are also a big part of the game. Fire can spread and take down your walls and towers which creates a real feel of the chaos of war.

It's who the better strategic commander is, not who can level up faster with fairy dust and gold coins.
It's dividing and concurring enraged enemy forces, not gathering grain for the winter.
It's swiftly destroying the opposing King and all his men to become the one true King of the battlefield.
It's BattleAxe.

Objective: be the first reaching target space (150) with a matching die roll
 - moving by turns -

basically a 'snake and ladders' approach with a few specialities:
 - very large board
 - war game theme 
 - belittlement of aerial war
 - according instructions 'suitable for the very young ones' 
which should give this game a special place..
should had been published end 1930's/beginning 40's  
cute meeples
winners (1st/2nd/3rd) get gold/silver/bronze medal

In this lightweight fantasy game you get a random race (elf/undead/human/orc or demon) and a random saga (fight/duel/treasure/mist/crown).

The races all have a leader and 3-4 soldiers. Elf, undead and human have 1 character with a ranged attack.

Each saga has a different goal. Fight saga is a battle between all characters. Duel is just the leaders fighting it out. In Treasure saga you´ll fight to get the most treasure chests. Mist saga is a bit like chinese checkers were you´ll have to get your characters over on the other side and stop your opponent getting across. The crown saga is a battle to have most characters around the crown at games end. The end of the game is random. An hourglas will start at a given time, and when you draw a red hourglass the game ends. The board is also  randomly drawn. You´ll start at opposite ends.

The 5 races has small variations in their stats(move/dodge/attack and defend) and a few characters can shoot, if line-of-sight is good. Each round you can move and attack (close combat or ranged). If you start your turn in combat you´ll have to dodge to be able to move away from combat. All characters have 1 life, so if a character is hit and fails to defend it is dead. You have to roll equal-to or above your stat.

From the back of the box:

An easy and challenging game for the whole family. You are in charge of a group of warriors and must win the saga by using strategy and fantasy. The different groups, sagas and sceneries make the game a new adventure every time you play.

Each player selects a team of generic fantasy creatures, ranging from humans and ełves to undead and demons. Each team consists of 4 to 6 pieces.

When teams have been selected, one of the players rolls a ten-sided dice to determine which saga the players will play. These range from simple "Death matchs" to gathering chests.

Now the players form the board by placing lagre hexagon map pieces. Each map pieces contains smaller hexagons and terrain features like houses, trees and rocks.

Each saga (scenario) has a trigger. When a certain point in the game is reach, for example by losing half of your team, a set of "Time piece" markers is placed beside the game board. Each full turn a marker is turned and when a red time piece is revealed the game stops.

In the end points are calculated by simple measures such how many playing pieces you have left or how many treasure chests you've got.

Are you fast enough to be the first to the nucleus of the egg and getting the rest to leave you behind? Do not hesitate to challenge your friends, siblings or parents for this thrilling game about reproduction.

The first to reach the egg, win glory for himself and his posterity. However, this will not be easy because the road is littered with questions about men, women, pregnancy, love and so much more. As if this was not enough, you should really watch out for the other sperm. They will do everything for you to interfere with you at unexpected moments to attack and chase away!

The Game:
-has an exciting game concept about sex education, combined with scientific content
-lowers the threshold to talk about sexuality
-was developed by experienced authors in collaboration with Sensoa (Flemish Centre for Sexual Education)
-can be played with two to four players or teams

Spin the unpredictable wheel and follow the instructions. You must solve a question to the card you want to get or else the wheel decides your fate? Collect as soon as possible the entire family of one of the 7 vehicles and defeat your opponents. Who will win this game traffic?

It is a family game about road safety and traffic rules. It was read by the cell education of the Belgian Institute for Road Safety.

The game:
-promotes the knowledge of the highway
-allows young and old to think about road safety

Objective: be the first entering space '48' (school)
- moving by turns -
simple 'Roll and Move' game
Roll the die - ending on any space follow instructions printed on the board
remarks:
published ~1900

Legend of Nara: Battle Bugs was a line of electronic action figures based on insects inhabiting a ecologically ravaged planet and battling over energy sources.

The game itself pits the mechanical insects in an arena and are guided by a tether attached to the backside of each bug. The objective is to force the opponent out of the arena. There was also sold a separate racing track set where the bugs could be let loose to careen around the track.

Each unit comes with a core similar to a "nano bug" and an interchangeable insect costume. The shapes and weights of the costumes and cores could have an effect on each bugs speed and maneuverability.

Battle bugs were sold individually, in two bug packs and the racing arena track sold separately.

Product also included an MMO and a transforming action figure set.

Weekend in Vegas is a fun romp through America's playground. Games can range from a 12 turn game(short game) to a 48 turn game (an actual weekend in Vegas). Players choose a character at random who each have some special attributes that give them an edge. The object of Weekend in Vegas is to accumulate the most points before the end of the game. Players gain points by completing various objectives in the game such as seeing shows, riding attractions, dining out on the town and even partying at the clubs. As players scramble around town trying to get it all done they will find it will take cash, comps and even a little luck in order survive a Weekend in Vegas

Objective:
be the first reaching target space
 - moving by turns -
each player rolls the die moving forward; ending on a marked space/image follow instructions...

remarks: due to logo & theme game should had been published between 1936 (Olympic Games in Berlin) and 1939 (change of logo);
game/theme added to Nazi-indoctrination in those days

three different games are proposed:
 - moving/acting by turns -
1. choose one of the six different routes, being the first reaching your target space
2. start at six different positions being the first reaching the centrally located city of 'Eisenach' 
3. be the very last one after hunting/catching successfully all other players

remarks:
should had been published in between 1936/1938

Educational game for young children.

It suggests that an older sibling or relative reads out the cards.

A double word is one that consists ot two separate words making up one word. Clues, riddles really, are on each card. This is read out. The first player to call out the double word, wins a counter and the first to win 5 counters wins the game.

Example of a double word
One word = Salad
Other word = Öl
Together they make Salatöl (a dressing for salads).

The game consists of two sub-games.
In the first game players take the role of journalists and try to get to the sat-van as quick as possible in order to upload the three pictures they have to capture in this game. The pictures are all braunschweig-buildings or sites and have a UFO sighting on them.
This is a Malefiz kind of adaptation.

In the second part the game (backside of the board) journalists try to get the cover-story (word-guessing approach) before other players can beat them by running up the stairs of the publisher's office first.

The game consists of two sub-games.
In the first game players take the role of journalists and try to get to the sat-van as quick as possible in order to upload the three pictures they have to capture in this game. The pictures are all Mainz-buildings or sites and have a UFO sighting on them.
This is a Malefiz kind of adaptation.

In the second part the game (backside of the board) journalists try to get the cover-story (word-guessing approach) before other players can beat them by running up the stairs of the publisher's office first.

This game consists of two sub-games. In the first game players take the role of journalists and try to get to the sat-van as quick as possible in order to upload the three pictures they have to capture in this game. The pictures are all Oberhausen-buildings or sites and have a UFO sighting on them.

This is a Malefiz kind of adaptation.

In the second part the game, journalists try to get the cover-story before the other players can by running up the stairs of the publisher trying to beat the others to his office.

This is a Malefiz kind of adaptation.

A cooperative, card-driven game where players take on the role of ordinary citizens trying to drive of the little green men who are invading their town.

You will move from location to location, rescuing trapped citizens, battling aliens and facing unexpected events.

The objective is to reach the alien landing zone and defeat the alien army in a final, guns-blazing showdown.

GAMEPLAY
The basic mechanic involves building a deck from a random selection of the game's location cards. Each game will be a different experience as players will not know which locations will be available in each play.

Each player chooses a character template (a hero) which will determine the items, skills and abilities the player will have at their disposal.

One at a time, the location cards are revealed and resolved.

There will be events to manage, battles to fight and citizens to save. Heroes will find items as they go, putting these items to use in order to accomplish the task of defeating the aliens.

Combat is handled by rolling a pool of custom dice, which include hero dice, alien dice and friendly fire dice. By managing the results of each roll, the players will battle their way to victory and possibly, to defeat.

Iron-Core is primarily as a vehicle vs. vehicle combat system. Although infantry play an important role, they are not the primary focus of the game. A typical game will consist of one to five armored vehicles and, three to seven squads of infantry or support units.

The game is designed to play like a WWII simulation. Every effort was taken to use and distill actual WWII vehicle and combat data in the creation of the vehicle stats and game play. Unlike modern warfare, not every shot will hit its target and many hits will not penetrate your opponent’s armor and result in an instant kill. Two evenly matched vehicles may find themselves in an extended engagement, trading shot for shot until one finds a soft spot and makes the kill.

The decision to base the play style and game mechanics on a more antiquated technology was a conscious one, born from the desire to add more depth to the combat than a simple “Roll D6, four or better, you kill your target”. Vehicles, regardless of the manufacturer represent more of a financial investment than infantry units do. With this in mind, the number of large units required to play Iron-Core have been reduced as the vehicle and infantry survivability has been extended to a more descriptive and cinematic feel.

Our goal is to provide a game system that rewards tactics and punishes suicide runs. Applying bonuses and penalties based on unit position, unit formations, moral, and support fire and tactics; you will find that an inferior force can defeat a superior force with the application of some tactical forethought. Many of optional rules may slow play but highly recommended.

There are many game systems available, each have their own strengths, weaknesses and focus. If you are looking for a brigade level combat game, Iron-Core may not be the best choice. If you want a dynamic vehicle vs. vehicle combat system where having the biggest and the best does not equate to an “I win” button, then Iron-Core may be the game for you.

Simple, well-made trivia game for 'Canadian' families.  No random die-rolling, just answer the trivia questions and progress across the game board on your choice of routes.

The goal is to be the first player to make a round trip on his/her route across Canada. Players who correctly answer questions in categories including General, Geography, History, and Arts for the 'Expert' players, move forward from city to city, across the board.

Family Edition cards have two sides - one with easier questions and the other with more difficult questions, allowing families of all ages to play together.

Includes 1,440 expert level questions in four categories (geography, history, arts, general) and 1,080 junior level questions in three categories (geography, history, general).

family: History: Wars of the Guelphs and Ghibellines

boardgame: Zen Garden: Tea House and Tools
description:
Zen Garden Rules:

Each player begins the game with a Tea House and Tools tile.

The tile is placed using the same rules as a lantern tile.  However, when you place it, you have a choice which side you want to place face up - Tea House or Tools.

A tea house is treated much like a lantern - it is wild, and it can still be a part of a pattern.  However, it cannot be replaced like a lantern.

A tools tile can be replaced exactly like a lantern.  However, it is a part of NO landscapes.  It cannot be a part of any pattern.

This game consists of two sub-games. In the first game players take the role of journalists and try to get to the sat-van as quick as possible in order to upload the three pictures they have to capture in this game. The pictures are all Essen-buildings or sites and have a UFO sighting on them.

This is a Malefiz kind of adaptation.

In the second part the game, journalists try to get the cover-story before the other players can by running up the stairs of the publisher trying to beat the others to his office.

This is a variant of Bohnanza using horses instead of beans; it is a licensed product for Wendy magazine, a German magazine focusing on horses. (There is also an animated TV show and so forth.)

from the Bohnanza entry:
As card games go, this one is quite revolutionary. Perhaps its oddest feature is that you cannot rearrange your hand, as you need to play the cards in the order that you draw them. The cards are colorful depictions of beans in various descriptive poses, and the object is to make coins by planting fields (sets) of these beans and then harvesting them. To help players match their cards up, the game features extensive trading and deal making.

The game is not identical to Bohnanza. The deck does not include an equivalent to the Garden Beans (6s), and from two to five of each type of horse are foals. When a foal is turned up as one of the cards to be traded, if there is only one player breeding that kind of horse they get it automatically. If a paddock has a foal on top when it is scored, it is worth nothing.

Other differences include the lack of the ability to buy a third paddock (field), the lack of a rule prohibiting scoring a paddock with a single horse under some circumstances, and the inclusion of rules for two players.

Game play is conventional round the board turns with risk and reward squares. The players hold shares in companies and gain or lose money whilst trying to get shares from other players. They can also buy and sell bull and bear positions to bet on rises and falls in company stocks.

The goal of the game is to acquire all the shares of a company, which causes the game to end, and have the most cash when the game ends.

Trenches is a fast-paced card game for two players, set in the trenches of late WWI.

The players will fight off for the best of 3 conflicts in order to win the campaign. Each conflict is decided using victory points, that are awarded by breaking a stand-still between two sides of trenched foes.

Each conflict represents a battle to hold 5 different objectives. To win the objectives, each player drafts a hand of 6 cards that will be played by turn (face down) on a chosen objective, until all players pass or finish their hand.

Then the players reveal the cards and start activating the special actions, in ascending order. Each side tallies the objectives they have obtained to determine if they broke the tie and won the conflict!

The game features a unique intense experience of drafting, deploying, deceiving and triumph!

Objective: be the first ending on space 66 (German coast) - starting in Friedichshafen
- moving by turns -
simple 'Roll & Move' game; cards are drawn to determine starting positions; ending on a marked space follow instructions glued inside the lid.

remarks:
should had been published ~1910

Objective: be the first collecting the key (01.01.2000) & reaching the centre of the board 
 - moving/acting by turns -
Each player moves according die roll with her/his hero, trying to make her/his way from the outer to the inner circles. During this path you move along spaces where you have to draw cards (action/events); collecting numbers/cyphers you are challenged/blocked by the so called 'Whytooky', a bad spirit. To master this spirit use your action cards.
remarks:
game was published short before millennium change - raising  awareness for upcoming challenges. Game was produced by Ravensburger.

Ebenezer Scrooge's Eggnog Dash is a dice-rolling token collection game set in the world of Charles Dickens' Victorian England. The object of the game is to be the first player to collect all 6 eggnogs in your eggnog tray and retrieve the prize within the market shop (tin house).

Each player receives 18 game board pieces to build their path to the eggnog. It is up to each player to arrange the tiles in whatever order they would like. Each tile has a gold or silver 
back. Flip a gold tile over to take (1) eggnog from any other player when landed on. Flip a silver tile over for a lose one-turn event when landed on. Players can have any number of 
these gold or silver game pieces on the game board to vary the player experience. The more colored pieces played the more action during the game. The stealing of eggnog from other players gives the game more drama and fun.

Each player gets a market shop (tin house) to place at the end of the game pieces. The market shopis to be placed near the last tile leading to the eggnog stash. Place one Santa hat into each market shop. Players can also have someone place candy or even money in 
one or more shops during the pregame setup, making sure no one sees which shop has the extra prizes. This leads to a fun surprise bonus if the winning player also has extra treats in their market shop.
Players move via a dice-rolling mechanic, while play is influenced by pre-play tile placement and tile-specific actions. Players also may take opportunities to steal other players' token during play.

In Pop Goes Froggio a friend of the frog is selected by spinning a butterfly unit. After this all players at once try to find this friend by flipping over lily pads which are scattered around the room. The player who succeeds may launch the frog off his lily pad and try to catch it.

boardgame: Xenoid: The Arcade-Style Solo Space-Shooter Card Game
description:
A new menace is threatening Earth and its inhabitants. Earthbound observers detected a huge armada of exotic, bionic spaceships, the Xenoids, headed towards our home planet. You are a veteran pilot piloting a heavily armed prototype fighter spacecraft, the "Exterminator". Equipped with state of the art laser and shielding technology, your piloting skills and the "Exterminator" are the only way to stop the armada of invaders and save humankind from certain damnation. Will you be able to stop the Xenoid intruders?

This card game for one player tries to emulate the gameplay of classic arcade-style top-down space shooters. You control a spaceship that starts with 3 Lives, 3 Shields and a Weapon with Power 1. You are facing an armada of enemy (Xenoid) Spacecraft which move downwards towards you while you try to destroy as many of them as possible by performing one out of 3 actions each turn: Move - Shoot - Charge (your Weapon).

The Game features 5 Waves (Levels) with enemies becoming stronger and more numerous as you advance through the stages. 
You collect Power Ups (such as Weapon or Shield Upgrades or additional Lives etc) which pop up at random when enemy crafts are destroyed. In between stages you can buy Power Ups by spending points you acccumulated during the Waves. Each destroyed enemy goes to the Score Pile and may be used to pay for Power Ups in between Waves.

The goal of the game is twofold: The primary goal is to survive all five Waves. When all your shields are depleted you lose one life, and when all lives are lost it means "Game Over". The secondary goal is to strive for the highest possible score by destroying as many Xenoid ships as possible. Again you score points for every destroyed enemy spaceship. So in your first game you will set your initial high score which you can beat in consecutive games. Note that you even set a score if you don't beat the game...

Objective: be the first catching all enemies or capture their headquarter
- moving by turns -
2 players (or groups) - red Indians & (blue) cowboys) try to catch each other by rolling the die; 
remarks:
published during late 1950's/early 60's 
similar to - compared with Tom Mix in Texas

In this game layers roll the two 12-sided target 
dice and calculate the target number by using the blue die as the base and the red die as the exponent. Then roll the three 6-sided scoring dice and combine their numbers using mathematical operations to match, or come closest to, the target Number.

Objective: block your opponent's balls
 - acting by turns -
each player moves two coloured balls each to block other player's movement; the quicker the game proceeds the higher the possibility to stumble..
remarks:
published ~1920/30's

boardgame: Hull Breach: In Defiance of Dictators
description:
The Basics:

Hull Breach is an award-winning Card Game from Not-So-Broken (NSB) Games. First released in 2011 as an 'Alpha Run'; then Re-released in it's final version in 2014; as Corporate Wars and Loyalty & Vigilance. Now, we are moving to complete the set with In Defiance of Dictators.

This space-based Card and Dice tabletop game which features epic combat between warships, firefights during Marine boarding actions, and a multitude of clever tactics and galaxy-shaking events, Hull Breach is one of the fastest-growing platforms in the gaming world.

In Hull Breach, you assume command of an advanced space station belonging to one of our several in-game factions and fight it out with one or more other Commanders to achieve galactic domination.

The object of the game is to protect your Station - your home in space - from other Commanders while trying to capture or destroy theirs. Be warned - they’re trying to do the same thing to you. It's a tough galaxy and at the end of the day only one Commander will remain standing. Only through careful planning can the most daring gain command of the galaxy.

Each deck of Hull Breach cards consists of fifty-five (55) cards, including a Station, two Station Module enhancements, two Stockpile cards to track your resources and economic wealth, with fifty others to help extend your power across the galaxy. Each pre-constructed deck sports an effective array of the different card types you’ll need to wage war against your opponents: Upgrades, Options, Modules, Marines, Ships, Events, Tactics, and Breakthroughs. Once you have a few games under your belt you can mix and match cards from different decks to create your very own personalized fighting force and compete against some of the finest commanders in the galaxy.

Best of all, while we encourage customization and deck-building, this isn't one of those "collectible" card games that will have you tearing through randomized foil packs of cards to find the ones you want. Hull Breach plays right out of the box and you'll know where you can find every card in the game without having to drop a ton of cash on additional boosters or turn to the secondary market to field a competitive deck.

Also, Hull Breach is not only a game, but a developing universe where the players have the opportunity to affect the outcome of the fighting between the factions. In every box you receive a fiction supplement to help you immerse yourself in the Universe’s rich backstory, and we are currently in negotiations to expand upon that literary presence. In fact, several of the Reward levels below will allow you to create your own historical presence in the Hull Breach universe.

I'm a returning Commander (player); so what can you tell us about the new Hull Breach Expansion: In Defiance of Dictators?

Hull Breach!: "In Defiance of Dictators" focuses on the rising conflict between the Republic Defense Force and the Colonial Unaligned Forces in the wake of the Corporate Wars. Once again, you take control of one of the faction-controlled stations, build up your fleet and forces, and do battle with any who would attempt to encroach upon your space. Each box set includes four (4) pre-constructed decks (ready to play directly out of the box) complete with all new stations and cards that pit 2 RDF Commanders against 2 Colonial Commanders. Are you ready to fight off the imperialistic monsters, or crush the rebel scum? You decide, you command!

You asked and we listened! Over the course of the last two expansions, we have consistently continued to listen to our players and fan base, in order to continue to expand and improved our Hull Breach Universe. In Defiance of Dictators will include:

- New Team Play and Team Victory Rules

- alternate Victory Conditions; Diplomatic and Economic Victories

- even more Faction-Only cards and effects

- 65 never-before-seen printed cards and artwork, and many necessary revisions to our First-Edition cards

- two new Ship classes; the Freighter and the Transport; in inspire trade between players and aide in the valiant Marine-heavy decks

- a revised Rulebook, complete with easier access rules for beginners; and a digital video rule book put together by the awesome team at 'Skip the Rules'

- a completely all new Fictional Supplement that focuses on the Republic Defense Forces and Colonial Unaligned Forces

- everything you need to enjoy our new Organized Play system

if you have any other questions feel free to email us direct at hullbreachthegame@gmail.com
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
November, 20 1943
Gilbert Islands, Tarawa Atoll

After hours of intense naval bombardment, the Marines of the US Second Division sat some 500 yards from shore, their landing craft impeded by the shallow reef. As the artillery ceased, it was time to take to the beach in what would be the deadliest 76 hours in the entire Pacific Theater of World War II.

Leathernecks ‘43 is a fast-paced dice game which mixes push-your-luck elements with important tactical decisions.  You control a squad of Marines advancing up the beach towards enemy machine gun positions.  Rolling the soldiers on your dice allows them to advance, but you might also roll machine gun fire which slows them down.  Each suppressing fire result pins down one of your soldier dice and if you accumulate three suppressing fires in a single turn, your entire squad is pinned down and none of your soldiers advance!  If you’re lucky, you can use smoke grenades to avoid the fire and push your squad up the beach.  Your goal is to assault the bunker, but you also need to outpace your opponent’s soldiers!  At the end of the game, you score bonus points if you moved your different soldiers farther than your opponents, so you need to pay attention to what they are doing!

—description from the designer

boardgame: Survive: Escape from Atlantis! – Dolphins & Dive Dice Mini Expansion
description:
The Sea Creatures around the sinking island of Atlantis have always been dangerous… but now these behemoths can move faster and dive across the seas to attack Swimmers and Boats!

However, friendly Dolphins now can be found in these waters as well!  The Dolphins are there to protect your Swimmers, but will they be enough to keep you safe as you try to Survive!

“Survive: Dolphins and Dive Dice“ mini-expansion is compatible with “Survive: The Giant Squid” and “Survive: 5-6 Player” mini-expansions, each sold separately.

NOTE: The red Creature Die is not used with this mini-expansion, but rather is a small gift to those who preferred the engraved red Die from the original blue box edition. Enjoy!

NOTE:  You must have the base game Survive: Escape from Atlantis! to enjoy this expansion.

Mages in the Marketplace is a highly interactive deck-building game where each player takes on the role of one of four mages. Each mage may also select one of four familiars, minor elementals that grow in power as they are fed magic. These mages are vying to become the richest and most famous, each for their own individual reasons.

The twist: Players take their turns simultaneously, and when it is time to decide which card they would like to purchase from the center, all mages hide their selection, revealing them together. Any player who is alone purchases their card without worry, but any time multiple players wish to purchase the same card, a duel takes place between their familiars. The crowd cheers for the winner, who then purchases that card, whereas the losing familiar becomes hurt and its master must find a new card to purchase.

The mages include:
The Magician: a master of showmanship. He uses impressive magic to generate extra Fame.
The Seer: a mage with the ability to see into the future. She can influence what order she draws her spells in.
The Sorcerer: a chaotic mage with little control, and less desire for it. His unpredictable nature makes him difficult to defeat in battle.
The Wizard: an old academic with a deep understanding of magic itself. His mana generation is second to none.

The familiars come in four basic flavours, Air, Earth, Fire, and Water, and as one feeds their Elemental they may blend them together into more complicated beasts - by combining Fire and Water, for example, one may create a Steam Elemental. All combinations are represented, including the all-powerful Omni Elemental!

Note: "Mages in the Marketplace" is a working title.

American Trivia Family Edition features mostly knowledge-based content rather than trivial tidbits. Family Edition cards have two sides - one with easier questions (Junior side) and the other with more difficult questions (Expert side), allowing families of all ages to play together.

The goal is to answer the most questions correctly to advance along the map of the United States and return back to the Start.

The fun filled trivia board game created in the spirit of baseball.  It has everything a trivia board game should, including questions covering not only baseball but a wide range of general knowledge.  Just like Americas favorite sport, you win or lose Baseball Fever with runs, hits, balls and strike outs.  All it takes is strategy, skill and a little bit of luck.

Warfare is a fast paced, constantly changing, strategic contest for immortal glory, all taking place in your living room.Meet your friends on the field of battle and see who has the mettle to lead their army to victory!

Warfare is played with 2-4 players facing off with four unique Units on a 13x13 grid. Players can use a turn to move, redeploy, attack, or attempt to capture a target.

the players battle each other by rolling their individual dice. whenever a player wins a battle they receive a Victory Bonus card which gives them buffs that can affect the attack, defend or movement number they roll,or can be special actions like "Nuclear Strike" or "Deploy 2 Units". the losing player must remove their unit from play and discard it's Unit Type card and any assigned victory bonus cards.They can always redeploy their unit and get a new Unit Type card, this way the Unit's change constantly. having more than one unit in attack range of an enemy or a target adds to a attack, defend or capture roll.

once the players have collected 2 victory bonuses on one Unit they can attempt to capture one of the corner targets. the corner targets have different defense Numbers and the players have to roll to beat them. if a player rolls less than the defense number they forfeit a Victory bonus.

once all four of the corner Targets are captured. the players race to capture the center "Primary Target" the first player to capture the Primary Target Wins!

From the box:

"Not all of the Dark Consul’s minions slash and bite. Some, like the succubi, seek to corrupt and beguile. To many they are the most dangerous monsters of all and Vandella is their champion. This unique Mini-Boss will sow confusion and disarray among the Heroes invading your dungeon and add a little pleasure to their pain.

Fully Assembled Succubus Vandella Miniature
Succubus Vandella Mini-Boss Game Card
Unique Temptress Perfume Treasure Card"

boardgame: Kipling: The Seemingly Saying Something Game
description:
Kipling (the Seemingly Saying Something Game) is a card/token voting game exploring themes of complex communication – saying one thing, but meaning another – for 2 to 8 players, ages 11 and over. The game is based Kipling’s six questions, in our case ordered as Why, What, How, for the intent and (in no particular order) Where, When and Who for the content.

On each turn, one player is the Speaker, who is randomly dealt a Why, a What and a How card, which can be Good, Neutral or Bad. From these, the Speaker makes up a sentence and says it to the player on their left, the Spoken To, in the style required by the cards without showing the cards to the other players.

Each other player then votes on each of the three categories, Why/What/How and chooses Good/Neutral/Bad, in clockwise order starting with the Spoken To player. During and before this phase the Speaker and Spoken To can play Jokers to double or redouble the score for that round if they choose to. Once votes are in, the Speaker reveals the cards they were dealt, and the scores are calculated; one point for each correct guess, with the potential for an extra point for the Spoken To each turn, because they will have an extra token for voting.

If nobody or everybody finds the correct combination of three intent cards, the storyteller scores 0, and each of the other players scores 1 point for each individual part they got correct. Otherwise the storyteller shares the same score as whoever found the complete answer; usually a score 3 or 4. Jokers might double this to up to 4 or 8 depending on cards in play and who was the correct player.

The game ends when a player scores 16 or more points, but this threshold should be increased for larger numbers of players. In either case, the player with the most points wins the game. If there is a draw, the player who spoke most is the winner.

boardgame: Squirrels Fun House: Lost and Drunk
description:
Squirrel goes out for a few drinks with his friends and ends up getting smashed. When he goes to walk home, he finds himself in a hallucinated alcoholic wonderland!  
     The objective in this game is to be the first one to get Squirrel home. Sounds easy enough, but the twisted trail loaded with booby traps and treasures, along with the drinking challenge, creates a great board game for all drinking game fans.
     First pick a starter. The game comes with some creative ideas for this. Then everybody rolls one dice and moves along the trail accordingly. The trail is filled with short cuts filled with Jail spots. When a player lands in Jail, they must finish their drink before returning to gameplay. On the main yellow trail, players are likely to land on the Golden Acorns. Landing here means the player gets bonus turns by guessing high, low, odd, or even, based on their previous roll, and they keep going untill they guess wrong. Simply follow the yellow trail and everybody will make it to the finish, but the twisted trail makes this an interesting tricky challenge. Luckily the game comes with a map that will show you the way. The game also comes with sober cab rules so everybody can play. If you are a power hour fan, you will definately like the drinking challenge built into this game. The challenge is that every time you land on a "D" Spot, you must take the number of drinks that you rolled to get there. Over all, This is a unique and simple drinking board game that anyone over 21 can easily learn, and start the challenge of being the first player to get Squirrel home alive.

Monopoly set of Duisburg, a German city in the mid-west region.
All the traditional elements of rolling, moving, purchasing, earning, and spending.

Oblasta is a simple, fast playing, two player abstract stategy game. 
  Players take turns rolling two sided playing pieces, and using them to perform actions on the board. Each side allows the player to perform differnt actions on. But, the player is limited to eight pieces, and the pieces used to roll, are also the pieces used as units on the gameboard. So players are forced to try and strike a balance between units in their hand powering units on the board, and units on the board actually fighting the conflict.
  A player wins by either, eliminating all the opponents offensive units, or by postioning his own offensive units in an unbreakable defensive postion.

In this game players are entrepreneurs who produce, transport and sell goods to 6 European cities.

The game introduces the full business cycle where primary resources need to be bought, transformed into secondary resources (materials) and then into final goods ready to be sold to consumers. Factories are built, upgraded and close down, workers are hired and replaced by machines as players try to maximize their profits observing the environment and the competitors.

This whole process is embodied into a dynamic economic environment. Cities develop as population migrates, new industries are built, closed or relocated, wages are increased and resources/materials prices fluctuate. Development brings higher demand, which players try to control through advertising.
Players also need to manage the transportation circuit and either invest now for future payoffs (opponents pay more to the owner of a route and the latter earns money when population migrates through his network) or try to handle the long-term costs. 
Contracts which will be available each round, will inject players with critical cash (as there are no loans) but ending unfulfilled they will incur penalties.

For 2 players (and the solo) there is also an automated player who exploits opportunities and antagonizes the players without being complicated.

The game ends after the round when either all spaces have been built or one player has built all his factories (for the one and two players additional conditions for the automated player may also end the game).
At the end, each player's efficiency will be calculated as a percentage between 0 and 100, taking into account a number of parameters such as cash, sales, production lines, etc.A hidden card, dealt randomly to each player from the beginning, will solve ties.

The official GI Joe game of Action - Strategy - Offense - Defense.

The object of this game is to be the first player to score 15 points.

The game board Attack and Defense Area is divided into 91 grid squares.  Each grid square is divided into 4 squares on which the soldiers move.  The soldiers can move forward, backward, sideways, and diagonally.  In the center of the Game Board, there are 3 bridges.  The Attacking soldiers can move across the Game Board only by crossing the bridges.

Build - Destroy - Scheme

Build the blueprints for Space Colony Hex: The Space Station of the future! Construct a wonderful new Space Colony with your fellow Engineers. But be careful, some of them are not who they claim to be. Prevent the Sneaky Con Artists from running away with all the money before the glorious Space Colony Hex is finished and stop the money-hungry Government Agents from bloating up the blueprints and making our sleek new Space Colony break the bank. Start scheming for Space Colony Hex's glorious completion or utter demise!

Space Colony Hex is a quick, tile-placement game with hidden teams. Players add tiles to the blueprint, bump other tiles off, and steal tiles from one another to achieve their hidden, sometimes shared, objective. The unique objectives will lead players to create makeshift alliances one minute then turn on each other the next. The ever changing blueprints will keep players on their toes looking for an opening to make the perfect move.

'Look and Learn' (English name) is designed to stimulate a child's ability to "discriminate between objects, to compare them, to grasp relationships between them, and to sort according to common characteristics." (From the box bottom.)

Up to seven different games are possible using the multi-picture boards and picture tiles included. Three of them are described here:

1. Picture lotto
Each player gets a player board. Boards that aren't needed and the corresponding tiles are removed from the game. The remaining tiles are shuffled and placed on the table, colour side up. The game master picks up one tile after the other, shows the picture and names the pictured object. The player whose board contains the same picture calls out, gets the tile and places it on his/her board. The first player to have covered all the images on his/her board wins the game.

2. Picture quiz
The game master shows a picture without naming it. The player who has the picture on his/her board calls out, but they only get the tile if they can name the object. The opposite way is possible too: The game master doesn't show the picture but names the object shown on the tile. The player who finds the picture on his/her board gets the tile. The first player to have covered all the images on his/her board wins the game.

3. Memory lotto
Each player gets a board. He/she gets 30 seconds to memorise the images. Then the player boards are placed on the table, face down. The game master picks up one tile after the other and shows the image or names the object. The player who thinks the image is on his/her own player board calls out. The tile is placed face up next to the player board. If two players call out simultaneously, the game master puts the tile at the bottom of his/her stack. If an image isn't claimed by any of the players, the tile is removed from the game. When the game master has used all the tiles, the players reveal their boards. For each matching tile, they get two points; for each incorrect or missing one, one point is subtracted. The player with the most points wins the game.

More game variations are possible using the different colors on the backs of the picture tiles and multi-picture boards.

The box indicates that it was "Made in Western-Germany".

The object of the game is to become the wealthiest player by the buying and licensing of franchises. Genuine franchise companies are used in the game, such as Shakey's Pizza, Robo Car Wash, Wiener King, Lawn Doctor, Natural Cosmetics, and several others.

Each player starts with $110,000. Players roll dice and move their pawns around the board, and may purchase the franchise system depicted on the space they land on, if it is currently unowned. If another player lands on the same space, he must become a franchisee, and pays the system owner a royalty fee whenever he again lands on that space. Only two franchises of any type may be sold, so anyone else landing on that space becomes a customer and must pay a fee based on the number of franchises owned. Players may freely buy and trade franchises between themselves.

If a player cannot pay the required fee(s), he is declared bankrupt and is out of the game.

"Clinical Anatomy is an incredible study aid that plays like a game! Memorize and learn the name, location and function of more than 600 muscles, 206 bones, nearly 60 organs, 150 named nerves and 150 named blood vessels, and dozens of joints in the human body." (From the website.)

Gameplay is nearly identical to Trivial Pursuit. Players start with their token on any spot on the board. Dice are rolled to move the tokens around the board, and the players answer questions based on the subject their token lands on, such as "GA101" (General Anatomy 101), "GA102", "Cadaver Lab", and "Potluck". Correct answers garner the player an individual letter to add to their collection. The first player to collect enough letters to spell "clinical anatomy" is the winner.

A monopoly (from Greek monos μόνος (alone or single) + polein πωλεῖν (to sell)) exists when a specific person or enterprise is the only supplier of a particular commodity.

Monopoly is a roll-and-move game where players move around the game board buying or trading properties, developing their properties with houses and hotels. When opponents land on players owned property, the owning player collects rent from the landing player. Owning sets of properties increases the rent paid to the player who owns them, by the player who lands on the square. The ultimate goal is to have the most money and drive opponents into bankruptcy.

The classic Monopoly with constructions and other elements of Offenburg, a city located in Germany.
The classic Monopoly with constructions and other elements of Föhr, a North Frisian Island in Germany.

This designer has always been a little quirky (he is a friend so he won't mind me saying it) and he deveoped his various skills at making a statistical game that was unusual, this game.

As usual with these games, there are numerous charts and tables enabling you to replay Pro Volleyball matches. I believe the pro league did really exist, in Canada.

The designer adapted his tried and trusted his soccer game rules and researched statistics for every world cup team from 1930 to 1983.

As usual with statistical games, you throw dice and check the roll or rolls against various charts and tables. It is fairly quick to play and allows a complete World Cup competition to be replayed in an evening.

The designer is most noted for his statistical games that create games for sports not usually depicted in such games. Britain hardly ever developed stats games up to 1980 or so, and Gary filled the gap in the market by making games for Cricket and Soccer. However he must have felt that he had to make at least one American staple and so he published this.

As usual with statistical games, all you need are two dice and all the charts and tables provided within the game. Thus you can try to replay a whole season (if you fancy playing over 1,000 games!)

This is definitely one of those games where the name directed the mechanic. The concept of karma – good and bad, owing or being owed a debt, and both being a responsibility – and a meta game element of card destruction (making every deck unique) and Real World ™ consequences (in the form of a verbal contract to play) sort of came after the pun of Karma and Armageddon.

The most important part of this game concept is that players may “help” you, even when you don’t want it, and that this will exact a real consequence to clear the debt. Of course, like a gambling debt, this isn’t enforceable. However, entering into the spirit of the thing is the whole point. Why cheat? You’re only robbing yourself…

Set up
There are two elements to this game:

1) A card game played with (what starts out at least as) a standard deck of cards. If there are Jokers, bridge rules or instruction cards, include them.

2) Redemption of Debt where cards used to help other players are IOUs for an hour of a player’s time on any reasonable, legal and responsible task.

To play (the first time) a Sharpie or permanent marker and a standard deck of cards (with jokers) is necessary for 2-4 players. For more than 4 players an extra deck of cards is added for each additional player up to 8 maximum; ideally this should have a different back design or be different size to allow separation for future 2-4 player games. These cards will be torn, ripped and marked, so will likely not be useful for other games afterwards.

NOTE: a deck where cards are missing (otherwise useless!) is fine as a starting deck. Subsequent play will likely not have all the cards anyway, so one or two missing now won’t matter. Reuse/Recycling!!! I feel better already!

Set up for the Meta-Game (the redemption of favours) is merely the agreement of all players that they will commit to an hour of time helping one another for each “Debt Card” they end up with. Where this occurs in game, the card will be ripped in two and both parts signed by helper and helpee; its recommended that a phone number or email address for each is also written down, unless players are well acquainted. The helpee then uses their half in play (for that game only) and later as a reminder that they owe a favour. The helper keeps their half to call in the debt.

NOTE: The hour of time and the task are to be mutually agreed between helper and helpee. It could be anything crom mowing a lawn, babysitting, fixing a computer, whatever is acceptable and doable.

NOTE 2: Debts do NOT cancel out. If Mike owes Paco a debt and vice versa, they don’t call it quits. They both do an hour for the other. This is a matter of honour!

Once a debt is paid, “Done” is written on the helpers half and “Paid” is written on the helpees half. Cards can be kept as a memento, burned ceremoniously or binned.

The challenge was to create a game that uses all the worst bits of many of the bad games that Flip the Table regularly disparage, including roll-and-play ("Surprise!") and press-your-luck games, but especially Monopoly because @tableflipsyou actually likes Monopoly!

The game essentially consists of players bidding for turn order, with payments paid to subsequent players, with the added complication of the need for money to be exchanged due to restricted access to various denominations. This is followed by choice of a variety of dice movement strategies, in order to arrive back at GO! with exactly $200 (no more, no less).

During movement, if two players arrive at the same square a variant of SNAP (The BANCE! action) allows for players to steal one another's money. NOTE: The game is deliberately complex and there is a strong possibility that it will last for a long time, depending on whether players have realised that it is intended to be torturous.

boardgame: Eagle Alight: The Lunar Lander Board Game
description:
In honour of the FAVOURITE Computer Game (Arcade), Lunar Lander, and the 40th anniversary of the (faked?) Apollo 11 Moon Landings, here is "Eagle Alight", a Lunar Lander inspired board game.

Use fuel, Fuel, FUEL to rotate and slow your craft for an “Eagle Has Landed” moment! This is effectively a simulation of gravity on two hex maps: One with a small playing piece for the larger wide scale view of the moon surface; the other representing the various landing sites by use of layered colour hexes, with a larger playing piece to represent the ship in close-up. In the interest of best representing the original game, the two boards need to be double sided, with one side giving the large scale where the two boards are placed side by side (landscape).
Brazilian version of Monopoly with Rio de Janeiro's famous neighborhoods and natural features wiyh the Olympic Games theme.

Objective:
be the first getting 7 'Wissens-Bons'
 - acting by turns -
Each player moves along a travel-route all over the world. Stepping on a 'star' space you must give correct answer (sometimes multiple choice) to receive one 'Bon'.

Intro: A set of 32 GamePatra that are based on characters of forthcoming online game - 'War of Fascinating Galaxies' (WarOFGa). These trading cards make learning and memorising Physics concepts fun and part of a game.

Aim: The game can be played in many way and the aim is to gather maximum number of cards for your collection.

Game Play: Each player brings a pack or all equally divide a pack
Both players reads out the number on the card at the top
The payer with the lesser numbered card can ask for the title of the other player’s card. If she/he can recall the contents of the card, then she/he does not have to surrender the card

Sharrash is a mysterious and well-organized community of mutant rats endowed with inhuman intelligence. They live underground, in tunnels and canals of the destroyed cities, along with degenerate humans and mutants who serve the Sharrash. They do not allow strangers anywhere near them and fiercely defend their nests, and their fighters will not hesitate to give their life for the pack.

The main advantages of the Neuroshima Hex! Sharrash army are mobility of HQ, the ability to blow up even the fastest enemy units and to paralyze the slower ones, and the possibility of bypassing the enemy's defense lines thanks to the mortars. Another advantage of the Sharrash is a difficult to eliminate Foundation Tile (Hole), which can easily thwart the enemy's plans.

Sharrash's disadvantage is a smaller number of fighting units than in other armies, their low mobility, low toughness and initiative, as well as a complete lack of armors.
family: Versions & Editions: Board Game Versions of Role-Playing Games
