boardgame: Race to Adventure! Expansion Pack: Dinocalypse Now and the Hollow Earth
description:
Race to Adventure! expands with this double-mini-expansion pack!

In the Dinocalypse Now mini-expansion, you'll dodge psychic dinosaurs as they burst through their time-portal ... and travel to the Century Club's most secret base on the island of Kauai. Can you protect your clues and win the Race all while on the run from a reptilian menace?

In the Hollow Earth mini-expansion, you'll Race into subterranean depths by sinkhole, underground river, and cave entrance. Will you be the one to uncover the lost treasure of the Neanderthals?

boardgame: Star Fleet Marines: Module II – Last Stand
description:
The next STAR FLEET MARINES module is LAST STAND, portraying desperate battles by isolated units on faraway planets, fighting without hope that rescue can arrive in time.

New scenarios include: Last Stand on Fang Hill, the Siege of Nucklow, the Siege of Phen Dien Du, Dragons’ Den, Tank Battle in the Zasu Pits, and more.

New units include: outposts, heavy transport shuttles (able to land tanks and other armored vehicles on enemy planets), ground weapon vehicles, and ground bombardment shuttles.

New rules include external passengers and new terrain (depressions, cliffs, mountains).

New counters include: Hydran light infantry and heavy armor, Romulan tanks, and more.

24 pages, 280 die-cut counters
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
The game is situated in the southern polar ice cap, on an ice float which the heat of the sun is melting. Baby penguins traveling on the ice are trying to reach their mother before their way is cut off.

The game board depicts an ice float constructed of many ice tiles laid face down. The players move their penguins one at a time, trying to bring their baby penguins A) next to their own mother or B) next to an opponents mother. Every turn starts with the active player flipping and removing an ice tile from the board, possibly revealing a special action for that turn, and consequently making the ice float smaller.

The tile special actions are:
Scorching Sun: Flip and remove an extra tile
Belly Slide: Extra move
Empty: Nothing happens
Ice float moves: You can move an ice tile with a penguin on it.
Whale: Move an opponents penguin anywhere you like on the board (it gets scared).

The game ends when the last free ice tile is flipped, and the scores are calculated. You get positive points for every penguin of your own colour next to your mother penguin, but also negative points for every penguin of an opponents colour next to your mother penguin.

Game that appeared in the 1992 children's annual, The Desperate Dan Book. The rules:

Howdy, folks.
It’s the Cactusville Cattle Market and the local ranch owners are in town to buy up cattle. The biggest buy is a herd of buffalo which has been split amongst four neighbouring farmers. You have each been hired by one of the farmers, to get his share of the herd home first.
So let’s get them steers a’rollin’!

This is a game for two to four players.
You will need a dice and coloured counters (coloured pieces of card will do just as well). Each player must start on their own coloured circle in the middle, the highest roll of the dice deciding who goes first. The players must follow their coloured arrow and go round the board in an anti-clockwise direction. If they land on a square with instructions on it they must follow these instructions. The winner is the first to get his or her coloured counter to the ranch of the same colour.

(A longer version of the game can be , played where each player has an equal amount of counters. When each throws, they can then decide which counter to move. The winner here shall be the one to get all their counters home!)

In this educative game, the objective is to conquer the most continents on the game board by using your collection of dinosaur tiles. The dinosaurs compete using five characteristics: weight, size, agility, ferocity and the period of their appearance on Earth. This game is not only a trivia about 120 dinosaurs, but players also need to demonstrate a keen tactical sense.

boardgame: Heavy Gear Blitz! Forged in Fire; Southern Field Guide
description:
Forged in Fire; Southern Field Guide contains the complete army list system for the Southern Republic Army, MILICIA, Humanist Alliance Protection Force, Mekong Peacekeepers, and Eastern Sun Emirate Retinues as well as rules for fielding Mekong Mercenary Guild and AST Protectorate forces.

Forged in Fire continues the story of the War for Terra Nova and updates the timeline for the South to TN 1951.

- Heavy Gear Blitz! Army List construction rules and shared cadres for the SRA, MILICIA, HAPF, ESEG, and MDP.
- Faction specific cadres and model swap lists.
- Rules for fielding Mercenary Guild and AST Protectorate forces.
- Detailed backgrounds on the Southern Leagues and their military forces.
- Southern Vehicle Compendium
- Details of the battle for Port Oasis.
- Miniature Gallery
- Datacards

This full color 176 page book is a Field Guide for use with a copy of the most current Heavy Gear Blitz! rulebook. Dream Pod 9 miniatures, six-sided dice, and a measuring tape are also required for play.

For two or more players, ages 8 and up.

Soft Cover, ISBN: 978-1-927334-59-1

Draughts for 3 players, as recorded in the files of the British Museum in London.

Each player has 10 pieces. Each player starts from one of the flat sides of the triangular board.

Each player can capture pieces from both of his two opponents and this is carried out as in normal Draughts (the rules do not say that jumping can only be in one direction, i.e. you cannot jump a piece by doing a move rather like a knight in Chess). Each piece can be moved in three directions, straight ahead, or to the right of 'straight ahead' or to the left of 'straight ahead'. Crowned pieces can be moved in any direction.

Pieces can be 'crowned' by reaching the back starting row of his opponents. This is precarious because at either end of your side of the board, your opponents' back row is only five hexes away from each other.

The game is over if there is only one player left or if two players are so hemmed in that they cannot move any more.

In WordSmith, players continuously smith together cards to build words and score points. The game will challenge your literary skills and test your reflexes. Smith off against your friends and family to see who’s the best WordSmith.

All WordSmith cards contain different 2-lettered combinations. Players use a WordSmith Card from their hand with a WordSmith Card from the center play area (Workshop) to build words. Once a word is smithed, that player replaces the Workshop card with the WordSmith card used from their hand and keeps the Workshop card for points. A new WordSmith card is drawn to replenish the WordSmith card used from that player's hand.

boardgame: Justice League: Face Off Dice Game
description:
Game description from the publisher:

Play hero or villain in Justice League: Face Off Dice Game, a push-your-luck dice game of bluffing and battling. Roll the special hero/villain dice in the sculpted dice cup that features both Batman and The Joker. Push your luck as you bluff your opponents with villains or keep rolling to try for more hero points.

Easy Pack expands Legend - The History of 1000 Miglia race game.

Thanks to the Easy Pack drivers can now play Legend without writing or calculate their racing time!

You can use your cockpit board to keep track of time, damage and wear and set speed on your Car Performance Card (provided with  basic game).

Easy Pack is also a valid support to introduce new gamers to Legend

In the set-making card game Disgrace & Favours you play the role of a socialite who is trying to gather influential people to your cause by "persuading" them to promote you. You do this by sending your servant to snoop on them, find out an embarrassing secret, then ask them nicely to help you out...or else.

Each card in the game appears twice, and if a player has her secret revealed by an opponent she becomes disgraced and the other identical card is forfeit, even if it is in a set.

Disgrace & Favours includes an element of luck as players have to try to guess what each other are up to during the snooping phase. Set disruption is also part of the game, and ruining someone's plans will bring you a large chunk of satisfaction. Naturally you can try to defend against this by getting hold of both cards or taking the disgrace token for yourself.

Two sets of 12 stones each are placed in a 5x5 square grid. The first two rows in front of a player, plus the two rightmost pits on the third row contain that player's stones. On his turn, a player must displace one of his stones, performing one of the following legal moves: 1) a stone can be moved orthogonally to an adjacent free field, 2) a stone may jump in a straight line over at least one same-coloured stone, and onto the next free field, 3)a stone can jump over at least one same-coloured stone and into a pit occupied by an opponent's stone which is then captured and removed from the game.

The winner is the first player to capture 10 stones from his opponent or to leave the opponent without legal moves. If both players have only four stones each in play, the game ends in a draw.

The 'Trio Trio' Clemens Gerhards' game set (2010 edition) includes rules and components to play this game, along with two additional Frank Stark's games (Trio Trio and Soli-Bi).

Simple print and play two player battle card game. 
Each attack in Hitta consists of an initiation followed by a decreasing sequence of cards (each card being of a lower value than the preceding card) ending with a dice roll to finish the attack which needs to be higher than the lowest (last) card played in the sequence. The points removed from your opponent are equal to the number of cards in your sequence.
The defender will get several opportunities to block the attack by trying to guess the next card the attacker will use or to try to match one of the dice with one of their own cards.

The game is played with a deck of Zombie Rider Playing cards. All 56 cards including special gaffs and jokers are used.

1. The game starts as each player receives one of the random queen cards.

2. After the queens are dealt each player is dealt 5 cards. The queens are held in the hand so the other players don't know which queen the other players have.

3. The player at the dealers left begins. They lay a card down to start a trick, each player must play a card from that trick if they have it. If they don't they must draw a card then play any card. They player with the highest card with the same suite that was started wins the trick, unless a gaff or joker is played. The player that plays a gaff or Joker gets to take the trick.

4. Roll off: If multiple gaffs or jokers are played the players must enter into a best of 3 roll-off with Zombie Rider dice. If there are more than 2 jokers or gaff cards played on one hand. The players roll one die each, the player with the highest roll gets a bye in the first round as the other players roll against each other. The highest total best of 3 wins the roll off. If any player in a roll off rolls double ZR (the engraved picture on the 1 spot) they win the roll off immediately.

5. The player who won the last trick begins the next trick, the game ends when someone runs out of cards.

Scoring: 
3 points for every trick won that matches the same suite as your queen.
2 points for every trick won that includes a gaff or joker.
1 point for every trick won that does not match the suite of your queen.
1 point for every roll off victory.
-1 point for every card left in your hand.
The game is played in sets, and points are recorded after each set. The first person to earn 25 Points is most decrepit queen.

Based on the popular steampunk-themed minigame, Frontier Skies brings players on a tabletop journey through the unique world of Gaia Online. Players will compete to build the finest airship while managing their crew, stockpiling supplies and maintaining their valuable discoveries. In the end, only the most cunning captain can rule the frontier skies as the richest adventurer around!

In more detail, Frontier Skies consists of four seasons, spanning a total of twelve rounds of play. Each round consists of four phases: market, adventure, trade and draw. During the market phase, players compete in an open market to purchase new and upgraded parts for their airship. Purchasing new ship parts improves speed, attack power and cargo capacity, giving players special bonuses and extra space to carry valuable discoveries.

In the adventure phase, players embark on a new journey by revealing the top card in the adventure deck. The trade phase allows players to trade discoveries with each other, deciding which are worth keeping and which should be sold off to gain gold and free up cargo capacity for their next adventure. During the draw phase, players activate special abilities, gain an additional discovery, and compete for end-of-season and end-of-game goals which reward players with even more gold.

At the end of the final round, each player calculates their total value to find out which captain has amassed the greatest riches!

description:
In this game, the player must survive long enough to collect 8 pages about the Slender, a horror that stalks, either killing you, or driving you insane to the point where you commit suicide. 
To play, you move, and then roll to see if Slender appears. If you make it to the next location, you can check to see if a page is there.

The more pages you collect, the more likely the Slender will appear, it will become harder to get away from it.
You will have to be quick, as your flash light will slowly run out. Once it is dead, it is nearly impossible to win.
Even if you collect all 8 pages, you are still killed, but you can have a sense of accomplishment.

Based on the Slender: The 8 pages computer game.

boardgame: Land of the Lost Board Game
description:
from the back of the box

The Prehistoric Land of the Lost - a dry and hot desert, ferocious dinosaurs roaming a steamy jungle, and a steep, rocky precipice, these are just some of the dangers that await you! Join the Porter family and their friends in their quest for the elusive time portal and escape from this primitive land. Escape from Scarface, the ferocious Tyrannosaurus Rex. He will chase you around every path - look out because he's hungry and is coming your way! Also avoid Shung, the Sleestak leader, as he attempts to trap you in his lair! Be the first player to collect three power crystal cards and make your way through the prehistoric pitfalls to the time portal, and then spin to win!

It is notable that this appears to be based on the Krofft Production's 1991 remake of the series rather than the 1974 series.

"Play The Oregon Game and, if you know more Oregon facts, history and simple trivia than your opponent, you win.  The Oregon Game will test the skill of the staunchest native Webfoot and yet the multiple-choice questions offer an entertaining way for children and newcomers to learn more about Oregon." (Taken from the rules)

The active player reads aloud the question and four possible answers.  He then silently guesses the answer, checks the back of the card and if correct, moves his peg forward.  If incorrect, the other player then gets a chance to guess and move (if correct).  Players take turns as the active player until one has reached the end of the scoring track for the win.

boardgame: Heart of Crown: Far East Territory
description:
Heart of Crown: Far East Territory, the first expansion for Heart of Crown, requires either the base game or the Fairy Garden expansion to play, and it may be integrated with other Heart of Crown expansions.

The set contains 72 cards in the Japanese release:

     1 new Princess Card: Ouka, Far East Mathematician Princess
     12 new types of Common Card (5 of each type) 
     1 Revised version of Princess Flamaria from the base game
     2 types of Revised Common Cards from the base game with updated text, Charm Witch and Royal Purveyor (5 of each type)


The English release contains:

     1 new Princess Card: Far East Princess of Sums Ouka
     12 new types of Common Card (5 of each type) 
     12 randomizer cards
     12 dividers

Heart of Crown: Northern Enchantress is the second expansion for Heart of Crown. It requires either the base game or the Fairy Garden expansion to play and may be integrated with other Heart of Crown expansions.

The set contains 72 cards in the Japanese release:

     1 new Princess Card: Anastasia, Witch of the Northern Limits

     1 Revised Princess Card from the Far East Territory expansion (Ouka, Far East Mathematician Princess)
     1 Revised Common Card from the Far East Territory expansion, Samurai (5 copies)


The English release contains:

     1 new Princess Card: Princess of the North Anastasia
     13 new types of Common Card (5 of each type) 
     13 randomizer cards
     13 dividers

A press your luck dice rolling area control game themed around city building.

Players take turns rolling "Permit Dice" in attempt to get matching permit colors in order to place a one of their buildings on to the city board. Players press their luck by rolling more Permit Dice in efforts to get bigger buildings and to score more points but watch out for the Stop Work Orders or you might not be able to build anything on your turn.

Players earn points as their buildings get placed each turn and bonus points at the end of the game for Block Monopolies, Zone Mastery, and most buildings adjacent to each other. The player with the most points wins.

Two new winter village tiles for the game Keyflower: the Emporium and the Monument.  Available exclusively through Spielbox magazine edition 2013:03 (Summer 2013).

The owner of the Emporium gets 3 points for each set of one green worker and two workers of any other colour (including green) that they own at the end of the game.

The owner of the Monument gets 4 points for each set of one gold and two resources of any other type (including gold) that they own at the end of the game.

boardgame: Flames of War: Market Garden – The Allied Invasion of Holland, September-November 1944
description:
Publisher Blurb with updates by user:

This book is a supplement for the World War II miniatures game Flames of War. It covers the battles for Oosterbeek, Arnhem, Eindhoven and Nijmegen during Operation Market Garden, September 1944. This is compilation of A Bridge Too Far and Hell's Highway books. The original printed material was 160 pages in length across the two books. However the Market Garden compilations total reaches 340 pages. The Allied book featured 12 new forces and 11 old favorites, and the Axis book added 14 new armies as well as including the five original German lists.

description: From the game box cover: "A little like checkers and backgammon but easier to play... A two player strategy game with infinite possibilities."  Patilez is a race game where the winner is the first player to move all his numbered playing pieces from their initial positions to their designated spaces along the center line of the board while avoiding being hit by your opponents unique white piece and sent back to its start.

"Autorenn-Spiel Nürburg-Ring" game is basically a roll-and move and go around the track auto-racing type game. Penalties are listed on spaces on the board marked with letters "S", "R", "B" and "T". Players must finish the game by exact count in order to win.

Game description from the publisher:

Here there be dragons!

Dragons are fearsome! Dragons are cool! Dragons have LOTS of treasure for munchkins to loot! Munchkin Dragons has fifteen new cards. The treasures are wondrous indeed ... but first, you've got some dragons to slay. Good luck.

In OXYGIN, players make words from letters of the same suit, and "Gin" when they have a hand full of words. A bonus is received for playing the longest word.

The game is played in rounds with players taking multiple turns per round. The first player to begin the game is the player to the dealer’s left. A player’s turn is comprised of three steps (take a card, discard a card, and declare "Gin" if possible) and once completed, the next player going clockwise has their turn, and so on. This continues until the endgame is triggered for the round by a player who declared "Gin" during their turn. All players reveal their hand and any words are challenged (if needed). The player with the longest word wins the hand.

Gerontopoly game is aimed at helping health professionals and students gain greater awareness and sensitivity toward aging-related changes. The object of the game is to gain as much life satisfaction as possible without losing money, while avoiding developing chronic diseases. Players roll the dices and move their pieces around a board much as they do in similar recreational games of chance, but their "purchases" are mundane personal aids such as dentures, hearing aids and walkers. Players of Gerontopoly can find themselves detained in the nursing home, adult day care center or hospital. On the upswing, each time they pass GO, they receive $300 from Social Security. During the game, players try to avoid getting "Chronic Disease" cards and attempt to collect as many "Life Satisfaction" cards and as much money as possible. They try to accomplish this by answering various questions about aging including: *With normal aging, which declines the least--hearing, smell, taste or vision? Answering 'taste' earns the player $50. The acceptance of Gerontopoly by non-health care professionals helps to widen the scope of people learning about aging lifestyles.

In a 2-phase study, the acceptability and efficacy of the game was evaluated. Using a 22-item, postgame questionnaire, the 190 dental students, 23 dental hygiene students, 25 college sociology students. Results also suggest the game can change attitudes and enhance awareness about the concerns of the elderly.

Munchkin Kobolds Ate My Baby! is a 15-card booster themed on the Kobolds Ate My Baby! role-playing game that can be added to any Munchkin base game.

NOTE: This expansion was contingent on 9th Level Games reaching $50,000 or more on their Kickstarter campaign to raise funds for a reprint of the original KAMB! game. Said goal was reached AND exceeded (over $65,800 was earned with an original goal of $11,000 just to reprint the original game).

Drake: The Dragon Wargame is a fantasy miniature wargame with a twist. Beautiful dragon generals lead your army, their deadly minions and subjugated troops complementing your initial force. Everything else is called forth as you need it.

Craft the skills and abilities of your leaders with a custom card deck. Each powerful dragon has its own separate deck of spell cards, which is chosen by the player. Do you wish for raw power and destructive energy, or will you use mystical spells of protection to create a small force of indestructible veterans? Force Cards hold the inborn abilities and stats of the dragon commanders and their minions. Creation & Construction Cards allow you to “Summon” warrior beasts, flying enchanted creatures, and powerful energy nexus. Enhancement Cards give you options to “Beef Up” your troops with deadly ability bonuses. Snap Cards enable you to affect your opponent’s turn, countering their magical abilities at a price. Fast paced and involving, Drake allows for quick responsive actions, especially on your opponents turn!  Dragons are powerful, mystical, and highly intelligent beings, and they deserve a world where they are the dominant force of destiny.  Welcome to our world.

description:
Are you a geek? Maybe even a little nerd? You love pixel art and fantasy but are too lazy to learn complex rules?  Then GRABLIN is for you!!! A fun dice game based on simple rules, to play with your friends.

Throw your dice! "Grab" the treasure and start running! GRABLIN is the dice game that mixes luck, greed, and cunning. Choose whether to risk it and grab more treasures, or to play it safe. Steal your adversaries treasures, but pay attention! Behind every corner there's always the risk of a red skull, ready to bust you.

Game Rules:
A) Choose 3 DICE from the dice box, ROLL THEM, and add them to any other dice you've rolled so far this turn.
B) If you have 3 SKULLS showing, your turn ends; return all dice you've rolled this turn to the dice box.
C) If you have 3 FISTS showing, return them to the dice box in order to steal a TREASURE from another player, then go to STEP(D).
D) Otherwise, you may choose to go back to STEP(A) to continue rolling, or you may end your turn: for each group of 3 TREASURES showing on your dice, keep 1 TREASURE; return the other TREASURES and all FISTS and SKULLS to the dice box.

The player who gets 5 TREASURES first is the WINNER

boardgame: Swatters: Large Scale Bug Hunting Miniatures Rules
description:
Based on the Song of Blades engine and written by Andrea Sfiligoi, Swatters lets players create sci-fi battles between humans and alien bugs from books, movies or their own imagination.

Each player or team pits their human or alien forces against their opponent using miniatures and a tabletop area to create a battlefield.  The game includes rules for adapting several popular tropes (power armor, xenomorph aliens, alien arachnids, interstellar marines, etc.) and has several scenarios to play through.

Currently sold as a 45 page full-color PDF download from the publisher.  The game is based on the Song of Blades miniature wargame engine that's been used for everything from fantasy to ancients to Napoleonic warfare.
family: Series: Song of Blades and Heroes

description:
This is a small(ish) game about traveling across a small island in hopes of killing all the bosses in the castles. You will fight many monsters, and hopefully upgrade.

On your turn, you move, then check for monsters. After that, you move again, but any monsters on the board will move after you. You may fight them, or try to run away.

If you enter a castle, you will have the option of slaying the boss, or maybe something else (like searching for something).

Combat is the simple mechanic used in most FF gamebooks. Roll 1d6. If it is equal to or lower than the stat being tested, you succeed (hit the enemy).
Fight Game from 1950 using a spinner to find results of each round. A decision to a knock out are possible. Game was designed by Frankie Goodman of the US National & Intercollegiate Boxing Club

Game designed to teach people about the new currency that was due to come into effect in Britain in 1971. Leans heavily on Snap but neatly expands the idea of seeing matching cards. You had to be alive at the time to know how people were worried about getting rid of pounds, shillings and pence. A decimal based currency is so much easier and sensible but the British people took some time to get used to it. Games like this helped.

Rules:
1. Any number of persons may play this game.
2. One player deals the cards in rotation until the pack is exhausted, giving a conversion table to each player.
3. Each player keeps his cards, without looking at them, face down on the table in front of him. Then as his turn comes round he turns up the top card and places it before him, making another stack.
4. If the top exposed cards on any two piles
(a) two identical decimal currency cards (e.g. two cards both reading ‘25 new pence’), any player may call 'SNIP’:
(b) two identical old currency cards (e.g. two cards both reading ‘10/-'), any player may call ‘SNAP’;
(c) two cards expressing the same value but in different currencies (e.g. ‘1/-’ and ‘5 new pence’), any player may call ‘SNIP-SNAP’.
5. If the call is correct, the caller picks up the two piles concerned and adds them, face downwards, to his hand. If the call is incorrect, the caller must give each player one card face downwards from his hand.
6. The player who eventually holds all the cards is the winner.

A card game published by EJIN Institute. This card game is for 2 to 5 players. 4 to 5 players are recommended. One player control a group of police officers. The number of police officers in one game is equal to the number of other (zombie) players. Other players are zombie players. Each zombie player has their own house for sending zombies to the police station. The police must fight against the swarm of zombies. If all police officers are dead, the zombie players win. If the police can get rid of all the zombie players, the police player win.

A longer version of the game can be played, in which each player take turn as the police player and see who is the best defender. If the polcie player wins, the number of cards remaining in the draw deck is counted. The player with the most cards left in the draw deck in the winner.

Introspection is a game of over 1,300 yes or no questions divided into 6 categories: Getting to Know You, Your Family, Your Relationships, Work, Your Psychology and Your Religious & Philosophical Beliefs. With the yes or no cards provided, you predict what your respondent will say, yes or no. If you are correct, you move forward on the board..incorrect, you move your game piece backward. The dice is color-coded which corresponds to the quesion categories. If you roll purple, go to the Psychology questions! Should you land on any of the 26, Life Question Spaces, you must relate a short story from your life to the other players (2-6 players).Some of the Life Questions include, Aloneness, Regret, Love, Intollerance, Jealousy etc.In order to win the game, you must cross the finish line and Move into NIRVANA..but first, you must relate 2 stories, one about enlightenment and spirituality.

boardgame: The New U.S. Merchant Marine and International Salesman
description:
The Game is called: "Game of The New U.S. Merchant Marine Prepared under the direction of the U. S. Ship Board and International Salesman".

Object of the game: the players go on a competitive trip around the world spin, move and try to carry more cargoes than other competitor. The game ends, then one of the players traveled around the world and come back to New York port. The winner is the player who earned the largest amount in cargoe value delivered.

A game projecting the power of the US via the Merchant Marine after WWI. Ships travel a Mercator projection map showing the globe as colonial possessions of the European powers. The German holdings are indicated as "Former German Possessions". The islands north of New Guinea are designated as "Japanese Mandate". Key & scale at the bottom right; the index at the bottom left is of "Ports of the World & Chief Exports", including Australia (Freemantle, Adelaide, Sydney, Hobart). A circumnavigational route around the globe is marked in red with player directions printed near each spot. 
Game board 88 x 51 cms, divided into 4 panels, original wooden box, 2 spinners, 6 cast metal ships pieces & original pencil. Full directions are printed on the inside of the box cover. The cover has an attractive lithographed view of New York harbor with a ship in the foreground.

Players look for pair of tiles but instead of similar tiles there must be picture on one tile and word on another tile.

Player with the most pairs at the end of the game is the winner.

Like in usual Memory players look for pair of tiles but instead of similar tiles there is a mathematical operation on one tile and answer on another tile to look for in this game.

Player with the most pairs at the end of the game is the winner.

Like in usual Memory players look for pair of tiles but instead of similar tiles there is a mathematical operation on one tile and answer on another tile to look for in this game.

Player with the most pairs at the end of the game is the winner.

A set of flash cards containing music theory elements ( notes in different clefs, key signatures, notes of different values , etc. etc.) published in the late 19th century whose instructions contain 10 different games of different types.

Four  of the more distinctive  games in the instructions are :

-- A simple drawing/drafting game where a player keeps drawing cards from the stock until they match the time value ( eighth rest, 16th rest, half note, etc.) of an initial top card. After an initial go-round players  match the initial value with a card from their hand if possible, if not they resume the drawing process. First player out of cards wins.

-- A variant of this game where after all the cards are drawn, players form " melds' of a whole note's value ( 4 beats) and these are  ( two variants) then scored Rummy-style or game is played until someone has eight melds.

--A forerunner of climbing-to-X-number sorts of games ( O No 99, Zeus on the Loose, et al.)  where players play cards of rests and notes to get up to a whole note's value of 4 beats.

--A children's game with a pre-Knizia type of interesting scoring.. all cards are given out to all players-- them the players with the most of each value get to score the amount they have. ( E.G.   Person with 5 16th-note/rest values has more than everyone else, therefore they get 25 points, each of the cards counting for as many points as cards held.)

Who hasn’t been to the toy store or the dollar store and seen the plastic army men and said “There has to be a game there”?  Well I asked myself that often and finally took the plunge and wrote one.  Originally the game was only army men but I couldn’t resist the idea of using cowboys, Indians, and for some odd reason, firemen.  After all we are already playing with plastic dudes, we can include them all!

The basic idea here is to go to the Dollar store, spend less than $10 and have a fun miniatures game for two people.  This doesn't include terrain of course.  I recommend playing at the FLGS!  (buy something when you are there)

All the units are put into a bag and mixed up. The first player pulls a guy out of the bag and decides if he wants to keep him or reject him. Rejected units form a pile (not returned to the bag). If you keep certain units you get a bonus draw (if you keep an Indian you can draw a bonus guy from the bag or from the reject pile). The next player than can pick a unit from the bag OR the reject pile, if they pick from the reject pile they also get a second unit from the reject pile on top of any bonuses the first unit gives. So if I took an Indian from the reject pile I’d get to take another unit from the pile AND a unit from the bag or pile, the bonus units do not give extra units however)

Terrain is placed, units are placed near the edge of the table and it’s time to play. Basically it goes like this. Both guys flip cards from a deck, face cards are set aside, when the total you flipped equals 10 or more they stop. The total is the number of 6 sided dice they get for the turn. These are assigned to the individual units. Army men can have a maximum of 3 dice for example.

Now players take turns. On your turn you activate one unit. That unit then spends at least one die. The dice are used to move, shoot or perform special actions. Moving units are subject to opportunity fire, units performing opportunity fire are subject to covering fire. Of course these actions cost dice, and that limits what else they can do. Dice are indeed a precious commodity!

Remember those removed face cards? They are used to activate special powers and leadership powers. Your special power is determined by what the majority of your units are. Mainly army men? You get the artillery strike! Mainly Cowboys? You get the high noon duel! Leadership powers include healing, damage reduction and group fire. Another tough decision is how to spend those face cards!

The round ends when all the dice are spent. First check to see if any unit has died (taken enough wounds). Yes you don’t realize you are dead until the end of the round. Well you can be killed outright during the round but you get to spend all your dice on a retaliatory strike! No dice are wasted! (except for all those ones I rolled) Next you check victory, if you roll a singe d6 and its higher then the total number of guys you have left your army runs away! It’s possible that both armies run away on the same turn. Then no one wins, or both people do, fight about it.

SUMMARY from the designer:
"Shades and Glamour is a card game based on an alternate history of the English Regency.  The game mimics social life in 1814 — if one adds magic. The goal is to improve one’s social standing. Social standing is the value of one’s cards. One improves it by acquiring better cards. As with all romances, the game ends when a character becomes betrothed by losing his or her last card."

AVAILABILITY:
The game is available from the designers website as a print-and-play.  Some character cards are only available directly from the author at conventions.  The game additionally requires a standard deck of playing cards and a pen and paper.

GAMEPLAY SUMMARY:
Each player will play the character on their character card.  Each character card has a different balance of a number of different attributes.  The attributes each map to a suit of a standard set of face cards.  Each player holds five facecards with which one will attempt to acquire the other player's character cards from them.

Players will take turns making advances via one of their attributes toward another player.  That player can respond in kind, or counter with a different, attribute (analogous to each suit having a different suit which can trump it).  Players then simultaneously reveal their played cards and add that total to the corresponding attribute on their character card.  The player with the higher total triumphs, and takes the opposing player's character card.

If one does not hold a suitable card one can attempt to bluff by playing a face card as if it is suitable, but when it comes time to compare scores, only the attribute on the character card is counted, with no additional strength given via the face card.  Alternatively one can opt to not participate in an exchange at all, before the face cards are revealed, but one will lose a turn as a penalty for doing so.

Play continues in rounds until only a player loses her last card.  Points are counted, and the player with the highest points wins.

Century 21-opoly is a marketing/promotional board game released by Century 21 Real Estate. It is a derivative of Monopoly and its game play is identical to Monopoly in most respects.

The primary difference is that the properties, utilities, cards, etc. are oriented to the Century 21 Real Estate corporation. Aside from this, using the roll-and-move mechanic, players buy properties, build houses and hotels, and collect rent from fellow players. The player who doesn't go bankrupt is the winner.

An adventure game based on the research of objects.
The first who will find all the elements that compose the 3 objects chosen at the beginning of the game will win.
Each object is made by 4 different parts, that you can find all over the islands where the "survivors" are settled.
When you think to have found your parts, you have to sign them with a pawn of your own colour, and when you think to have found all your pieces, you go to see the solutions cards: if you have the right pieces, you have won! ...On the other side, if you have wronged pieces, you have ended the game, letting other players try to win.
The background is a post-nuclear place, where the "survivors" must try to create all objects that they were able to use before the war (like the telephone, a bicycle) starting from singular pieces found on the islands, and to assembly them, to come back to the normal state of life.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Players select tokens and start them from the middle Square. High roll of the dice decides the starter.
Each player in turn rolls the dice and moves his token the number of squares indicated.
Tokens can move in any direction, therefore players always have a choice of letter squares to land on.
As a player lands on letters, he writes them down in consecutive order. Players try and collect letters which give the best chance of building words of consecutive letters. The words may be of any length, and may have overlapping letters.
Example — PEGAGAYLYZX contains the words — PEG ; GAG ; GAY ; GAYLY.
If a token lands on an "ANY LETTER" square, the player chooses any letter he wants.
Tokens cannot pass each other, and cannot land on the same squares. Players can therefore try to block their opponents. If a token is blocked so that it cannot move in any direction, it loses that turn.
The words that are built, count one point per letter. The example above is worth 14 points.
The first player to collect 20 points is the winner, or, the game can be shortened or lengthened, by deciding at the start of the game what the winning score should be.

A variation on this game is "SCRAPWORD”
Each player chooses any word and all the players write it down. All the players will have the same list of words. The game plays the same way as "AWKWORDS" but when a token lands on a letter that the player has on his list, he crosses it out.
The first person to cross off every letter in every word, is the winner.

Chess Game for 2, 3 and 4 Players, from Germany, Berlin.

Theme: As described in the rules of the game, 2, 3 and 4 Players play chess clockwise. Most of the classic chess rules apply here. But anyway, Schach4 has its own rules which are brief and which are quick and easy to understand.

Game-play: It's fun to play chess on one board with up to 4 players. Everybody can fight against everybody. Team play is also possible. It's a challenge for beginners and advanced chess players. And professionals will break sweat when they encounter 2 or 3 armies on the battlefield.

Goal: The player or the team whose pawns are standing at last on the playing field wins the chess match.
3-by-3 bingo game for children learning to read.  Players match words or pictures from drawn tokens to corresponding words on their boards.

Publisher Blurb:

Battleships Armada: The fifth installment of the Star Fleet Starmada series! Dozens of new ships include the biggest in the Star Fleet Universe, the massive battleships: Federation Mars, Klingon B10 Invincible, Romulan King Condor, Kzinti Patriarch, Gorn Godzilla, Tholian Death Dealer, Hydran Monarch, Lyran Cave Lion, and the Andromedan Devastator. Also includes heavy dreadnoughts, light dreadnoughts, and other special classes. This Product also includes all of the battleships from FC Battleships Attack and all of the heavy and light dreadnoughts from FC boosters 16-17-18 plus "some other new ships". This is the NOVA edition; be sure you don't really want the ADMIRAL edition.

Publisher Blurb:

The Beatles Edition of Scrabble allows players to enjoy traditional Scrabble game play or rock the house with a customized Beatles theme. All you need are custom HELP! cards and words related to the Fab Four to earn bonus points. Draw black wooden letter tiles from a custom Beatles tile bag and place them onto customized tile racks. It's Scrabble with a twist that's sure to be a number one hit.

The scenario pack features 10 scenarios to be played in a double blind format.  6 of the scenarios are set in World War II, and 4 are from the French Indochina war in 1954.  Each side knows its own forces, but has limited information about the other side.

MM15db: Crossroads at Suxy - Suxy, Belgium, 11 May 1940

MM16db: Eve of Destruction - Oktiabr'skii State Farm, west of Prokhorovka, 11 July 1943

MM17db: Busting the Bocage - Vicinity of St. Martin de-Tallevende, France, 6 August 1944

MM18db: Magen David - Vicinity of St. Martin de-Tallevende, France, 6 August 1944

MM19db Strangulation: Hill 674, Hill 674, Dien Bien Phu, Indochina, 22 March 1954

MM20db Isolation: Ban Kho Lai, Ban Kho Lai, Dien Bien Phu, Indochina, 22 March 1954

MM21db Asphyxiation: Dominique 3, Domiminque 3, Dien Bien Phu, Indochina, 30 March 1954

MM22db Frustration: Huguette 7, Hugeuette 7, Dien Bien Phu, Indochina, 2 April 1954

MM23db Djebel el Messeftine, Mateur, Tunisia, 6 May 1945
MM24db Fort IX, Warsaw Poland, 26 September 1939

This is an unofficial fan-made expansion and is not affiliated with nor endorsed by Donald X. Vaccarino or Rio Grande Games.

Victory over your neighbors has always been kind of a big deal for you, but just when you thought building yourself the single most impressive empire in the land was all you had to worry about, the stakes went and got higher. Your victory points are now tied to your very life! It all started with that foul-cursed gold. In the future, you will never trust anything given to you by a man in a village who won't stop spinning when you try to look at him. Of course, now you have access to incredible power, but each time you use it, you bring yourself closer to your own demise. With great power comes great responsibility — or something like that.

Fortunately, you aren't alone in your fight. A ragtag band of mercenaries has arrived in the kingdom to save the day — or rather to fight for whoever has the coin. Your mercenaries can wreak havoc on your opponents, but if a stronger warrior takes them by surprise, the resulting curses find their way back to you. This war has turned you into a gambling man (or woman, as the case may be). How much of your own life will you risk to bring down your opponents? Oh, and remember to get your shots.

For Duel, players need the Treasure cards, Victory cards, Curse cards, and Trash card from Dominion or Dominion: Base Cards to play. As with other Dominion games, players must choose ten sets of Kingdom cards for each game.

Duel introduces several new "Curse" cards to the game of Dominion, the generation of a more advanced "Curse Deck", and a new token: the "curse token". It also introduces a "life points" system adding another win-condition to the game; in games with this condition, curse tokens modify a player's "Life Points" total. (If the "Life Points" win condition isn't used, whenever a card requires a player to gain +Y (curse tokens), that player must take one curse token for each curse token specified by the number "Y". Each curse token is worth -1 VP.)

Duel also introduces a new "Attack/Action/Reaction" card type, the "Warrior Cards". The strength of each Warrior Card is represented by a number within a shield symbol for each Warrior Card. The strengths of each Warrior Card range from 1-10, with 1 being the lowest strength and 10 being the highest strength. When a Warrior Card is played as an Action, the player playing the card first resolves the actions printed on the card in order from top to bottom (e.g. “Gain a Silver” for the Silver Knight; “+1 Card then +1 Action for the Warrior Angel). After unique actions have been resolved, resolve the “Each other player gains a Curse” effect common to all Warrior Cards. This effect causes each player to draw a card from the Curse Deck or play a Reaction card in response.  These Warrior Cards can also be played as a Reaction card to another player playing a Warrior Card as an Attack card; In this case, the two Warrior Cards "Duel" eachother, and the one with the highest power wins the battle (and unique results are followed as indicated on the cards and within the rules).

boardgame: BattleWin!: Battle To Win The Ultimate Battle Of Win
description:
On the distant planet of BattleWin the BattleGods have summoned all the greatest Battlers of the universe together to compete in the Ultimate Battle of Win.
Using a combination of Strategy, Bluffing, and Luck.
Battlers will Battle to Win by reducing their opponents health points using numbered "Battle Army" cards combined with "Battle Strategy" Cards
The Loser is the person with the least amount of Health points at the end of the game.
Do you have what it takes?

Setup:
Players face each other with a deck of 18 Battle Army cards (2xcards numbered 1-9) and 4 Battle Strategy cards (recruit,heal,battle and block)
Take your 4 action cards and keep them in your hand.
Place your health point card where other battlers can see it. Using a marker (a coin or some other object) to indicate your health status.
Decide on the maximum amount of Health you can have for the battle. This cannot be exceeded.
Battlers shuffle their Army cards and place the deck in front of themselves. 
Each player draws one card from their Army and places it face up on the Battlefield.

Playing the game:
The game is turn-based with each player taking simultaneous turns.

During each turn, the Battlers secretly choose between one of 4 Battle Strategy cards to play.
They then place the card face down on the table.
Battlers then simultaneously flip their chosen action card to reveal their Battle Strategy

More than 2 players varient: Players must say the name of the person they wish to battle after placing their Battle Strategy card.
(Or alternativly place a card with the players number on it down with their Battle Strategy Card)

Strategies are as follows: 
Recruit = You draw 1 card from your deck and place it on the battle field (from left to right) You can only have up to 5 cards in total on the battlefield
Heal = You draw a card from your deck and regain health points to the value shown on the card. The card is discarded afterwards
Battle = You attack your opponet with the total value from adding up current Army cards you have on the battle field (Army cards are discarded afterward)
if your opponent plays either their Recruit or Heal card your battle card cancels them out and your damage is dealt. 
Block = The first 3 cards of your opponent's Battle Army are blocked if they played their Battle card.

Game ends:
The game ends when one Battler has lost all of their Health points.Or when one Battler has depleted all of their Battle Army card deck.
(if more than 2 people are playing the remaining battlers take one more turn after one of the above end conditions have occured)
The Winner is the Battler with the most Health Points at the end of the game

It is 1900. A new soft drink craze is sweeping the nation, and soda-pop companies are springing up overnight to take advantage. All of these companies use more-or-less the same ingredients in different proportions to flavor their fizzy treats, and there is a limited supply of these ingredients on the market. You must outbid your competitors for the ingredients you need for your secret Kola recipe.

Kola is a standard auction game in which "4"-value cards have special powers, most of which force the next player in turn order to bid in a particular way or pass. The cards in winning bids become the lot for the next auction. Each player starts the game with a secret-formula card, detailing the distinct combination of ingredients the player needs to score a victory point.

In "Non c'è 2... senza 3!" the tiles are randomly spread out on the table, face down. The player on turn flips one of them and then looks for another one with the same image. And what is the difference with a common memory game?!? The fact that each image is repeated 3 times and the tile you look for doesn't have to be on the table, it could be in your opponents loot! you have to remember this in order to steal it!

Snake Clash is a simple and fast Print & Play board game inspired by the game in the Tron movie (1982). The rules of this game are very simple, it can be learned in 1-2 minutes.

Snake Clash is a line-drawing game. The goal is to create the longest snakes. The winner is who has the highest total length of his/her 4 snakes at the end of the game. Each player can start one snake from each side of the play-field (from one of the four larger circles). One snake from left, one snake from above, one snake from right and one snake from below (4 snakes in total.)

Player's turn comes after each other. Player rolls a die, that determines the allowed direction(s) of his/her next line-segment. (There is a compass on the play-field that shows the directions associated to die-roll.) He/she can extend one of their snakes by a new line-segment. (Or start a new one, if he/she doesn't started 4 snakes yet.)
Snakes can't cross each other and can't touch the same point on the play-field.

When each player have least one finished snake (When the further lengthening is impossible.), the game changes. No more die-rolls, players can freely draw a 1-segment line to any adjacent point from the end of any of their snakes.
 When nobody can draw lines anymore, the game is ended. The player wins, who has the largest total line-segment number.

A very long time ago, when science sucked and magic was really trendy, when there was a really huge offer of heroes and, luckily, they did not want to be paid, when behind each door you could find ugly but precious treasures or cute but lethal monsters and a dice roll could often decide the destiny of the humankind...
... in other words, when the world was a funny place, some incautious accepted each dangerous mission that was assigned by the most powerful characters of the time. The fate was on their side and their gestures became legendary.

Could you beat their fame? Be incautious yourself and try to build an even more legendary legend!

At the beginning of each round all the dice are rolled and grouped by their value.

In turn, each player chooses one group of dice and place it beside a deck of his choice in order to recruit Heroes, accepting new Missions or discover new lands (and the monster that lives there!) recovering special items.

When all the dice will be placed, each player in turn will use one or more heroes from his hand in order to defeat the monsters. The colour of the dice used to discover the land determines the skills the hero must have in order to participate to the battle.

The recruited Heroes will be added to the player’s hand, while Missions, defeated Monsters and Items will be placed in front of him side by side forming the timeline of his legend.

At the end of the game, the player will gain points for each item, monster and accomplished mission.

To be accomplished, some of the missions requires the player to discover certain items or to kill a specific monster (so only the cards placed after the Mission in the timeline will be considered), while some other requires the player to have already discovered certain items or killed a monster (so, in this case only the cards placed before the Mission in the timeline will be considered).

The player with the most points will be the winner.

Isle of Domination is a turn-based strategy-economy Print & Play board Eurogame.

Players can build cities, armies on game map. Gameplay goes around resources. There are two types of resources: Countable resources like food, wood, stone, ore, test-tube and gold. Special resources like horse, iron, fish, wheat, marble, diamond and mana are used to develop (science) or build (building) something. There is no storage for resources, a player needs to have access (by having a city near the resource) to certain number and type of resources to build something. Food is the most important resource, each city, extra population and army eats one food. You need food-surplus to build a city/army/extra population, but no problem if your food production goes under your food production (no starving to death).

Each player has an economical page (like character page in RPGs), that shows the state of his empire. Players use small  markers (for example: coins) for recording various things.

Player's turn have 4 phases:

     random event phase: A random event happens, that has effect to all players, usually a positive thing to each player.
     money phase: Player adds the number of his/her gold production to treasury. (Gold is the only resource in Isle of Domination, that is storaged.)
     army moving phase: Players can move their armies by grid lines of hexes. Combat system is very simple: if attacker's army is stronger than defender army, defender army has to be withdrawn. No dice rolling this time. Army power is based on sciences, and have modifiers by terrain, city or buildings.
     building phase: Player can build a city (on a hex edge occupied by an army), increase population, build an army, develop a science and/or build a building in this phase.


The goal of this game is to reach one of the six Victory Conditions. It is possible that all other Players have stronger economies, stronger armies and bigger empires, but still you win because you reach one of the Victory Conditions.
There are 6 different types of Victory:

     Territory Victory: You dominate by having a large number of cities.
     Military Victory: You dominate by having a very strong and large army.
     Population Victory: You dominate by outnumbering others in population.
     Science Victory: You dominate by knowledge. (You develop Future Science 2.)
     Cultural Victory: You dominate by your people's cultural influence. (You build Cathedral.)
     Magical Victory: You dominate by magical superiority. (You have a city near every Mana resource.)


Informational page contains exact numbers regarding the conditions above.

Game map has a random factor - dice-rolls determine one extra resource for each hex. This way, certain resources may have shortage or abundance, so diferent strategies can succeed in each game.
Randomized map preparation may make some of the Victory types easier than others. But when several players pursuit the same victory, it will be much more difficult to achieve it.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

boardgame: Walk the Plank! Ghost Meeple Mini-Expansion
description:
By very popular demand Mayday is making this really silly and totally unauthorized (by the designer) expansion available. The ghost meeple will be awarded to the first pirate who is fully eliminated from the game. (All 3 pirates are out.)

This player will continue to push/shove and generally cause mayhem with his ghost pirate, haunting the rest of the crew. He can't do physical things like cut or extend the plank, but can push and shove, but can't be pushed or shoved himself.

A pre-teens game for girls, published in an annual for Princess Comic fans. A Goose-type game, spin and move, obeying any instructions on the squares you land on. First to 60, wins.

The rules:
First you will need a top with numbers on it which you can make yourself out of a piece of plain postcard and a matchstick (see the picture above), or a dice.
Two or three of you can play this game and you will need a small coloured counter each (or a coloured bead) to represent you on your pony as you trek along. Each of you must throw the dice or spin the top in turn, and go forward the number of squares shown by the top of the dice, or the side of the top that comes to rest on the table.
But when you have reached the proper square you must follow any instructions that are lettered beside it. The winner is the one who gets back home first.

A game for pre-teen girls (although there is no reason why boys can't play). Published in an annual for Princess Comic readers. A simple Goose-type game. Roll and move but remember to obey any special square instructions. The first to get to Square 86 and roll a 6, wins.

Tavern Dice is a push your luck dice game!

A party of heroes heads to the tavern, to drink and play before heading to a quest.

Press your luck rolling those dice, try to score points for each wizard, Rogue, Knight, Archer or Barbarian...Don´t roll the skeleton or you will lose a die.

The more of the same hero you roll on consecutive rolls the more point it will score... if you don´t roll the one you need you will lose all your acumulated points.

No more dice? You are kicked out of the tavern, into the nice mud puddle!

Flipping Fishes is a fun dexterity race game for up to 4 players or teams.  Participants have cardboard "frying pans" which they use to fan or "swish" paper fish across the raceway (floor) and onto the same color plate at the finishing line.

From the box top "A fantastic fun family race game - flip your fish along the race alne and onto the dinner plate using only the frying pan!  No Touching with your hands!"

Trivial-Pursuit-like game that has players moving tokens around the game-board to answer questions in various topic categories.

Object Of the Game

The object of the game is to be the first player to work your token up to the center of the board and answer a question correctly at LEVEL 5.

Town Center: Expansion n°1 consists of four additional player boards for Town Center and nine wooden cubes in an eighth color.

This player board simulates the construction of Paris – bridges, the Seine, and two small islands – and will add quite a bit of variety to the gameplay.

You can use the nine brown cubes to create a three-dimensional player board during set-up, with the cubes representing hills and mountains. Two scenarios (San Francisco and Rome) are provided in the rules, but you can also try to map the topology of your hometown or do a completely random set-up. If you crave variety, you can customize the game every time you play!

Town Center: Expansion n°2 consists of four additional player boards for Town Center and twelve wooden cubes in a seventh color, with two different starting configurations.

This player board simulates the construction of New York City as a city with such an incredibly high density has to have its own version of Town Center. The board is topologically completely different from the original, which should alter the deepest-rooted habits of even the most experienced players. The twelve new dark green cubes represent the citizens of the city, adding new situations and new opportunities for construction to the basic game. After all, it would be a shame to build The Big Apple as a ghost town!

boardgame: Andy Pandy and Teddy are Fast Asleep
description:
Game published in the Andy Pandy's Annual, aimed at pre-school children. One player is red and the other player is blue. The red player travels along the red line and the blue travels along the blue line. Both lines are equidistant from the winning space in the middle of the board.

Moves are determined by the throw of a die. When the blue player lands on a yellow square, he goes forward two spaces. When the red player lands on a green square, he goes forward two spaces. If either player lands on a grey square, that player has to go back three spaces.

The first player to reach the centre illustration of Andy Pandy and Teddy having a sleep on a garden bench, wins.

Andy Pandy was created for the BBC television slot that later became Watch With Mother. In 1952, 13 episodes were recorded and these thirteen shows continued to be broadcast for 18 years! In 1970, the BBC made another 13 shows, this time in colour.

This is the first expansion to Krosmaster: Arena. It contains:

     Four new figures (Drop Knight, Maskun John, Jems Blond, Flame Knight)
     Four character cards
     Four minimaps
     Two token sheets
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
For centuries, the two kingdoms of F and B have fought each other in a peculiar way. Whenever war is declared, The Flag of The Old Empire is placed in the middle of The Ground, the contested borderland between the two kingdoms, and the two armies engage in battle to take control of The Flag and carry it into the opponent's lands: the first army to do so is declared victorious.

A Fair Battleground is a non-historical wargame set in a pseudo-Napoleonic era. To win, you must push the opposing army back or outmaneuver it, so that your own army can advance and eventually carry The Flag into the other player’s territory. Meanwhile, the opponent can engage you in battles to stop your advancement and try to take control of The Flag.

In A Fair Battleground, skillful deployment and maneuvering is more important than raw impact, but typical winning tactical moves of the era like attacking the opposing armies from behind are forbidden. Asymmetry between players is given not by a different mix of forces or objectives, but rather by the interplay of two pairs of opposing roles (Advancing vs Contending and Attacker vs Defender) that the two players take and exchange during the game. These roles determine both what the players can do and when they can do it, as well as which of the different armies are more useful to each of them.

Battle resolution is basically luck-less, but a simple optional rule that adds a random factor is provided for players who prefer luck-based systems.

---

A Fair Battleground is a print-and-play game that uses a board and 20 counters; the players must supply 9 euro-cubes or similar objects to be used as markers, and, optionally, two six-sided dice.

A Fair Battleground was an entry in the 2013 Two-Player Print-and-Play Design Contest.

Pompker is a "push-up poker". It's a pun in polish language, because it's a combination of words "pompki" (push-ups) and "poker".

The entire gameplay is similar to Texas Hold'em but there are some exceptions. The players do not have any starting amount of "money". Instead of money they bid with push-ups. During the game they can bid how much they want but bidding too much is a risk of losing. After finishing the round there is a winner who does not have to do the push-ups that he bid. All other players have to do the push-up according to how much they bid. The winning player gains as many points as all other players did push-ups in that round.

Each loosing player gains the points too (unless he passes and bids 0). He gains as many points as he did push-ups in that round.

If any player is not able to do the push-ups that he bid, he is out of the game and he scores minus points for the push-ups that he was supposed to do (e.g. if he bid 50 and he does 40, he gets  -10 points).

The game end when all players but one are eliminated. The winner is not the last player in the game, but the player who scored the most points.

					Rules SummaryLost Legacy is a game of risk, deduction, and luck for 2–4 players. You start the game with one card in hand from a deck of sixteen cards. On a turn, you do the following:


         Draw: Draw the top card from the deck and add it to your hand.
         Play: Choose one of the two cards in hand to play and place it face up in front of you.
         Effect: Carry out the played card's effect, after which the card is considered as discarded.
         End: If there is at least one card left in the deck, the turn goes to the next player; if not, the investigation phase starts. Using the investigation speed indicated on the card each player has in hand, players take turns guessing which player (whether yourself or someone else) holds the "Lost Legacy" card; this card might also be in the "Ruins", a location that holds one card at the start of the game and possibly acquires more cards during play. Rules:
'A Fascinating Board Game'
In this game, you and a friend each try to reach the Treasure. All you need to play this game are two buttons and a coin. Get buttons which are different in colour, for one button will be your marker, and the other button will be your friend's marker.

You both place your buttons in the Starting Box. The first player tosses up the coin. If the coin falls Heads, he advances his button two spaces; if it falls Tails, he advances his button one space.
The players take turns. Each player, of course, follows the board directions. As you see, sometimes a player may have to go back a box or two; sometimes he may lose his next turn; and sometimes he may be lucky and advance a few boxes. That’s the fun of the game.
The player who reaches the Winning Box first, wins the game.

Rules:
All you need to play this football game are two counters and a die. Throw the die to see who kicks off first - highest begins. Then each player throws and moves in turn. When a player lands on the tail of an arrow he moves directly to the head of that arrow. The white arrows are good passes and take you more quickly to the goal. The black ones are free-kicks awarded against you and take you farther from the goal. If one player lands on top of another, this is a foul and he must go back 7 positions.

To score a goal, you must throw the exact number required to reach the ‘Goal’ circle. If a number is thrown that is too many, you must go backwards for the number left over and await your next turn before you can shoot again . . . and so on until you get the exact number to score.

Try seeing how many goals you can score against a friend in half-an-hour (you can, of course, make it longer if you like). You might also form a local league, but if so all games should be the same period. Whatever the time-limit, the most goals, wins.

description:
Carcassonne is a tile-placement game in which the players draw and place a tile with a piece of southern French landscape on it. The tile might feature a city, a road, a cloister, grassland or some combination thereof, and it must be placed adjacent to tiles that have already been played, in such a way that cities are connected to cities, roads to roads, etcetera. Having placed a tile, the player can then decide to place one of his meeples on one of the areas on it: on the city as a knight, on the road as a robber, on a cloister as a monk, or on the grass as a farmer. When that area is complete, that meeple scores points for its owner.

During a game of Carcassonne, players are faced with decisions like: "Is it really worth putting my last meeple there?" or "Should I use this tile to expand my city, or should I place it near my opponent instead, giving him a hard time to complete his project and score points?" Since players place only one tile and have the option to place one meeple on it, turns proceed quickly even if it is a game full of options and possibilities.

This big box includes the basic game, Inns & Cathedrals, Traders & Builders, Princess & Dragon and The Tower. The English version also has The River.

description:
Players assume the role of fairytale princesses getting ready for the ball - Cinderella, Rapunzel, Snow White and Sleeping Beauty. They move around the board collecting dresses, shoes, accessories and make-up, combining them on their personal "princess board" to create a look. Their looks will earn them "glamour points" if items correspond to the fashion trends defined by the fashion towers.
Players must move they princesses according to dice rolls, and lead towards on of the 4 fashion towers - each of them corresponding to one type of item. The princess collects these items and may wear them, combining them so as to be as fashionable as possible. Cards on top of the towers are kept revealed, and indicate which is the current fashion trend - which also means that when you reach a tower and collect the item, a new one will start a new trend. There are four different possible trends for each of the four item types, and they form four sets of items (each set has it's own shoe, make-up, dress and accessory).
The game is a roll-and-move, with special spaces along the board that may help of hinder the princess and bifurcations to minimize the luck component. When a princess arrives at the Ball, the space in the middle of the board, she compares her fashion items to those on the towers, and get a glamour point for each match (but loses the item on doing so). If she have all items of a set, she gets points for them all, ignoring the fashion trends.
The game ends when the first of the towers run out of items, which means a winning player may speed up the ending. When it happens, the princess with most glamour points wins the game and the heart of prince charming.

description: Each player chooses a color. Players then take turns placing a vowel tile of his or her color on the board until all the vowels have been placed. The remaining letter tiles of all the colors are mixed together. Each player then draws three tiles, and players alternate placing one tile on the board and scoring for any forward or backward words, including embedded words, created by the placement. Words of a single color --- your color or an opponent's --- score more. Each player replenishes his hand following the placement. Play to an agreed-upon number of points.

The board features spaces numbered sequentially from 1 to 100. Each player selects a marker and a chip of a given color and places his or her marker on 50. An initial flick of the chip indicates the direction in which a player will move. Subsequent movement is determined by cards. Players take turns drawing a card, and all players must move according to the dictates of each card. Some cards require you to flick your chip or to invert your chip before you move; other cards give you a choice, such as either moving the distance indicated on the card or taking your chances with a dice roll. The first player to move past either 1 or 100 on the board is the winner.

description:

In Monster Zoo, you are a Zoo Keeper catching monsters in the wild for your Zoo - while trying to prevent other players from doing the same.

With over 30 unique monsters cards in the base game, there is a wide variety to the actions and combinations you'll see during a typical game.

The first player to build a full Zoo wins!

boardgame: Ciber: IT's all in the game
description:
In IT's All In The Game you try to place your consultants at the best positions with ongoing projects. The better you do this, the more points you score each round.

Meanwhile you have to keep an eye on the deadlines. And try to stop other players from making you lose your consultants for the rest of the game.

Every playing round exists of more action rounds. At the start of each playing round you receive 2 senior consultants (playing cards). In your turn you can perform 1 action.

Actions:
- Place 1 consultant with a project
- Move 1 consultant from a project to another project.
- Pay 2 points and a consultant. Choose another consultant (without the other players seeing which one).
- Pay 2 points and a consultant. 'Upgrade' this consultant to a principal of the same color. Show other players this principal.
- Pay 6. Same action as above, but withouth showing any card to the other players.
- Pass.

A project has 5 phases. Consultants have to be placed first at the 1st phase. Only then can a consultant be placed with the second phase. And so on.
The last or the last two consultants always have to be principals.

If a project is finished (that is consultant for phase 5 has been played) it is scored, the project and the consultants are put at the discard pile. A new project is then placed on the table.

Once all players have passed, the playing round is over and points are scored.

Every project is scored. Points being awarded 6/4/2/1 (later 10/6/4/2). The further towards the end of the project a consultant lies, the more points it generates.

After scoring all cards remain with the projects. Players can therefore score multiple times for the same consultant.

IT's All In The Game in a few words: 
- Lots of interaction. 
- Tactical choices. 
- Basic game with two expansions. 
- Unexpected depth.

For now it is only available with Dutch game rules.

Each player begins with a card that represents one of four strains of bacteria. Each player maintains a hand of five cards that can include mutation cards that will permit the player to grow other players' strains in addition to his own. Other cards are random events, antibiotics (used to interfere with other players' cultures), and bacteria (used to help grow one's own cultures). Players play one or more cards on their turns, and play continues until all cards have been played. Players receive points based on the success of their cultures and a bouns if they have grown all four cultures.

The game comes with 108 flat pieces of different shapes and sizes. Players take turns drawing a card that contains six items and then, within a time limit and using any of the pieces, creating the item that matches a die roll. The other players attempt to guess what is being created, and they may ask for the first letter of the item. Creator and correct guesser score either two points (no first-letter clue) or one point (clue given). Play until each player has constructed five items.

Long ago in Seven Cities of Gray, two adepts began a journey to learn forbidden majicks. Over three ages they created magical objects and potions, translated arcane cantrips, and discovered lost artifacts of four ancient mages: Arden, Balor, Canna and Dynin. In time their powers grew to the point where they, and their allies, fought each other with spells and wards across the Seven Cities for the title of Master Mage!

Mage Clash is a game for 2 players, lasting about 30 to 45 minutes.

Players will need the tiles and cards for the game, 5 six-sided dice, a set of traditional D&D dice and 80 eurocubes in 2 colors.

The game is played in two phases.

During the first phase - Learning Majick - players will play tiles (Magic Items, Mystic Objects, Magic Spells, Casting Wards, Potions and Cantrips) from their hands by paying Elemental Resource costs. Each type of tile has a different power associated with it and the collection of sets allows a player to gain victory points or have the choice of Special Artifacts to be used in the second phase. Discard tiles boost a players army and mana abilities.

The first phase lasts eight rounds and mid-game victory points scored

In the second phase of the game - Combat in the Cities - players use their Magic Spells, Casting Wards and Special Artifacts to combat each other in seven cities. Some cities have special powers of their own that affect the combat; each city is also worth victory points.

Each Magic Spell has a certain number of dice associated with it. Players roll five dice, taking a subset of these, augmented by their Casting Wards. Armies act as hit points in each of the cities and players combat each other until one player wins 4 of the 7 cities.

Phase Two victory points are added to those of Phase One to determine a winner.

Best Fantasy-Themed Game category in the 2013 Two-Player Print-and-Play Design Contest!

You are a monarch, like your parents before you, a ruler of a small pleasant kingdom of rivers and evergreens. Unlike your parents, however, you have hopes and dreams. Now's the time to put your knowledge to the test because your favorite super heroes are joining forces with your favorite matching game to create the most super matching game the world has ever seen! The Marvel Comics Edition of MEMORY features the iconic comic book covers of your childhood with new super twists. Play power cards that release different strategies or steal a card and change the fate of the game. Match the most cards, and you'll be the one saving the day!

This game is no longer in production.
If replacement pieces are needed, please contact SimplyFun. We'll do our best to replace game pieces as long as they are available in our warehouse.

Players each receive a starting card that has three elements: a number, a color and a fruit. Everyone tries to win three more cards that each match a different element of their initial card. The tricky bit is this: to claim a card, you must be the first to cover it up with your hand. But then you must recall the three elements on the card in order without peeking.
