Or you could choose one of the other archetypes, each double-sided, to give you a wide and diverse variety of options. Plan your moves carefully as the city is ever-changing and you'll need to gain influence with different social circles to gain the influence you need. Things will take a dramatic turn as morning changes to evening and the new friends you made will become more valuable than ever. You'll compete over a pool of available friends, so you'll need to carefully choose which people will be your new friends and when to make them. Your new friends, in turn, could help you gather more influence and with that crucial end-game score.

The goal of Suit Up is to have the highest Score at the end of 16 rounds (each the equivalent of an hour of the day). There are several things that add to your Score, namely:
1) Friends you made (individually)
2) Having a representative of all four areas of influence (Muscles, Brains, Suits and Cash)
3) Having the most Friends of an area of influence (amount varies according to setup)
4) Remaining Influence (at a low conversion-rate).

(Note: This listing covers only the compilation versions of Saboteur that include both the base game and the expansion; for a listing of the expansion-only versions, go to Saboteur 2 (expansion-only editions).)

In Saboteur, each player takes on the role of a gold-digging dwarf or a saboteur who wants to hinder exploration of the gold mines — but each player knows only his own role, so the digging may or may not go as planned!

Each turn, a player either lays down a tunnel card to dig from the start card toward one of the goal cards (or potentially away, if a saboteur) or plays an action card to help or hinder someone. If the diggers manage to find the gold hidden under one of the goal cards, then the diggers share the loot found there; if the gold can't be reached before the deck runs out, the saboteurs profit instead. After three rounds, the player with the most gold wins.

This version of Saboteur includes the base game plus additional role cards (the boss, profiteers, geologists), new action cards (steal gold, change your role), and new tunnel cards featuring doors, ladders and bridges. What's more, the gold seekers can now be divided into teams — blue vs. green — and only those on the team that finds the gold score anything — assuming that anyone finds the gold at all, of course...

2000 point value army for the fifth playable faction for the AT-43 franchise.

Box Contents
• 1 unit of 4 Warmongers: basic super-soldiers
• 1 unit of 4 Sharpshooters: elite super-soldiers
• 1 Prowler: basic combat strider
• 1 Vandal: the most powerful Cog combat strider
• 3 heroes from the C-Naps line: C-Naps G09, C-Naps G36 and C-Naps G45
• 3 high walls
• 1 container
• Introductory Rules
• 1 Cog tactical manual
• 1 game poster
• 12 dice
• 1 “Cog” ruler
• 1 explosion template
• 8 game cards

Objective: Be the fastest on a circumnavigation of the world
 - moving by turns -
simple 'Roll & Move' game with following mdifications:
1 ending on a space with flags add or subtract additional spaces
2 you have to reach stopovers within a diven time (days); if you don't achieve it you're out
3 the earlier you reach a stopover the more you achieve bonus cards (waiting for the other players)
4 game ends at Friedrichshafen after all players arrived; player with most bonus cards wins

remarks: 
published probably 1930

Objective: be the first getting all of the meeples in your home base on board of the Zeppelin
 - acting by turns -
each player starts with 6 meeples, placed on her/his home base;
roll the die: if it shows a 1 - 5, place one of your meeples onto the correspondent waiting position; with a '6' it ends up with a safe seat in the cabin. Turn ends after a '6' or if all waiting positions are occupied; if not - it's up to the active player to roll the die again.But if a waiting position is occupied you must take that meeple and put it into your home base.
remarks:
published ~1920/30s
due to the fact that these kind of games were played for money players must 'bought into'; before the game started they had to draw a chip - top was neutral, reverse was yellow or (in one case) black. The lucky one (socalled fare dodger) got her/his stake back at the end of the game - no matter what position.

This is an small game self-published by Yasushi Nakaguro.
This is an operational level wargame of the German campaign to conquer Norway in 1940.
The game has only 3 pages rulebook and each side has only 15 naval units.  And game contiues 15 turns.
Naval movement is an area movement.  Combat occured in the same area.  Landing and air power are included but very abstracted.
The German player has 2 basic strategies, one is conquering Norway by landing force and the other is advancing one of his trade raider ship to the Atlantic.  In both cases, the victory is finally decided by victory points.  They are gained by conquering Norway or advancing trade raiders or eliminating enemy units.
The game has small map which are printed on the back side of the rulebook.
The game can be played within 1 hour.
Second game of "long-map mini game trilogy".

GODS: Intervention is a fast strategic tile placement game set in the early years of human civilization. Players represent gods that influence the civilizations by granting them skills they may use to develop.

GODS does not use any board. It is played directly on any table. In each players turn they choose to pick and place one of the five different tiles available; a temple or one of the skills; Architecture, Conflict, Culture or Trade. Temples represent a headquarter for your devotees and may be placed anywhere on the table except in a civilization you already have a temple or next to another temple. Skills must be placed adjacent and orthogonal to another tile.

Placing a skill activates the surrounding and adjacent tiles. Choose between using their effects or to collect points.

Each game uses a set amount of each tile and as soon as one of the different tiles are run out the game ends. At that time the player with most points of the skill they have least of wins.

GODS is a brain burner. Players needs to be strategic while it is extremely difficult to predict the other players moves. A conflict may arise to destroy en entire civilization or through trade a civilization may loose much of its competence.

Objective: be the first to reach your home base with all (4) meeples
 - moving by turns -
'Eile mit Weile/Pachisi' variant with following modifications
1 the number stepping out of the base and another die roll is the "1". 
  If you don't use the "1" to step out, ist counts double and you may roll again.
2 you can't be thrown out if your meeple rests on a 'bank'
3 you block all others if there a two meeples (same color) on the same space

The game was published about 1932.

Directions are on inside of top cover. The game board is the inside bottom of the bottom box. Components: spinner dial and four wood colored player pieces.

Players spin the spinner and move their pieces (gliders). The object of the game to keep gliders in the air. The last player who stay in the air wins the game.

Directions are on inside of top cover. The game board is the inside bottom of the bottom box. Components: spinner dial and four wood colored player pieces.

Players spins the spinner and moves they pieces. The object of the game to obtain the highest number of points in going aloft four times and make as many drops with a parachute.

From McLoughlin & Sons 1885

"Game of Black Cat" card game with 53 playing cards, two counters and an instruction booklet in a paper covered cardboard box; playing cards have printed images of cat parts such as whiskers, hind paws, eyes etc. on one side; box cover lithographed with the image of a black cat's head;

The first published miniatures wargaming rules of the 20th century. A rulebook on miniatures and other props for playing children's games.  A companion to the designer's later title Little Wars. Floor Games is a crude miniatures and RPG hybrid.  Creating the gameplay area is large part of the gaming experience as designer Wells explains "...the setting-out ... is half the game."  While specs and dimensions for setup are given, many of the details like combat resolutions are left for the children to make up on the fly.

Floor Games has rules for two games and suggestions for four minor games.  The designer gives dimensions and parameters for setting up The Game Of The Wonderful Islands and  Of The Building Of Cities.  While "...glance ... shortly at some other ... uses of the floor..." means that Wells briefly suggest ideas for four minor games: Funiculars, Marble Towers, Castles And War Games, and But Very Little Of War Games.

The Game Of The Wonderful Islands -- play two competing explorers, Captain G. P. W and Captain F. R. W of settling tribal islands that are modeled after Greek civilization.

Of The Building Of Cities -- play as rulers of the fictional twin, modern cities, like London and Westminster.  A crude city management simulation game.

Expanded by Little Wars

135 pictures are on the table. The clue you are given matches none of the pictres... Two pictures has a word sounding like the clue word you are looking for... And that's the answer! 
So think quickly and find the two matching pictures before everyone else!

The goal of the game is to name the word by pointing two pictures. To win a point you must be the first to point the good pictures.

100 pictures are on the table. The clue you are given matches none of the pictres... Two pictures has a word sounding like the clue word you are looking for... And that's the answer! 
So think quickly and find the two matching pictures before everyone else!

The goal of the game is to name the word by pointing two pictures. To win a point you must be the first to point the good pictures.

boardgame: AT-43 Hero Box: Therian Omega Tiamat
description:
Tiamat is a rank omega overseer, the greatest commander of the whole Therian army. In control of her specially designed golgoth, she fights the enemy in hand to hand combat, reaping infantry as well as armored fighting vehicles with the same fascinating ease. Her mastery of nanoresources allows her to come back again and again, as long as there are overseers to steal their bodies!
 This Hero Box contains:  
1 miniature  
1 nanogenerator  
1 card : Tiamat

Z. is a 2-4 player card battle game that pits Survivors against the Fallen (zombies). It combines elements of Magic: The Gathering and other card/tabletop classics with video game sensibilities and a horror movie atmosphere.Each new match allows the players to create and live out their own zombie horror film. Every card in Z. is a high quality image featuring real people and real horror makeup/effects in place of 3D art or illustrations.

In Z., the Survivor and Fallen players take turns playing new cards from their hand, which are drawn from their deck. These can include new characters, weapons, traps, etc. Additional resources needed for playing cards are generated automatically at the beginning of each turn, but may also be manually increased or altered through various gameplay mechanics.

(Since the Fallen cannot “think” and strategize per se, the Fallen player not only controls his or her units directly, but also acts as a sort of director for the overall events of the game . Many of the Fallen’s cards and especially Killstreaks can modify the core rules of the game.)

In the middle of the play field are three Location Decks, each one representing a unique setting (Cabin, Police Station, Hospital, etc.). Everything in Z. revolves around these Location Decks:

-Survivors must send out characters (units) to search the Location Deck for Supplies, items, other units, and objectives.
-The Fallen must simply wipe out the Survivors before they complete their objectives, regardless of what mode is being played.
-Survivor and Fallen units that occupy the same Location can encounter (attack or use abilities on) each other.

The cards found in each Location Deck are also themed after that Location, adding a greater variety to the gameplay and types of cards available. Not all Location Deck cards are Survivor-friendly, however. These Locations are usually overrun with Fallen, as well as other dangers and obstacles.

The Z. Year One "Core Set" includes over 450 cards, with all six Starter Decks for the Survivors and Fallen, plus the first Location Set.

Pocket Footy is a dice game based on Australia Rules Football. 7 custom made dice represent players and actions on the field to simulate a fast, action packed game of Aussie Rules.

It's light, quick and simple to play. 7 dice, 1 bag and instructions - that's all you need!

The 7 dice represent 4 key playing positions, the Umpire who adjudicates who has possession of the ball, a Contest die that determines who wins the ball in open play and the Pass Percentage die that determines if a pass has been successful.

The aim is to get the ball down to your Forward positions and kick a goal.

Aussie Rules football is a fast game and possession changes hands a lot. This game too is fast and hectic with players swapping dice as possession is won & lost.

After 4 quarters of play, the winner is the player with the highest total of goals and points.

It is a dice game, so luck is involved. There is the option to play some advanced rules that allow for re-rolls and a push-you-luck mechanic.

Please enjoy it for a fast, light filler that Aussie Rules fans will love!

DreadBall: The Futuristic Sports Game is the hyper-kinetic sci-fi sports game of unparalleled speed and ferocity – a fast, fun and tactical miniature sports board game written by Jake Thornton and created by Mantic Games.

Two coaches compete for victory with teams of beautiful miniatures on a stunning sci-fi pitch. The game is easy to learn, yet challenging, with carefully orchestrated plays and counter attacks hinging on positioning and the mercy of the dice gods.

This expansion adds an additional team made up of Male Corporation Miniatures, the Trontek 29ers.

The base game contains 12 myrmidon pieces (two each of six different types) (as well as a 6×7 checkered board and a cotton case). This expansion contains 28 additional pieces (two each of 14 new different types) in order to provide quite a few additional playing configurations.  The base game allowed 720 starting combinations for each army, amounting to 518,400 distinct setups, with just its 6 pieces for each team.  The Myrmidons Expansion gives each player nearly two million (1,860,480) starting configurations, for well over three trillion (3,461,385,830,400) setups for a six-piece-per-side setup.  If you choose the new 12-piece-per-side option, each player will have nearly seven trillion (6,704,425,728,000) setups.

This expansion was first available through Crowdfunding: nestorbooster.

From the United States of America (who could imagine) comes the world’s best football board game. El fútbol en casa is truly football in the house. Your house. No, it is not a computer game where you pretend to make bicycle kicks, backheel passes, fantastic dribbles, terrific shots, and remarkable saves. It is not a game where the player with the best hand-eye coordination wins.
It is, rather, a football board game where knowledge of football strategy, tactics, and formations is more important than the ability to quickly punch a button or move a lever.
Why pretend you’re something you’re not? You’re not a football superstar and a computer will not make you one.
Why not play a football simulation game where your brain controls movement and play rather than some software program? You don’t have the physical abilities of the great players but you may have more overall, intrinsic knowledge of the game they play.
El fútbol en casa is a chess-like game of strategy and tactics where, in addition to good and bad fortune (always present in any sporting event), knowledge and skill determine the winner.
You are not a player or a coach. You are the team. You pick the formation. You move the players on your team and pass the ball. You make the tackling challenges and take the shots on goal. You commit fouls, suffer injuries, and get carded. You win. You lose. In short, you experience what a football team experiences.
This is an accurate, well-researched, mathematical representation of association football. All 17 Laws of the Game are incorporated in the play.
In addition to the Basic Game that pits two teams of equal strength against one another, a Tournament Play booklet is included that describes how teams of different strengths can play each other. (You could, for example, play all the games of the World Cup Finals.)
This all-wood game is recommended for players 13 years of age and up. Knowledge of football is helpful but not necessary. Also, many materials are included to individualize your game. (If, for example, you don’t like the color of your team’s kits or jerseys, you can make your own.)
  
(Designer's explanation)

Domain is a tabletop card game for 2-4 players, or up to 8 players during team play matches. It focuses on a world building element, where all players contribute to an ever expanding playing field. Roads connect to Soul Wells, which are used to score the game and provide the magical energy players use to summon monsters or cast hexes. Players move along the system of rolls with a dice roll. Roads may also have other effects, such as reducing movement over harsh terrain, or increasing a player's draw.

Summoned Monster cards may be stationed in a controlled Soul Well to guard them against other players, or left in a  player's hand, so that they may attempt to conquer other players' Soul Wells. So there is a strong balance between defending your territory, while having enough strength to expand into enemy territory as well. Hexes are used to manipulate the playing field, support combat or increase draw capacity.
 When combat does occur, it has been designed to be accessible and casual, in which battles may be resolved quickly and easily without complex counting or score keeping mechanisms. This keeps the focus of the game on strategic use of the world and unique card abilities to play to a Soul Well's power.

By matching Soul Well or monster Domains with your Lord's (player token piece), a player can boost the power of his creatures, and make his territory more difficult to conquer, or activate specific Domain abilities for his monsters.

An average game takes 30 minutes to an hour for 2 people and 1 hour to 3 for 4 players. It all depends on how players shape their gaming world :)

boardgame: Fairy Tale Assassin League: Snow White?  Not Anymore
description:
Fairy Tale Assassin League is a storytelling card game that asks the question: "What happens when Snow White goes to Las Vegas and turns bad?" There are no more happily ever afters.

The game starts with players choosing a fairy tale character; one will be the villain, the remainder good. Each character starts in one of six locations (restaurant, parking lot, theater, stage, kitchen and back alley). The first act of the game is to come up with reasons why the bad guy has to go down. The bad guy starts by bragging about how bad they are. The heroes then say how they have been personally hurt by him. Players can jump in with suggestions for other people’s stories.

The rest of the game is about bringing the villain down (and maybe to justice). The bad guy starts the game. Players take turns to add to the story, assisted by a tableau of scene cards (representing some key point in a revenge story plot) and action cards that can be played from hand. Another player’s version of events can be challenged by playing an action card. Challenges are resolved by rolling dice, with the winner gaining the card their opponent used. All cards are worth victory points.

The aim of the game is to score the most victory points while also telling an interesting story.

Multiple FTAL decks are available, and they can be combined for a larger scale game.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Snitch! is a stand-alone game based on cops working to solve crimes by hearing "The-Word-Out-On-The-Street".  In this game, different types of information is gathered from Snitches.  In order for this information to be valuable to the authorities, it must be, not only found, but then also delivered to the Police Station / DA.

Cops start with nothing, and must establish sources, and then use them to break the case wide open!

Snitch! is inspired by all of the many American TV shows about Police Work.

boardgame: AT-43 Hero Box: Captain H. Newton
description: Captain H. Newton is an officer and hero of the U.N. army. Aboard Number One, she has no rival. Besides no matter if she is on foot or aboard her Fire Toad, she can repair any armored fighting vehicle including her own. And destroying her strider is not enough to eliminate her: the miniature on foot is then deployed to carry on fighting!

Atis-Astarte is an unusual rank alpha overseer, a commander for the Therian army and its first hero. She enjoys battle so much that she specialized in close combat. Leading her grim golems, she attacks anything within her reach with celebrated fury. Her secret move: a unique routine allowing her to teleport her unit in contact with the enemy!

boardgame: AT-43 Unit Box: Steel Tac Arms
description:
This Unit Box contains a unit of three steel TacArms: two standard TacArms and a TacArm leader, each equipped with two laser guns. One of them can be promoted Sergeant. Particularly resilient, the TacArms are also capable of piercing even the thickest of armor with their great laser guns. Showing exceptional accuracy and impressive attack rate, these weapons make the steel TacArms one of the most dreadful units in the White Stars army. For further tactical possibilities, one of them can become a Sergeant.

Contents: 
• Box of 3 miniatures, 3 Steel TacArm, 1 Torso / kit leader, with laser gun x 2. 
• 2 cards : Steel TacArm Sgt, Steel TacArm

Grim golems are assassins and hunters. With their optical jamming devices, they can step out on the frontline without fear. There is no point establishing any complex strategy: they run straight for the enemy, drowning him in waves of fire before finally engaging him to give him a taste of their reaper blades.
• Box of 6 miniatures.
4 Grim golems. Armament: reaper blades/reaper blades.
1 Grim golem. Armament: flamer.
1 Grim golem leader. Armament: reaper blades/reaper blades.
• 3 cards : Grim golem.

The Strielitz Kolossus chastise the enemy of the Revolution with steel and fire. With grenade launcher fastened to one arm and a flamer fastened to the other, they make their enemies tremble in righteous terror.

Standing testimony of the success of the Red Blok, Strielitz Kolossus units are irreproachably proficient: what they cannot burn, they pulverize. They reap the infantry and mow through battlesuit units with terrifying casualness.

The Strielitz Kolossus are infantry hunters, comfortable at all distances and against any category of infantry.

This unit box includes:
•Three miniatures: 2 Strielitz Kolossus, 1 Strielitz Kolossus Sgt.
•Two cards: Strielitz Kolossus, Strielitz Kolossus Sgt.

boardgame: AT-43 Unit Box: Fire Toad U.N.A.
description:
Toads are the Type * AFV of the UNA in AT-43 and come in four different flavours. Toads are quick with 25 movement, They are deadly, with a wide assortment of weapon loadouts for every situation, and they are cheap. While not having the armour of the Red Blok, Toads are very resilient when in a Mind company. This list gives all AFV’s a repair for free so a unit of 3 toads can repair 3 damage a turn. This is before you even start to throw Steel Trooper Engineers into the mix. The toads are fairly quick and are great for flanking the enemy.

Fire Toad
armed with 2 laser guns the Fire Toad is the jack of all trades. It can take out everything from afv’s to infantry. This is the most common variant of toad used in many lists because of this fact.

Light Prince
A hybrid between the Iron Rain and the Fire Toad, the light prince packs a MG and a laser gun. It is able to throw 3 mg shots and 1 lasergun shot at its target. It still retains the ability to engage AFVs while being able to put out enough fire to have a hope of killing infantry that are in cover.

Iron Rain
This guy is a battlesuit killing machine. A full unit of Iron Rain’s put out 18 MG shots a turn. When shooting at a type *** infantry unit in full cover you’re bound to score a few wounds due to the sheer number of dice. The Lancelot is better suited to killing more lightly armoured units and grounding those suits that don’t have stability (Red Blok and Therians). if you’re taking these guys, its to neutralize your opponents ungroundable battlesuits. Otherwise you’re better off with Lancelots.

Lancelot
The Lancelot is an infantry killing monster. with 2 grenade launchers it puts out a lot of indirect fire. Note that the card is a misprint and each grenade launcher only gets 1 shot instead of the printed 3. Even still, a unit of 2 of these guys can get the all important double damage template. This weapon grounds units and ignores cover so it is a better choice if you are facing Red Blok or Therians as their type *** infantry can still be grounded.

Contains: 
-1 figurine: Fire Toad Mk IV, 2 light grenade launchers, 2 light laser cannons, 2 light MG
-8 cards - Fire Toad, Fire Toad Sgt., Fire Toad Light Prince, Fire Toad Light Prince Sgt., Fire Toad Iron Rain, Fire Toad Iron Rain Sgt., Fire Toad Lancelot, Fire Toad Lancelot Sgt.

Party game, What? Girls Night Edition, is a spin-off of the original What? party game.  Made for girls only, this party game is great for large groups of people. A hilarious party game, your girlfriends will be guessing who could have possibly said what! Players answer three categories of questions: General, Fill In The Blank, and Sticky Situation.  Girls can be as honest or as deceitful as they want, but the goal is to fool your friends so they can't guess your answer!  Shock value anticipated as players give their most creative answers.  Fun party game for bachelorette parties, girls night, pajama parties and any occasion where the girls can kick the boys out for a few giggles!

Catan: New England is an expansion for The Settlers of Catan that requires all of the components from that game in order to play other than the hex-shaped resource tiles. Instead of creating an island from those tiles, players compete for resources and building locations on a fixed map that features the six states that comprise New England in the U.S.: Maine, New Hampshire, Vermont, Massachusetts, Connecticut and Rhode Island.

The StoryIt is the 23rd century. There is an energy and resource scarcity on Earth. The planet is exploited and the world economy is about to collapse. Desperately Spaceships be sent out to explore the neighboring planets. A crystalline substance, shining like gold, with incredible energy content is discovered on Mars. It gets named Rich Yield Crystal Energy, or in short: RYCE.

RYCE could satisfy the insatiable hunger for energy of Earth if… yes if it is possible to collect the material on Mars, and bring it back to Earth. Every space traveling nation is called to participate and thus to secure a part of the energy source. But the difficulties are considerable. The extraction system must be developed and transported to Mars.

Hostile life conditions and water scarcity in this EMPIRE OF SAND do one last thing, but the challenge is accepted.

But then the shock: Mars is inhabited. The Martians live in underground cities and were not detected by the sensors.The Martians turn out to be cooperative and strongly point to the dangers of the planet. The mighty CRAWLERS, which move through the sandy deserts of the planet and cause dangerous sandstorms. And a mysterious faction, that calls itself THE BROTHERHOOD and that has no sense of humor when competing with their resources.

A difficult task, which can only be overcome with the support of all factions...if it were not for the rivalry among the nations!

				
				
					DescriptionRyce: Empire of Sand is a area control and resource-management strategy game with a thematic background on a variable game board with interactive mechanisms, low downtime, low luck elements and a variable and calculable game-time. Conflicts in the game are solved by majorities instead of fights and war.

Each player takes the role of one of six space traveling nations of Earth (USA, Europe, Russia, China, Japan, India). The Players land on Mars with their bases, recruit astronauts, and send astronauts out to collect resources, install mines, build tunnels, and taking over control of mines from other players. Collected resources are spent in more astronauts, new mines, new technologies (to upgrade the collection of resources, the amount of astronauts, the movement-range etc.),or in influence to the three mighty factions on Mars (The EARTH, The MARTIANS, and the BROTHERHOOD). From the factions the players get control of special units and also get influence cards, that they can play in their turn to get different benefits. Through the controlled mines, the level of technology and the influence on the factions and their special units the players get victory points. The player with the most victory points wins the game for himself and his nation.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
A set of seven additional dice only made available to those who participated in the game's KickStarter project.

- Three monsters (Harpy, Cockatrice, and Necromancer)
- Three legendary artifacts (Vorpal Sword, Aegis Shield, and Necromancer's Staff)
- One spell (Pain spell)

Pet Frenzied is played with thirteen pet cards and thirteen matching house cards.

Before the game starts, the pet cards are hidden (facedown) in the play area. Each player that receives a house card and goes in search of the matching pet. When they find it, they take a new house card and go in search of the next pet. This continues until all pets have been found. Whomever has found the most pets at the end of the game wins.

Touchy Feely is a game in which players have to find objects using only their sense of touch.

Twenty-six Touchy Feely trinkets (miniatures of real objects) are placed in a nylon container, the "Tube of Touch". Cards showing each object indicate what items should be found.

Three games can be played:

    Memory Match: Four cards are drawn. The players get five seconds to memorise them. They are then placed face-down. Now, both players simultaneous go searching for the items shown, and get sixty seconds to retrieve three items and place them on top of the cards. Whomever has the most matches wins the game.
    Speedy Feely: One card is drawn. Both players go searching for the object. When someone finds it, he takes the card, and the next card is drawn. Whomever has collected the most cards after sixty seconds wins the game.
    Hand Solo: A solo version of Speedy Feely, with one player trying to find as many objects as he can.


A fourth game, Kitchen Sink is played with custom items rather than the supplied trinkets.

Chain Letters is a game similar to Barrel of Monkeys that's played with a number of letter tokens. Each token has hooks that can be used to link them to the other tokens.

The letters are placed in a pile on the table. The players simultaneously attempt to create words by making chains of the letters, without dropping the chain or linking a letter upside down. The pile cannot be shuffled, so the words have to be made using the letters that are visible.

The game continues until no more words can be made; the player who has collected the most letters wins.

Game about travel with Auto, Train or Bus. Each player tries to find, while en route, the objects named on the cards he has drawn. Player scoring the most points after all the cards are exhausted is the winner.

The object of the game to see which of the players is first to cover the course from New York to Los Angeles, stopping in each of the cities en route.
The players spin the dial and move their runners as many spaces as is indicated.
The first player to move his runner into Los Angeles wins the race.
Railroad Game. No. 4661. Object of game is to travel with train from San Francisco to New York. The first player, who reach New York wins the game.

Each player places one counter, representing army, on each of his five starting places. Players moves they armies as the dial indicated.
The player wins when he has captured all the opposing armies.

"It's magnetic! Hilarious! Fast and furious fun!"

A two player children's TV tie-in game produced by Berwick in the UK in 1970s.

Game consists of:

- thin raised cardboard board with 4 small pegs in the centre to secure a cardboard chimney piece
- 2 thin cardboard walls, one of them with a chimney attached; the walls have slots in them to denote doors. The chimney has a small slot on one side to denote a mouse hole. 
- 2 plastic pegs to secure the walls
- 1 plastic "cap" to the chimney
- 3 plastic doors
- 2 plastic and cardboard sections that move - a fridge and a bookcase
- a cardboard kennel
- a cardboard shelf
- 5 cardboard Jerry "life" cards
- 2 plastic cheese pieces
- 1 plastic sausage piece
- 1 plastic dog bowl
- two plastic rods with magnets attached, the larger one used to control Tom, the smaller to control Jerry
- two plastic and card magnetic playing pieces, one for Tom and one for Jerry
- assembly instructions and rules

Each player has a rod with a magnet on end of it. The rods are slid under the raised game board and used to control the playing pieces. The rod controlling the Tom playing piece attracts the Jerry playing piece as well as the Tom playing piece.
 The object of the game is for the player controlling Jerry to get three items of food into the mouse hole whilst avoiding capture by the player controlling Tom. To achieve this they have 5 lives, represented by 5 cards. If the player controlling Jerry places three items of food into the mouse hole before they lose the 5 live cards, they win. If not, then the player controlling Tom wins.

A variant of Balyna Magnetic Super Soccer, for smaller children.

Components were made of thin cardboard and plastic, and were therefore easily ripped/bent/broken. That, combined with the frantic nature of the game, means that complete copies in good condition are extremely hard to find.

Shuriken is a board game of ninja combat for 2-5 players. Custom dice, a modular game board, 100 game cards and 250 plastic ninja figures bring fast-paced ninja warfare to the gaming table

Shuriken takes place over the course of ten rounds, each round representing one hour of the night. Each player takes control of a unique ninja Clan and 50 plastic ninja representing unarmed, sword, shuriken, and master ninja. During the game players place, move, and upgrade their ninja forces as they attempt to complete Mission Cards and capture as many enemy ninja as possible.

The game is won by the player with the most victory points at the end of the game. Victory points are earned by capturing enemy ninja, and by completing Mission Cards.

This is a different sort of trick-taking cardgame, playable with a regular deck of cards (part of the deck).  Part of the inspiration for the game is the concept of free will vs determinism, and the verses in the Bible texts of Romans 8.

First, the game is for two players only.  Second, one player knows what all the cards are that are dealt and decides which hands are to be given to each player, stacks their hand and always leads with their hand.  The other player ends up picking trump suit, without fully knowing what all the cards are in (unlike the player who stacked their hand).

Included with the game is a variant that adds bidding, with each player bidding differently.

This is a game playable on a Byzantine Chessboard (16 round and 4 wide) using Checkers.  The game is similar to regular Captain's Mistress (Connect Four), but players control which side of the board pieces enter (and how pieces will follow when entered).  In the game, players try to get 4 in a row, vertically, horizontally or diagonally, with a set queuing order for pieces entering a column.

Included is a variant where pieces get recycled, by forming 4 in a row, and winning by preventing their opponent from also doing this.

This is a press your luck game playable on a Byzantine Chessboard.  In the game, players try to cluster their pieces into groups to score more points (players score bonus points for their largest group).  players roll two dice and move around a lane, leaving a piece behind. If they land on a space they landed on prior, they end up losing all points in the round. Bonus points are awarded for forming larger groups.  Players have to decide when to quit, as is typical of a press your luck game, and failure to do so and landing on a space occupied prior results in no points earned for the round.  Players have to decide when to either quit or push forward.

This is the game Groups adapted to a Byzantine Chessboard, with 8 pieces aside, instead of 6, as seen in Groups.  In this game players attempt to get their pieces on the board connected into a single group of orthogonally (vertically and/or horizontally) connected pieces, after being connected diagonally only. Pieces start out adjacent to one another, and a player's pieces are all connected diagonally at the start of the game.

In the game, pieces move orthgonally, and stop on an empty space, with players alternating moves one piece at a time until one player wins.

Omada is Greek for the word "group".

This is a connection game playable on a Byzantine Chessboard.  In the game, players try to be the first to connect the inner part of the board to the outer part of the board, by either placing pieces from their reserve off the board, onto an empty space, or by sliding from inside or outside.  Players start with 16 pieces off the board, which can end up being recycled and relocated.

A win in the game consists of a player being able to trace a path of spaces occupied with their own pieces vertically or horizontally (not diagonally), linking the inner and outer circle of the board.

This is a race game playable on a Byzantine Chessboard.  Players roll a die and move one of their pieces.  Players have a different piece in a different lane and attempt to navigate their pieces home.  In the dice version, each player has 4 pieces, one of which fits into each lane (4 of the rows), and each piece faces off against a single enemy piece.

The game also includes a diceless variant where how far pieces move is not governed by a die roll, but the position of the piece in relation to other pieces on the board.

This game is a game in the Checkers family, playable on a Byzantine Chessboard.  In the game players will advance checkers that stay in a lane when moving, and may end up switching lanes when performing a jump capture.  A piece may also switch lanes if it promotes to a King piece.

On a Byzantine chess board, it has 4 rows and 16 columns.  Pieces stay in the row they are in during place, and only switch rows when jumping.  In the game there are 4 pieces per row per player, and players have a total of 24 pieces a side.

This is a game in the Backgammon family, playable on a Byzantine Chessboard.  The game is similar to regular backgammon, except pieces start out in home area, and are divided up into a number of lanes, rather than all fitting into one lane, as is seen in regular Backgammon.

Player attempt to get all their pieces go around the board and have them land on enemy home spaces to bear off.  Players may only do this if they have all their home spaces empty.  When hit, pieces go back to their home spaces, rather than off the board on a bar.  Players start with 4 pieces per lane (row), and have a total of 16 pieces to start per side.

This is a roll and move game (also playable without the use of dice) on a Byzantine Chessboard.  Object of the game is to be the last remaining player.  In the game players will in the regular game, move as far as a single six sided die the roll tells them to, trying to avoid barriers put up in the game by players as they move.  In the game, as players get eliminated, they end up removing barriers the drop as the move.  The game feels a bit light a light cycle race.

Object of the game is to be the last remaining player.

In the diceless variant, player's movement is governed by what other pieces are on the board.

This is an abstract strategy game playable on a Byzantine Chessboard.  In the game, players move their Sectorian spaceships along the intersections of the board, capturing spaces on the board, in order to be able to produce more spaceships.  Object of the game is to be the first player to get 8 of their spaceships on the board.

ViciGo is a 2 player abstract strategy game playable on a Byzantine chess board.

In the game, players will move their pieces on the board, to either perform a custodial (bookend) capture to remove enemy pieces, or to perform a "bloom" pattern around one of their own pieces, in order to capture spaces on the board.

First player to get a set number of their pieces on the board wins.

This is a 2 player abstract strategy game, playable on a Byzantine Chessboard.  In the game, players will move their pieces and try to capture enemy pieces by bookending enemy pieces between their own pieces, so the enemy pieces get frozen so they can't move.  A player will then end up moving one unfrozen pieces that is freezing an enemy piece and occupying a space to capture it.  A player is also able to leave a piece behind, if the player doesn't move one of their pieces next to an enemy piece (or have that piece moving perform a capture).

The game includes 3 play levels, the first one without a King/Queen/Royal piece, and then a second which adds one Royal piece, and a third variant which has the player have two Royal pieces.  Each level of play adds addition win conditions.

The trap and delayed capture are reused from the likes of King's Cross and Linebreakers.

Board;
The board is four concentric circle with 16 space each (aka a Byzantine Chessboard).

Setup:
The 32 Black and 32 white pieces are alternated like a circular checkerboard.

Rules: 
This game is a two player abstract strategy game in the Mancala family.  As players sow their pieces, the board position "erodes" and leaves the players fewer and fewer options, and more and more spaces to sow pieces into to be removed from the board.

Object of the game is for a player to capture more of their opponent's pieces than their opponent captured of theirs.

This is a strategic game of color change for Looney Pyramids that uncommonly incorporates all ten standard Xeno and Rainbow colors. The pyramids are meant to represent a vast array of differently colored autumnal leaves. The goal is to bag "leaves" (aka pyramids) worth twenty points before your opponent does.

The game is either played on a 5X6 board or on a full chess board (in the “Advanced” rules variant).

With each turn, players enter a new piece and move that or another one of their pieces on the board. At the end of their movement phase, the piece moved progresses to a pyramid of the next color in a sequence and is optionally changed to a different sized piece, each of which have a distinct strategic value. Identically colored pieces that are made into rows of three or more are removed from the board and scored.

Aliens are invading Earth!
Six alien races are coming to enslave humanity. Which of them will succeed?

The Great Invasion is a control area game with lots of bluffing for 2 to 5 players..

Each player has the control of an alien race in secret, nobody has to know which of them. Every alien has it's own special skills and everybody can use them to their own purposes.

In every turn, a player must choice an alien race, a place to invade and an action token. With that, has to change majorities without give clues to other players about his own race. First, place new invasors. Then, move them to an adjacent place if you want. And last, perform it's special skill.

Note that there is more different aliens in game than players. So you have to pay attention to other player's movements to guess which is their race. And which race is a non-playing one.

At the end of the game, everybody reveals his race and earn points according the majorities.

This are the aliens:

-Proteus: Giant amoebas. Skill -> An invasor comes to the place from an adjacent one, then kill one on main place.
-Enerllaiser: Energy monsters. Skill -> Teleport an alien from the place he is, to the one you want.
-Fetids: they stink. Skill -> Move a invasor to an adjacent place. From that adjacent place, move a different invasor to an adjacent place far away from the original. If at any moment there is no move possible the invasor dies.
-Space Zombies. Like normal zombies, but from outer space. Skill -> Bites an invasor and changes its race to another different.
-Ulfedar: Explote. Skill -> Kills an invasor and another one from an adjacent place.
-Spores. Copycat. Skill -> Copy the skill of another alien on the same place.

Educational game where children must carefully listen to the story and find the mistakes in that story grabbing the items or cards from the table. There are 26 items and 26 cards in this game what may included on the stories.

Player who has the most cards/items at the end of the game is the winner.

boardgame: The Heroic Dragon Ball Z Adventure Game
description:
Dragon Ball Game edited in English. The objective is to get all 7 dragon balls,travelling a board where there are 10 important locations which have 10 balls of dragon (only 7 are the real) and where events are produced by the cards.

The battles are decided by a bout of throwing balls to a crater where you put a figure to be cut down. The movement is done by obtaining dragon ball points, to be stealing in the game.

An entertaining game for little ones and customizable for more fans.

Fairly straightforward snakes-and-ladders style game with 600 numerical, verbal and non-verbal reasoning questions (200 of each type) used to determine progress.  Designed to help children improve their 11+/CAT scores (Cognitive Ability Tests used in the UK either as part of 11+/school entrance exams/streaming exams within weeks of starting secondary school) but with a "hard-mode" for adults consisting of a 30 second timer for each question rather than the 50 second timer for children. Cognosis Challenge squares add an extra dimension.

Cognosis is an exciting, problem-solving family game. It includes 600 questions based on the 3 question types that are used in Cognitive Ability Tests (CATs)*, tests that are faced by almost all children aged 10 or 11 in the UK.
Children have 50 seconds to answer the questions (the time allowed in a test) but over 12’s only have 30 seconds, making it a game for all the family. Land on a square and answer the question. Get it right and remain where you are or get it wrong and go back to where you came from. Land on a Cognosis Challenge square and challenge one of your opponents to answer a question of your choice – which category do they find the hardest? If they get it wrong, they go back.
Reach the Final Challenge square and answer the question to be crowned the Cognosis Champion.
Cognosis can be played simply as a fun game by all the family but it is also so much more. It gives children valuable practice in answering CAT type questions in the typical time allowed in the tests and so promotes familiarity and builds confidence. The best bit - as it is fun, children want to play it and don’t realise that they are learning and developing with every throw of the dice and every question answered. They also love beating their parents, grandparents or any adult so don’t think this is just a game for the children, they will want you to play too.
Cognosis with a twist
Alternative 1
If you're under 12 and want more of a challenge, why not play the game using the 30 second timer
Alternative 2
Going on a long journey but still want to play/practice, why not take a box of cards (you choose which type) and a timer ( you choose which one) and play simply by pulling out a question, turning over the timer and trying to get the right answer before the timer runs out.
Alternative 3
Out of the house and with a friend. Again, take a box of cards and a timer. One person pulls out a question and lays it flat and the other turns over the timer. Who can get the correct answer first? The first one to correctly answer gets to keep the card. Play for 15 minutes. The winner is the one with the most cards at the end.

Objective: be the first turning back to Europe (40)
 - moving by turns -
simple 'Roll & Move' game; ending on a red marked space follow instructions glued into lid;
remarks:
should have been published ~1920's

Objective: be the first reaching the top (sun)
 - moving by turns -
each player rolls two colored dice choosing one of the shown colors to move along. Throwing the same colors player may choose this color or roll again. 
remarks:
due to design game should have been produced during 1950's or earlier.

Objective: gain as many winning points as possible taking photographs from wild animals.
 - moving by turns -
simple 'Roll & Move' game; ending on a 'pictured' space you may collect appropiate card be the first one, otherwise collect winning points only. Some spaces send you back or forwards...
remarks:
game - as usual for GDR products - praises socialist countries..

Civilization Central America is a variant of Advanced Civilization. Players build cities, collect and trade commodities, and buy technologies, dealing with many recurring calamities along the way.

Set in Central America, the map includes the same number of territories, population density, and city sites as Advanced Civilization with the Western Extension Map. There are 3 commodities each in 9 trade card decks, for a total of 27 commodities. There are eight minor tradable calamities, eight tradable calamities, and eight non-tradable calamities. All commodities and most calamities are new. Players may purchase sixty-three new and revised technologies from the Civilization: The Expansion Project rules, with a unique point system used to progress on the Chronological Advancement Chart.

The game comes with all parts required for play except the rules, which may be downloaded from the publisher website and The General (Avalon Hill magazine) archives. Players need not own Civilization or Advanced Civilization.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
The Dynamite Promo is an additional card that can be added to the Saloon Deck.

It is a special weapon that allows a player to make an attack with dynamite, choosing a cover rather than a player.  Dynamite deals damage to every player in that cover and each player in reach of that cover.  Affected players may block with either gun or melee blocks.

Dynamite cannot be affected by attack enhancement cards, cover bonuses, or cover defense.  Dynamite is dropped when used.

Dynamite is not used in a team game.

Hydra is a game for two players to test their mental ability and logical thinking against each other. With a look and feel of game that could have been played by men of the Norse whilst they drank and feasted in their Mead halls or even taken on the raiding trips and played at sea.  Hydra is a completely new game of intelligence and cunning, you must create an unbroken line of tiles from one side of the board to the other whilst stopping your opponent from doing the same. The first person to do so wins the game.

A unique feature of Hydra is that it is played East to West and North to South or it could be West to East and South to North. The game gets its name from the style of play of creating lines that get broken like they have been cut and then you must start a new one, just like when Hercules cut off one head of the Hydra another new one or two grow back. The game mechanics are very simple with only three different types of tiles and a single hand count of rules to beat your opponent.

Game play can take just a couple of minutes when you first learn to play, up to tens of minutes or more as you and your opponents learn the subtle flow of cunning tactics that are wrapped in Hydra.

The game combined the "capture the leader" and geometric movement aspects of games like Chess, Xiangqi or Shogi with custodial capture such as is found in games like Petteia or Hnefatafl. Because capture then involves a combination of two pieces, control of the board is consciously distinguished from simply the ability to move to any given space. Hence, it resembles Chess up to a point but players have to think about relative positioning of their own pieces in combination and of the opponent's pieces to see what areas of the board they control.

Imperio is the Latin word which refers to having complete control over someone, to the power of life and death in principle. This game is about control of the board, which entails the power of life and death (so to speak) over the opponent's pieces. One has to think differently from most games average people today would know to achieve that type of control effectively.

Tom Vasel Dice Tower 2013 Kickstarter funding campaign promo character for Pixel Tactics

The Game Reviewer/ Tom Vasel brings dice to Pixel Tactics. Depending how the card is played, the card has the ability to add 1d6 to attacks, give 2 extra actions or reduce a hero's life to the difference between 2d6. Played as the starting hero (Tom Vasel) there is a chance to rout opposing units.

Finally played as a single time effect, it lets you challenge your opponent to another 2-player game, allowing the winner to destroy three heroes of his choice.

boardgame: Mage Wars: Dice Tower 2013 funding campaign promo card set
description:
10 Mage Wars promo cards made available through the 2013 Dice Tower funding campaign on Kickstarter:

The enchantment Akiro's Favor that lets you re-roll dice
Ballista, a war machine that shoots piercing bolts.
The tough Bloodcrag Minotaur.
Critical Strike to cause piercing attacks.
The mysterious Gravicor that prevents flying.
Hurl Meteorite a massive damage spell.
Lion Savagery another enchantment to cause piercing attacks.
The unavoidable Morning Star.
The Temple High Guard that can counterstrike even before the attack.
And the piercing Vorpal Blade.

Up to four adventurers will race to unlock the secrets of an ancient tomb. Players take turns rolling and moving up to the number of spaces rolled attempting to be the first to return all of their keys to gain access to the treasures within, but beware! The spaces can cause the tomb tiles to flip, swap, or rotate. Be the first to claim the Pharaoh’s priceless treasures to win.

The card called "Call to Arms" is a new Action card for Jerusalem.

Just add the card to the basic Action card deck to play with it.

The player who plays this card may move their squires from one area to another (with the exception of areas blocked by Barons). For each Silver Bezant paid to the Bank, you can move up to 2 squires.

Hoplomachus [ha-ploh-ma-kuss], or "Hoplo" for short, is a hex-based tactical board game set inside a gladiatorial arena. One to four players, each representing an ancient city/civilization thought to have been lost to the world, are now on Rome's doorstep vying for a place in the prestigious arena games.

Players have a very clear objective: Eliminate the opponent's champion. Champions start the game in the arena but are inactive and defenseless. They will not fight until the crowd is behind them. Each player takes turns drawing, playing and moving gladiators from their "hand" and assisting them with tactic chips.

Based on opponent's moves and play style, each person will need to adjust their strategies. Defend your champ, take over and hold the crowd favor areas, assault opponent's champion, cover deployment zones, eliminate tacticians to reduce enemy options, fight beasts to gain crowd favor, the list of tactical possibilities is endless.

Hoplomachus combines combat, tactics, chip (units) management, level progression (though crowd favor system), team play, player vs board, and so much more.

Training Grounds: This is the smaller, faster version of our full, award winning Hoplomachus game. Play, teach, travel, or host tournaments with the Training Grounds if the full game is too much! The Training Grounds features one of each of the basic gladiators and a tactic or equipment. Be the last gladiator standing!

Carpe Venatus!

The conquest of medieval Europe is one which battles kings and countries to dominate these lands through trade, attack, defense, strategic placement, movement and expansion. One requires the strategy of planning for successful offensive military execution as well as for defensive game play, and while the roll of the die may determine specific results, both bold offensives and conservative defensives shall impact the overall outcome. Only with banners held high and rule held sound would the victor reign medieval Europe!

In Castle Toppe - The Game of Sovereigns, players attempt to gain rule of medieval Europe through trade, building, expansion, and conquest.  Game play is pulsed with Event Cards which can advantage or disadvantage a player's resources, buildings and tactics.  Players can both strategically defend or aggressively conquest with boldness.  Boats and Ships are even used in expansions and battles.  The player to first successfully fly 5 banners (obtained through building a castle and/or a ship) in at least three ruling countries wins.

Road/Kill Ultimate Car Combat is a 2-5 player table-top strategy game of fun and furious futuristic car combat that can be played in under an hour. It is the pinnacle of smash'em-up car games, loaded with sweet looking customizable high-quality minis, tons of guns’n’gear and fast-moving play tactics. This game is first-class and like nothing else on the market today.

A Streamlined Experience

Road/Kill is designed to favor flow over detail, while still allowing a satisfying depth of player choice. The rules accommodate a wide variety of driving experiences, while innovative components like our Command Box, Maneuver template and Road tiles elegantly reduce clutter and keep the game moving.

A Game of Anticipation

Road/Kill is about assessing the situation at the beginning of each turn, and who will do what. The use of four iconic dice within a snap-tite command box allows players to set their actions in secret before being revealed simultaneously with other players. In this way, surprising twists and turns are sure to happen while rewarding players with good insight.

Build’n’Battle

Vehicles are easily designed on a template and customized to meet each player’s preference for speed, offense and defense. Once designed, vehicles are constructed with a wide variety of parts. You can be ready to play in minutes or paint your creation to perfection.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Board & Pieces
3 pieces including one King, and the same 3 for blue and 12 white pieces are initially placed on a Honey Donut board.

Movements
Players move their pieces in their turn following the rules below:
    Move a whole "straight line" of pieces which includes his/her piece(s) one square in either direction along the line.  "Straight lines" are composed of either 2, 3 or 4 pieces. All the pieces in one "straight line" need to be moved always together.
    With the move, one piece is pushed out from the board. The pushed out piece is placed in the square in which no piece is placed after the move. The King pieces cannot be placed back on the board as it is the end of the game when they are pushed out of the board.
    Moving the line backward right after the opponent's turn is not allowed.
    No pass is allowed.

Goal
The player who pushed opponent's King out of the board wins.

Board & Pieces
FlipFlop is played between two opponents. There are two versions of the game: One is played on 3x3 board and another is on 5x5 square board. Each player has 3 (5 for 5 x 5 boards) pieces, which have two faces: The Bishop and the Rook. 
 Movements
All pieces start as Rook, arranged at the first row on each end. As in normal chess games, Rooks move in a straight line, horizontally or vertically and Bishops move in a straight diagonal line, as far as they want.

After each move, pieces are 'flipped' and promoted from Rook to Bishop, then from Bishop to Rook.

Pieces cannot be captured, except when they are either in your or opponent's goal square.

Goal
 You win when any of your pieces is placed in the middle square in the last row, your goal square for two consecutive turns, or when there is no legitimate moves left for your opponent.

Board & Pieces
Rinne and Tensei are played between two opponents on a 5x6 square board.

Each player has 5 pieces, which move as King, Knight, Bishop, Rook, and Queen.

Movements
 All pieces start as King, one piece in the goal position on your end, then the four others on a M-shaped line on the 2 and 3 ranks.

Pieces move exactly the same as in normal chess games, except that they cannot be captured as in normal chess. Pieces can be captured, only when they are either in your or oppnent's goal square.

After each move, pieces are promoted:
In Rinne, in a fixed order: King -> Knight -> Bishop -> Rook -> Queen -> King...
In Tensei, promotion of pieces are determined by rolling a dice.

Goal
 You win when any of your pieces is placed in the middle square in the last row, your goal square for two consecutive turns, or when there is no legitimate moves left for your opponent.

Board & Pieces

A decorated board with a line, and 3 or 4 sets of 4 different sizes of disk-shaped pieces are used for 2 or 3 players.

Rules

Players place pieces on the line of the board. Smaller pieces can be placed on bigger pieces. The same sized pieces cannot be on top of each other. Pieces that are placed on the board, both by itself, and piled-up, are called stacks.

When all the pieces are placed on the board, the Game starts.

The player in turn take either of the following actions. No pass is allowed:

a) Take all the pieces in the same colors placed at the top of all the stacks.

b) Relocate stacks: Take one of the stacks and place the pieces all together on top of the other stacks on the board. Stacks can also be moved partially: A part of a stack, a piece or multiple pieces from the top of stacks can be taken and placed on top of the other stacks. Pieces can only be placed on top of bigger sized pieces here. Also, pieces can only be relocated on higher stacks.

The game is finished when all the pieces on the board are taken.

Goal

The player who took that last piece is the winner in the set.

Winner gains the number of the pieces that he/she took from the board in the turn as his/her score in the set. All the other players score 0.

The winner of the game is the player who gained the highest score when all the sets are finished.

Board & Pieces
T4 is played between two opponents on a 4x4 square board.
Each player has 16 units of 3 different kinds of pieces, 48 pieces in total. No pieces are placed on the board when the game starts.

Movements
Players take the following 2 actions in one turn:
    Take one of his/her pieces from outside the board and place it at an empty square on the board, then,
    Move a piece or a set of pieces from one square to another on the board.

Pieces can be placed at any empty squares or at already-occupied squares in condition that already-placed pieces are different kind(s) from the one that is going to be placed.

Accordingly, how pieces are placed at a square are 7 different ways as follows: 3 different single pieces and 4 different combination (sets) of pieces. 　

After placing a piece on the board, players move a piece or a set of pieces from a square to another.

Players can choose to move either a single piece or a set of pieces together, but cannot move a single piece out of a set of pieces from a square.

Pieces are moved as Queen in Chess: They can be moved any number of vacant squares diagonally, horizontally, or vertically.

Jumping over other pieces or moving to an already-occupied square is not allowed.

Goal
The player who succeeds in placing 3 respective (single) pieces or set of pieces in a horizontal, vertical, or diagonal row wins the game.

The player loses when he/she cannot move his/her piece(s).

The player who places 4 respective pieces or combination of pieces in a horizontal, vertical, or diagonal row loses the game.(This rarely happens.)

boardgame: Batman: Arkham City Escape – Hotline to Oracle Promo card
description:
"Hotline to Oracle" was a limited supply promo card contained with the game if purchased from a Brick and Mortar store.

It is a Utility Belt item the player can swap out for variety that allows for Batman player to draw additional combat cards if they're low on them.

In Zombies!!! 12: Zombie Zoo, the zoo has been infected and the animals are turning into zombies! The monkeys have escaped, but you still have the ability to override the automatic release on the other cages in the zoo. You must reach these cages in time or the animals will break loose, making your job even harder.

description: The Sierp is a light anti-infantry artillery armored fighting vehicle. Its high Movement allows it to remain in movement and to pick the best shooting point to harass enemy forces. Light mortars have devastating effects, but the Sierp has another major asset: this strider comes with a communication disruptor that cancels the opponent’s LP. Short, but tough, it is a nasty sting in your opponent’s side!

Inkermann 1854 is a tactical game simulating one of the battles of the Crimean War between Russian troops and the Anglo-French troops November 5, 1854 in the East of Sevastopol.

Inkermann 1854 offers two scenarios: a tactical scenario which starts with the first Russian attack on British positions and a grand tactical scenario which allows battle plan change, adding more units and which starts when the Russians troops begin to move at night.

A number of optional rules can be added.

A game turn represents 45 minutes, a unit one or two infantry battalions, one or two artillery batteries or one cavalry regiment.

Bilingual Rules and Charts (English/French).

boardgame: Meltdown: Save the Polar Bears Before the Ice Melts
description:
The first board game that melts

GEOlino, the German science magazine for children, wanted its young readers to experience the effects of global warming. Not with dry-as-dust theory, but in a completely child-oriented way. That's why we've developed MELTDOWN - the first board game that melts.

The aim of the game is to take a polar bear family from the permanent ice floes to safety on the mainland. It's a race against time, as the way leads across real, slowly melting ice floes, which children can make themselves with the accompanying mould, a bit of water and a freezer compartment. The chunks of ice are arranged on a blue polar sea sponge to form a small Arctic. The sponge is used as the game board and absorbs the melted ice at the same time. Now you can start saving polar bears.

MELTDOWN makes children experience the effects of global warming in a playful way. Without any wagging finger - just one hand to roll the dice.

The Dotch Yaga is here to win. It can potentially destroy two soldier units at a time, and there is nothing they can do about it. Even the heavy weapons mounted on armored fighting vehicles have difficulties causing the Dotch Yaga any serious damage. With a few shots, the Dotch Yaga must deny the enemy’s ability to control a target. All it has left to do then is deal with the remaining armored fighting vehicles, which its heavy Gauss will take care of with great efficiency.

This Attachment Box contains a storm golem overseer with nanoblaster and reaper blades, two storm golems with sonic gun, two storm golems with flamer and a storm golem relay with nanoblaster and reaper blades.

With these extra choices, your Therian soldiers will get real leadership and a wider range of special weapons. Flamer or Sonic gun, devastating rate of fire or napalm tidal waves? Adapt your methods to your opponent.

This Attachement Box includes an assault golem overseer with a nucleus rifle and reaper blades, two assault golems with nucleus rifle and three assault golems with flamer. 
Thanks to these extra choices, your Therian soldiers will benefit from true leadership and a larger range of special weapons. Flamer or nucleus gun, firestorm or surgical strikes, adapt your methods to the enemy.

Mystique Energy is a card game for 2 to 6 players that can be played with a Mystique deck.

To set-up, shuffle the deck and place it face down on the table. Each player then draws a card without showing it to the other players, looks at it, then places it face down before them. This is their "energy card" and can be looked at during play. Each player then draws another card; this is their "playing card".

The first two players lay their cards on the table adjacent to one another. Players take turns in counterclockwise order placing their card on the table adjacent to at least two placed cards so that the cards fit into an imaginary hexagonal grid, as shown in the image below:


 _   _   _   _
| |_| |_| |_| | 
|_| |_| |_| |_|
| |_| |_| |_| |
|_| |_| |_| |_|
| |_| |_| |_| |
|_| |_| |_| |_|
| |_| |_| |_| |
|_| |_| |_| |_|


After placing the card, the player draws another card from the deck (if possible) which becomes their new "playing card". The game ends when the last card has been placed on the table, and players then score as follows:


    Each player counts the number of cards of the largest connected group of the color of his "energy card".



    Each player counts the number of cards of the largest connected group of the suit of his "energy card".



    Each player counts the number of cards of the largest connected group of the number of his "energy card".


Finally, each player multiplies these three values. The player with the lowest value wins. In case of a tie, the player among those tied who played his final card first wins.

Stalingrad Hall n°4 is an ideal introduction to wargaming. The game, through two scenarios, recreates fighting in the Stalingrad "Krasnay Barrikady" (Red Barricades) building in Autumn 1942.

Game sequences are divided in turns :
A-Reinforcements
B-Events
C-Activation phase
D-Close combats
E-End turn

The German plays first by activating one of his 3 squads. A squad can move or fire. Russian player then activates 0 to 3 of his units. 
Once all units are activated, units adjacent to enemy units can engage close combat.

Available with French rules, but English rules can be downloaded from Vae Victis website.

description:
From the publisher's website:

"Monkey Dice is a fun, "press your luck" dice rolling game for the whole family to enjoy.

Each player takes their dice and tries to roll the most monkeys. AVOID THE POOP! If a player rolls 3 poops, their turn is over and they get NO points for the monkeys they have collected.

Monkeys can be rewarded with bananas to clean up poop for you, increasing your chances of winning."

There are twelve identical dice in Monkey Dice. There are three results on each die: Monkey (3 sides), poop (2), and banana (1).

On a player's turn, they will roll three of the dice. After rolling they keep monkeys and poop, then decide what to do with the bananas: they can re-roll them during the next set or keep them. If a player keeps a total of three bananas they can eliminate one poop they have collected.

The player continues by rolling another three dice, or stopping their turn. When they stop, they count the total number of monkeys they have collected and add that to their score. If they rolled three poops, their turn is over and lose the points they currently collected.

When a player reaches 50 points, the current round is finished, and they player with the highest score wins.

The Cottonwood Contest: Cousins is a stand-alone solo game. Two decks can accommodate two to four players.

The Cottonwood Contest: Cousins has more types of goods than The Cottonwood Contest, and it introduces a new type of item: vegetables that can be planted in fields and then processed after being harvested. It also does not include any field/pasture cards.

GAME OVERVIEW

Your grandparents were so impressed with the outcome of your parents' contest that they decided to have one of their own. You and your cousin are competing for a large parcel of your grandparents' land in the small but growing town of Cottonwood.

You have three years to show that you can make your farm more profitable. You may plant grain and vegetables, raise animals, and gather apples and mushrooms from the countryside. You may sell goods at each of the three Markets, and enter the income in your ledger.

Your cousin does not want the work of going to the annual market, and made a deal with a local store to receive the average price for each good sold. To determine your cousin's score, you will multiply goods sold by their average price.

The cousin who has the most money in their ledger after the third year wins the contest.

From the publisher's website:

Dicecapades! Dice-T is the fast-paced, dice-rolling, crossword game where spelling words and lots of luck=win! Try completing as many of your crosswords with your rolled dice. The more crosswords you can complete, the more points you will score! You might get lucky and get some bonus points and wild spaces, or you might have to swap cards with an opponent! Players of all skill levels can compete and win as you try to sell two- to five-letter words.

Easy to learn, challenging to win, Dicecapades! Dice-T is the ultimate friend and family word game that'll keep everyone on their toes!

In the game, players will get 12 dice and 3 "Dice-T" cards. The cards have crosswords on them, bonus points, or game modifiers. Players try to complete the crosswords by rolling their dice and using the available letters. Play is simultaneous.

The first player to complete all their crosswords scores the points on their cards. After 10 rounds, the game is over and the player with the highest points wins.

From the publisher:

"It’s the fast-moving dice game with a wild side! Players roll all 10 of their color dice at the same time. Dice that come up bones are wild – they count for any number you want. Keep rolling until one player has all 10 of their dice with the same number. Includes rules for 8 other games."

The Hekat is a light combat strider based on an ancient design, whose function is long forgotten. Terribly efficient in contact (it can even threaten even the mighty support golgoths), It moves at lightening speed and can carried out simple handling tasks such as moving low walls.

Paradoxically, this golgoth intended for close combat, is the dreaded enemy of any armored fighting vehicle! At first, the concept was met by the extremely fierce opposition of the most traditionalist overseers until it proved its efficiency on the battlefield.

Speed is the Hekat’s main asset. No other golgoth serving at the moment can challenge it in that field. Its powerful grim scythes allow it to tear through the thickest of armor. As for the infantry, it hardly needs any chewing.

The combination of its maneuverability and the simplicity in the use of its armament make it a remarkable armored fighting vehicle unit, whose deployment and use are exceptionally straightforward: Full throttle ahead, hit anything standing in the way, trample the infantry and slash through as many enemy striders as you can find until the unavoidable surrender.

This unit box contains a unit of 1 miniature, 3 low walls and 2 reference cards for AT-43.The Hekat is a light combat strider based on an ancient design, whose function is long forgotten. Terribly efficient in contact (it can even threaten the mighty support golgoths), It moves at lightening speed and can carried out simple handling tasks such as moving low walls.

Paradoxically, this golgoth intended for close combat, is the dreaded enemy of any armored fighting vehicle! At first, the concept was met by the extremely fierce opposition of the most traditionalist overseers until it proved its efficiency on the battlefield.

Speed is the Hekat’s main asset. No other golgoth serving at the moment can challenge it in that field. Its powerful grim scythes allow it to tear through the thickest of armor. As for the infantry, it hardly needs any chewing.

The combination of its maneuverability and the simplicity in the use of its armament make it a remarkable armored fighting vehicle unit, whose deployment and use are exceptionally straightforward: Full throttle ahead, hit anything standing in the way, trample the infantry and slash through as many enemy striders as you can find until the unavoidable surrender.

The O.K. Corral is another unofficial expansion to BANG!, available in English and Italian. It was designed by Steve Elmore in 2008, and functions just like BANG! A Fistful of Cards or BANG! High Noon.

Thus, after the first round, the Sheriff flips over an O.K. Corral event card that stipulates a gameplay change for the round. At the beginning of the next round (the Sheriff's next turn), they flip over an O.K. Corral card that replaces the old one. The old gameplay condition is removed and the new one is in place. For those of you who liked A Fistful of Cards and High Noon, this expansion might work just right for you. For players not a fan of these, there is a gameplay variation that removes the randomness of these event cards (having them flipped out in order at the beginning of the game).
